# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]**

### **Q1. (a) Differentiate between a Cartesian robot and a cylindrical robot based on joint configuration and workspace geometry. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **Cartesian robot** uses three **prismatic (P) joints** arranged along mutually orthogonal axes (PPP configuration). Its workspace is a **rectangular cuboid** defined by $x \in [x_{min}, x_{max}]$, $y \in [y_{min}, y_{max}]$, $z \in [z_{min}, z_{max}]$.

A **cylindrical robot** uses one **revolute (R)** joint at the base followed by two **prismatic (P) joints** (RPP configuration). Its workspace is a **hollow cylindrical shell** described by $(r, \theta, z)$ in cylindrical coordinates.

| Feature | Cartesian | Cylindrical |
|---|---|---|
| Joint config | PPP | RPP |
| Workspace | Rectangular cuboid | Hollow cylinder |
| Best for | 3D printing, CNC | Loading/unloading |

---

### **Q1. (b) Define the term "Degree of Freedom" of a robotic manipulator. What is the standard DOF count for a general-purpose industrial robot? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Degree of Freedom (DOF)** of a robotic manipulator is defined as the number of *independent parameters* (translations and rotations) required to completely specify the configuration of the manipulator in 3D space.

A **general-purpose industrial robot** is designed to position its end-effector at any arbitrary $(x, y, z)$ and orient it at any roll-pitch-yaw $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ — totaling **6 DOF**. This is the universal benchmark for spatial manipulation tasks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

#### **(a) Classify robots based on geometric configuration. With neat schematic descriptions, explain the four major geometric types and their workspaces. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Robots are classified into four major geometric types based on the arrangement of their first three (major) joints:

**1. Cartesian (Gantry) Robot — PPP**
- **Joints:** Three prismatic joints along orthogonal axes.
- **Workspace:** A rectangular cuboid.
- **Schematic description:** Three perpendicular linear slides — one horizontal (X), one horizontal (Y, perpendicular to X), and one vertical (Z).
- **Applications:** Pick-and-place, CNC machining, 3D printing, assembly lines.

**2. Cylindrical Robot — RPP**
- **Joints:** One revolute joint (rotation about vertical base axis) + two prismatic joints (radial and vertical).
- **Workspace:** A hollow cylindrical shell.
- **Schematic description:** Rotating column with a horizontally extending arm and a vertical sliding member.
- **Applications:** Machine tending, loading/unloading of lathes and presses.

**3. Spherical (Polar) Robot — RRP**
- **Joints:** Two revolute joints (one about base, one shoulder pitch) + one prismatic joint (radial extension).
- **Workspace:** A spherical shell.
- **Schematic description:** Base rotation, shoulder pivot, and telescoping arm.
- **Applications:** Forging, casting, die-handling, spot welding.

**4. Articulated (Anthropomorphic) Robot — RRR**
- **Joints:** Three revolute joints (waist, shoulder, elbow).
- **Workspace:** A doughnut-shaped sphere-like volume.
- **Schematic description:** Closely mimics a human arm — torso, upper arm, forearm.
- **Applications:** Arc welding, painting, sealing, complex assembly.

| Robot | Joints | Workspace | Key Strength |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cartesian | PPP | Rectangular cuboid | High rigidity, easy calibration |
| Cylindrical | RPP | Hollow cylinder | Good reach in radial direction |
| Spherical | RRP | Spherical shell | Good vertical reach |
| Articulated | RRR | Sphere-like | Maximum dexterity |

---

#### **(b) Using Grübler's formula, compute the DOF of a 5-DOF articulated robot having 5 revolute joints. Identify the application where such a 5-DOF configuration is preferred over a full 6-DOF robot. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Number of links including ground: $n = 6$ (5 moving links + 1 ground)
- Number of 1-DOF revolute joints: $j_1 = 5$
- Higher-order joints: $j_2 = j_3 = j_4 = j_5 = 0$

**Apply the spatial Grübler–Kutzbach formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5 \\
&= 6(6-1) - 5(5) - 0 \\
&= 6(5) - 25 \\
&= 30 - 25 \\
&= 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The manipulator possesses **5 DOF**.

**Applications where 5-DOF is preferred over 6-DOF:**
- **Palletizing and depalletizing:** Tasks involve only position $(x, y, z)$ and end-effector roll — pitch and yaw are unnecessary, so 5 DOF is sufficient and cost-effective.
- **Simple arc welding on flat surfaces:** Where the workpiece orientation is fixed, the missing 1 DOF is not required.
- **Paint spraying on symmetric objects:** Cylindrical symmetry eliminates the need for one rotational DOF.

Using 5 DOF instead of 6 reduces mechanical complexity, cost, and control difficulty, while providing adequate capability for the targeted application.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Choice**

#### **(a) Explain the classification of robots based on application areas. List at least six application domains with corresponding robot types and one example task each. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Robots are classified by application domain to align mechanical design, control strategy, and safety standards with operational requirements.

| # | Application Domain | Typical Robot Type | Example Task |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Industrial Manufacturing** | Articulated 6-DOF | Arc welding of car chassis |
| 2 | **Material Handling** | Cartesian / Gantry | Palletizing boxes on conveyors |
| 3 | **Precision Assembly** | SCARA | Inserting SMD components on PCB |
| 4 | **Medical / Surgical** | Redundant 7-DOF | Laparoscopic surgery |
| 5 | **Hazardous Environments** | Teleoperated manipulator | Handling radioactive material in nuclear plants |
| 6 | **Space Exploration** | Long-reach redundant arm | Satellite servicing (e.g., Canadarm) |
| 7 | **Underwater** | ROV with manipulator | Pipeline inspection, deep-sea mining |
| 8 | **Agriculture** | Mobile manipulator | Autonomous fruit harvesting |
| 9 | **Defense / EOD** | Tracked mobile manipulator | Bomb disposal reconnaissance |
| 10 | **Service / Domestic** | Mobile manipulator | Floor cleaning, fetch-and-carry |

**Design implications:** Industrial robots prioritize *speed, payload, and repeatability*; medical robots prioritize *precision, sterility, and safety*; space robots prioritize *reach and reliability in extreme environments*. The application thus dictates the geometry, DOF, materials, and sensor suite.

---

#### **(b) A robot has a revolute joint with an angular range of 270° equipped with a 14-bit encoder. Calculate the joint resolution. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Angular range: $q_{range} = 270°$
- Encoder resolution: $N = 14$ bits

**Step 1:** Compute the total number of encoder counts per revolution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Counts} &= 2^N \\
&= 2^{14} \\
&= 16384
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert the angular range to radians:
$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{range} &= 270° \times \frac{\pi}{180°} \\
&= 1.5 \pi \text{ rad} \\
&\approx 4.7124 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the resolution (smallest distinguishable angle):
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta q_{min} &= \frac{q_{range}}{2^N} \\
&= \frac{1.5 \pi}{16384} \\
&\approx \frac{4.7124}{16384} \\
&\approx 2.876 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad} \\
&\approx 0.01648° \\
&\approx 59.21 \text{ arc-seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** The joint can be commanded with an angular increment as small as **~0.288 mrad** (or ~59 arc-seconds). For an industrial assembly task requiring 0.1 mm positional resolution at a 0.5 m reach, the required angular resolution is:
$$
\Delta q_{required} = \frac{0.1 \text{ mm}}{500 \text{ mm}} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}
$$
Since $\Delta q_{min} = 2.876 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad} > 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}$, a **14-bit encoder is barely adequate** for this task — a 15-bit or 16-bit encoder would provide a safer margin.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing DOF with axes of motion:** DOF refers to *independent* parameters, not total joint count. A 6-DOF robot has 6 joints, but a 7-joint robot may have only 6 DOF if one is a dependent (passive) link.
> 2. **Forgetting the ground link:** In Grübler's formula, students commonly count $n$ as only the *moving* links, forgetting the **ground link (n=1 additional)**, leading to off-by-one errors.
> 3. **Workspace vs. Dexterity:** A larger workspace does not imply better dexterity. The articulated robot has high dexterity despite a constrained doughnut-shaped workspace.
> 4. **Joint notation:** The notation **PPP, RPP, RRP, RRR** describes the *first three (major) joints only*. Students must not misclassify a 6-DOF robot as "RRRRRR" in a context where only major joints are required.
> 5. **Unit conversion:** In encoder resolution problems, students often forget to convert degrees to radians (or vice versa) when comparing with linear resolution.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three primary classification criteria:** Geometry, DOF, and Application.
- **Four geometric classes:** Cartesian (PPP), Cylindrical (RPP), Spherical (RRP), Articulated (RRR).
- **A rigid body in 3D space has 6 DOF** (3 translations + 3 rotations).
- **Grübler's formula (spatial):** $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$.
- **Grübler's formula (planar):** $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$.
- **Standard general-purpose industrial robot DOF = 6.**
- **SCARA** = 4 DOF, used for vertical insertion assembly.
- **Delta robot** = high-speed parallel pick-and-place with 3–4 DOF.
- **Redundant robots** have DOF > 6, used in confined spaces and surgery.
- **Workspace volume formulas:** Cartesian (rectangular cuboid), Cylindrical ($\pi r^2 h$ shell), Spherical ($\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3$ shell).
- **Resolution formula:** $\Delta q_{min} = \frac{q_{range}}{2^N}$, where $N$ is encoder bits.
- **Repeatability** (typically ±0.02 to ±0.1 mm) ≠ **Accuracy** (deviation from commanded position).
- **6-DOF = 3 for positioning + 3 for orientation** (roll-pitch-yaw).
- **Joint notation rule:** $n$-DOF robot = $n$ active joints (in non-redundant case).
- **Application dictates design:** payload, reach, precision, environment all flow from the application domain.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Programming for Robots: Introduction to Robot Programming Languages and Simulators (e.g., Python, ROS Basics)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT865 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Programming for Robots: Introduction to Robot Programming Languages and Simulators (e.g., Python, ROS Basics)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robot Programming Language (RPL):** A formal, structured language used to specify sequences of motions, sensor interactions, and decision logic that a robotic manipulator or mobile robot must execute. It bridges the gap between high-level human intent and low-level actuator/sensor control signals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robot Simulator:** A physics-based software environment that emulates the kinematics, dynamics, sensor behavior, and environmental interactions of a real robot, allowing algorithm validation without physical hardware.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robot Operating System (ROS):** An open-source, meta-operating system (middleware framework) that provides hardware abstraction, device drivers, libraries, visualizers, message-passing, and package management for robot software development. It is **not** a true OS but runs on top of Linux.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are teaching a new intern to assemble a gearbox in a factory.

- **Teach-Pendant Programming** → You physically move the intern's hand to each step, and they memorize the motion. *Simple, but inflexible.*
- **Scripted Language (VAL, RAPID)** → You give the intern a printed checklist with exact coordinates. *Reproducible, but rigid.*
- **High-Level Task Language (Python + ROS)** → You tell the intern *"tighten all bolts to 25 Nm in cross-pattern order."* *Abstract, scalable, intelligent.*
- **Simulator** → A virtual workshop where the intern practices safely before touching the real gearbox. *Risk-free learning.*

The evolution is from **explicit motion commands** → **task-level semantic goals**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Purpose |
|--------|----------------|---------|
| **Hz (Frequency)** | 10 Hz – 1000 Hz | ROS topic publish rate |
| **TCP/IP Latency** | 1 ms – 100 ms | Inter-node communication |
| **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** | 6 (industrial arm) | Minimum for full spatial reach |
| **Cycle Time** | 0.5 s – 2 s | Pick-and-place benchmark |
| **Frame Rate (Simulator)** | 30 FPS – 240 FPS | Visual realism of physics engine |

> [!NOTE]
> A typical industrial robot (e.g., ABB IRB, KUKA KR) is programmed in its **proprietary language** (RAPID, KRL), but research and education increasingly rely on **Python + ROS** for portability and ecosystem maturity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Robot Programming Languages

Robot programming languages are classified into **three hierarchical levels** based on abstraction:

**Level 1 — Joint-Level (Lowest Abstraction)**
- Direct control of individual joint angles/velocities.
- Used in **Teach Pendants** and **Teach-by-Showing**.
- No task intelligence; pure motion.
- Example: Manual joint jogging on a FANUC controller.

**Level 2 — Manipulator-Level (Mid Abstraction)**
- Motion described in **Cartesian or tool coordinates**.
- Supports variables, I/O, subroutines.
- Example languages: **VAL** (Unimation), **RAPID** (ABB), **KRL** (KUKA), **AS** (Kawasaki).

**Level 3 — Task-Level (Highest Abstraction)**
- Specifies *what* the robot should achieve, not *how*.
- Uses natural-language or symbolic logic.
- Example: **Python**, **ROS Industrial**, **MATLAB Robotics Toolbox**.

### 2.2 Comparison of Robot Programming Languages

| Language | Paradigm | Used In | Strength | Limitation |
|----------|----------|---------|----------|------------|
| **VAL II** | Procedural | Unimation PUMA | Simple motion primitives | Vendor-locked |
| **RAPID** | Modular/PROC | ABB robots | Built-in motion primitives, rapid deployment | Closed-source |
| **KRL** | Structured | KUKA robots | Inline forms, high precision | Steep learning curve |
| **Python** | Multi-paradigm (OOP + script) | Research, ROS, AI integration | Massive library ecosystem (NumPy, OpenCV, PyTorch) | Slower than C++ |
| **C++** | OOP, low-level | ROS core, real-time control | High performance, deterministic | Complex syntax |
| **ROS (Python/C++)** | Distributed | Universal research standard | Modular, hardware-agnostic | Not real-time by default |

### 2.3 Popular Robot Simulators — Analytical Comparison

| Simulator | Physics Engine | License | Best For |
|-----------|----------------|---------|----------|
| **Gazebo** | ODE / Bullet / DART | Open-source (Apache 2.0) | ROS-integrated research |
| **Webots** | Proprietary ODE | Open-source (Apache 2.0) | Education, mobile robots |
| **CoppeliaSim (V-REP)** | Bullet / ODE / Vortex | Educational free / Commercial | Rapid prototyping, swarm |
| **MATLAB Simulink** | MathWorks core | Commercial | Control theory, Model-Based Design |
| **PyBullet** | Bullet | Open-source (Zlib) | Reinforcement learning, ML |

### 2.4 ROS Architectural Pillars

The ROS ecosystem is built on **five core concepts**:

1. **Nodes** — Independent processes performing computation (e.g., camera driver, path planner).
2. **Topics** — Named buses over which nodes exchange messages via **publish-subscribe** pattern.
3. **Messages** — Strongly-typed data structures (`.msg` files) sent over topics.
4. **Services** — Synchronous request/reply communication (client-server).
5. **Master (roscore)** — Name service that enables nodes to find each other.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ROS matters for KTU exams:** ROS decouples hardware from software. A path planner written in ROS Python can run on a TurtleBot3 in Gazebo and a real TurtleBot3 with **zero code change**.

### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Manufacturing**: RAPID/KRL programs run on 1M+ industrial arms globally.
- **Autonomous Vehicles**: ROS 2 powers autonomy stacks at tier-1 research labs.
- **Surgical Robotics**: Python + ROS used in da Vinci research kits.
- **Warehouse Automation**: Amazon Kiva robots use Python + ROS for fleet coordination.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Equation & Concept Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Typical Value / Formula |
|---------------|---------|--------------------------|
| $f_{msg}$ | ROS topic publish frequency | 10 Hz – 100 Hz |
| $B$ | Bandwidth (bytes/sec) | $B = f_{msg} \times s_{msg}$ |
| $s_{msg}$ | Message size | 1 kB – 10 MB (for point clouds) |
| $t_{latency}$ | End-to-end latency | $t_{latency} = t_{pub} + t_{net} + t_{sub}$ |
| $DOF$ | Degrees of freedom | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i$ (joint variables) |
| $T$ | Homogeneous transform matrix | $T \in SE(3)$ |
| $\omega$ | Angular velocity (rad/s) | $\omega = \dot{\theta}$ |
| $v$ | Linear velocity (m/s) | $v = \dot{x}$ |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since this topic is heavily conceptual, the analytical derivations below focus on **communication math**, **kinematic transforms**, and **simulator performance metrics** — all of which are KTU-relevant and frequently asked.

### 3.1 Derivation: ROS Topic Bandwidth Requirement

**Problem Context:** A LiDAR sensor publishes point clouds at 10 Hz. Each point cloud message contains $N$ points, each with 3 floats (x, y, z) plus intensity. Compute the required bandwidth.

**Given:**
- Publish frequency: $f_{msg} = 10$ Hz
- Number of points per scan: $N = 65{,}536$ (16-bit LiDAR)
- Bytes per float: 4 bytes
- Fields per point: 3 (x, y, z) + 1 (intensity)

**Step 1 — Compute message size $s_{msg}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_{msg} &= N \times n_{fields} \times s_{float} \\
        &= 65536 \times 4 \times 4 \text{ bytes} \\
        &= 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bytes} \\
        &= 1.048576 \text{ MB per message}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute bandwidth $B$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= f_{msg} \times s_{msg} \\
  &= 10 \text{ Hz} \times 1.048576 \text{ MB} \\
  &= 10.48576 \text{ MB/s} \\
  &\approx 83.886 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Insight:**
This bandwidth (≈ 84 Mbps) is within Gigabit Ethernet range. Over Wi-Fi (typically 50 Mbps effective), this LiDAR *cannot* run at 10 Hz — a classic exam pitfall. Solutions include **message compression** or **throttling**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: End-to-End Latency in a ROS Pipeline

Consider a perception pipeline: **Camera Node → Detection Node → Control Node**, each running on separate processes.

**Given:**
- Camera publish interval: $T_{pub} = 33$ ms (30 FPS)
- Network transit: $t_{net} = 2$ ms (local UDP)
- Detection processing: $t_{proc} = 25$ ms
- Control processing: $t_{ctrl} = 5$ ms

**Step 1 — Total latency for first frame:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{latency} &= t_{pub} + t_{net} + t_{proc} + t_{ctrl} \\
            &= 33 + 2 + 25 + 5 \\
            &= 65 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Steady-state throughput:**
After warm-up, throughput is bounded by the **slowest stage**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{steady} &= \min\left( \frac{1}{T_{pub}}, \frac{1}{t_{proc}} \right) \\
           &= \min(30.30, 40) \\
           &= 30.30 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum safe control loop frequency:**

$$
f_{control,max} = \frac{1}{2 \times t_{latency}} = \frac{1}{2 \times 0.065} = 7.69 \text{ Hz}
$$

Using the **Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem** analog, control loop must run below $7.69$ Hz for stability in this pipeline.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Degree-of-Freedom (DOF) Calculation for a Manipulator

For an $n$-joint serial manipulator in 3D space:

$$
DOF = 6 \times (n_{links} - n_{ground}) - n_{constraints} + DOF_{end-effector}
$$

**Standard Kuka KR 6 R900 Example (6-joint):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DOF &= 6 \times (7 - 1) - 0 + 0 \\
    &= 36 - 0 \\
    &= 6 \text{ (6 rotational joints, 6 DOF total)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the well-known **6-DOF rule** for full spatial reachability.

**Planar 3R Manipulator Example:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DOF_{planar} &= 3 \times (4 - 1) \\
             &= 9
\end{aligned}
$$

But constrained to planar motion: $9 - 6 = 3$ effective DOF. This matches intuition: 3 joints in a plane → 3 DOF.

---

### 3.4 Homogeneous Transformation — Foundation for Robot Programming

Every robot motion command ultimately translates to a **4×4 homogeneous transformation matrix**:

$$
T = \begin{bmatrix} R & \vec{p} \\ \vec{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $R \in SO(3)$ is the **rotation matrix** and $\vec{p} \in \mathbb{R}^3$ is the **position vector**.

**Rotation about Z-axis by angle $\theta$:**

$$
R_z(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Translation by $\vec{p} = (x, y, z)$:**

$$
\text{Trans}(\vec{p}) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & x \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & z \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Composite transformation:**

$$
T = \text{Trans}(x, y, z) \cdot R_z(\theta)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** The homogeneous matrix belongs to the **Special Euclidean group $SE(3)$**, which is closed under matrix multiplication. This is the mathematical foundation of every robot simulator — Gazebo internally tracks all bodies via $SE(3)$ transforms.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering Use |
|---------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Topic Bandwidth | $B = f_{msg} \times s_{msg}$ | Network sizing |
| Latency | $t = t_{pub} + t_{net} + t_{proc}$ | Real-time control |
| DOF | $6(n - 1) - n_{constraints}$ | Manipulator design |
| Transform | $T \in SE(3)$ | Pose estimation |
| Cycle Time | $T_{cycle} = \frac{1}{f_{ops}}$ | Throughput planning |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define a robot programming language. Differentiate between joint-level and task-level programming with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A robot programming language is a formal set of instructions used to command a robot's motion, sensing, and decision-making.

| Aspect | Joint-Level | Task-Level |
|--------|-------------|------------|
| Abstraction | Lowest | Highest |
| Specifies | Individual joint angles | Goal/objectives |
| Example | Teach pendant jogging | Python + ROS pick-and-place script |
| Intelligence | None | Built-in path planning |

**Mark Split:** Definition (1M) + Joint-level (1M) + Task-level (1M).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any three popular robot simulators and state one key feature of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Simulator | Key Feature |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Gazebo** | Native ROS integration; physics engines ODE/Bullet/DART |
| **Webots** | Cross-platform; ideal for mobile robotics education |
| **CoppeliaSim** | Distributed control architecture; supports multiple languages (Lua, Python, C++) |
| **PyBullet** | Lightweight; preferred for reinforcement learning research |

**Mark Split:** Each simulator with feature = 1 mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of ROS with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of **roscore**, **nodes**, **topics**, and **messages** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare three robot programming languages (VAL, RAPID, Python) on the basis of paradigm, abstraction level, and typical use case. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**Architecture Overview (2 marks):**
ROS follows a **distributed publish-subscribe** architecture. The **roscore** acts as a central name server allowing nodes to discover each other dynamically. Nodes communicate via **topics** (asynchronous) or **services** (synchronous).

**Component Roles (5 marks):**

| Component | Role | Example |
|-----------|------|---------|
| **roscore** | Master process; provides name registration, parameter server, XMLRPC | `roscore` command |
| **Node** | Executable that uses ROS to communicate | Camera driver, planner |
| **Topic** | Named channel for asynchronous stream of messages | `/cmd_vel`, `/laser_scan` |
| **Message** | Strongly typed data structure defined in `.msg` | `Twist`, `LaserScan` |
| **Service** | Synchronous request/reply | `/get_plan` |

**Execution Pattern (Diagrammatic in answer):**

- Node A publishes message on Topic `/scan`.
- Node B subscribes to `/scan`, processes, and publishes to `/cmd_vel`.
- Node C (controller) subscribes to `/cmd_vel`.

This **decouples** producers from consumers — a key ROS design principle.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(b) — 7 Marks

| Criterion | VAL II | RAPID | Python |
|-----------|--------|-------|--------|
| **Paradigm** | Procedural | Modular / Procedural | Multi-paradigm (OOP + script) |
| **Abstraction Level** | Manipulator level | Manipulator level | Task level (with ROS) |
| **Vendor** | Unimation (legacy) | ABB | Open-source |
| **Typical Use Case** | PUMA arms | Modern ABB industrial cells | Research, AI, ROS |
| **Strength** | Simple motion primitives | Built-in motion instructions | Rich AI/ML libraries |
| **Limitation** | Vendor-locked, obsolete | Closed ecosystem | Slower than C++ |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Modern robotics education and research favors **Python + ROS** for portability and ecosystem richness, while industrial deployment retains proprietary languages (RAPID, KRL) for safety certification and deterministic execution.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Gazebo-based robot simulator**. Discuss the role of URDF, physics engines, and sensor plugins. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ROS node publishes LiDAR point clouds at 20 Hz. Each message contains 100,000 points with 4 float fields each. Calculate: (i) message size, (ii) required network bandwidth, (iii) whether a 100 Mbps Ethernet link is sufficient. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Gazebo Architecture (3 marks):**

Gazebo has three core layers:

1. **Physics Layer** — Uses engines like ODE, Bullet, or DART to compute rigid-body dynamics, collisions, and friction.
2. **Sensors Layer** — Emulates LiDAR, cameras, IMUs, force/torque sensors with realistic noise models.
3. **Rendering Layer** — Uses OGRE for 3D visualization and Ray-based sensors for line-of-sight.

**URDF Role (2 marks):**
The **Unified Robot Description Format (URDF)** is an XML file describing the robot's:
- Links (rigid bodies)
- Joints (kinematic constraints)
- Inertial properties (mass, inertia tensor)
- Visual & collision geometry

**Sensor Plugins (2 marks):**
Plugins like `libgazebo_ros_laser.so` simulate a LiDAR by emitting rays, detecting intersections, and publishing `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` messages on ROS topics. This makes the **simulation indistinguishable from hardware** at the ROS message level.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $f_{msg} = 20$ Hz
- $N = 100{,}000$ points
- $n_{fields} = 4$ floats per point
- $s_{float} = 4$ bytes

**Step 1 — Message Size (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_{msg} &= N \times n_{fields} \times s_{float} \\
        &= 100{,}000 \times 4 \times 4 \\
        &= 1{,}600{,}000 \text{ bytes} \\
        &= 1.6 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Required Bandwidth (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= f_{msg} \times s_{msg} \\
  &= 20 \times 1.6 \text{ MB/s} \\
  &= 32 \text{ MB/s} \\
  &= 32 \times 8 \text{ Mbps} \\
  &= 256 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Check vs. 100 Mbps Ethernet (2 marks):**

$$
B_{required} = 256 \text{ Mbps} > B_{link} = 100 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Conclusion:** A **100 Mbps link is NOT sufficient**. We need at least **Gigabit Ethernet (1000 Mbps)** with ~74% utilization headroom, or apply **compression** (e.g., Draco, octree compression) to reduce message size.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> Students frequently make these mistakes and lose 2–3 marks:
> 1. **Confusing Bytes with Bits** — bandwidth is conventionally in **Mbps** (megabits), message size in **MB** (megabytes). Always multiply by 8.
> 2. **Forgetting protocol overhead** — UDP/IP adds ~7% overhead; TCP adds ~10–15%. Real-world bandwidth required = theoretical × 1.15.
> 3. **Ignoring header/footer of ROS message** — adds ~50–100 bytes per message.
> 4. **Calculating only single-message size** without multiplying by frequency.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Robot Programming Languages** are classified into **Joint-Level, Manipulator-Level, Task-Level** based on abstraction.
- **Proprietary languages** (RAPID, KRL, VAL) dominate industry; **Python + ROS** dominate research and education.
- **ROS is a meta-operating system**, NOT a true OS — it provides middleware, not kernel scheduling.
- **ROS Core Components:** Master (roscore), Nodes, Topics, Messages, Services, Parameters.
- **Publish-Subscribe pattern** decouples nodes — enables modular, scalable robot software.
- **Topics** = asynchronous streams; **Services** = synchronous request/reply.
- **Message-passing bandwidth:** $B = f_{msg} \times s_{msg}$ — *always in bits/sec for network design*.
- **LiDAR point clouds** can easily exceed 100 Mbps — drives need for compression or Gigabit Ethernet.
- **Simulators:** Gazebo (ROS standard), Webots (education), PyBullet (ML/RL), CoppeliaSim (rapid prototyping), MATLAB Simulink (model-based control).
- **URDF** = robot description format (XML) used by Gazebo for kinematics + dynamics.
- **Homogeneous transform $T \in SE(3)$** underlies all motion commands — $4 \times 4$ matrix with $3 \times 3$ rotation and $3 \times 1$ translation.
- **DOF formula:** $6(n-1) - n_{constraints}$ for serial manipulators.
- **Python's role in robotics:** glue code, AI/ML integration, ROS scripting, OpenCV, NumPy, PyTorch.
- **Cycle time benchmarks:** industrial arms achieve 0.5–2 s for pick-and-place.
- **Real-time constraint:** ROS is not real-time by default; ROS 2 uses DDS for deterministic communication.
- **KTU exam focus areas:** architecture diagrams, bandwidth calculations, language comparison tables, simulator selection rationale.

---

**End of Module 1 — Topic Notes**
*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 alignment, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels: Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evolution of Robotics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT865 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Evolution of Robotics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Robotics** is the interdisciplinary branch of mechanical engineering, electronic engineering, electrical engineering, information engineering, and computer science that deals with the design, construction, operation, and application of **robots**. The **Evolution of Robotics** refers to the chronological progression of robotic technology from ancient mechanical automata to modern intelligent, AI-driven autonomous systems, encompassing four distinct generations defined by their control architecture, sensing capability, and decision-making autonomy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Wording):**
> *"The Evolution of Robotics traces the technological lineage of programmable, multi-functional manipulators designed to move material, parts, tools, or specialized devices through variable programmed motions for the performance of a variety of tasks, beginning from the First Generation (Pick-and-Place) to the Fourth Generation (Intelligent/Smart Robots)."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (The "Plain English" Picture)

Imagine the journey of a "mechanical helper" over **5,000 years**:
- **Phase 1 — The Mechanical Toy:** Ancient Greek and Chinese inventors built water-powered statues and clockwork birds that mimicked life but had a single fixed motion.
- **Phase 2 — The Industrial Workhorse:** After the 1950s, robots became reprogrammable factory arms that could weld and paint cars with blind precision.
- **Phase 3 — The Sensory Assistant:** In the 1980s, robots gained "eyes" (cameras) and "skin" (force sensors), allowing them to adapt to uncertainty on assembly lines.
- **Phase 4 — The Intelligent Collaborator:** Today, robots learn from data, work alongside humans safely (cobots), and make autonomous decisions using AI.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of robotics evolution like the evolution of mobile phones — from the brick-sized **Motorola DynaTAC (1983)** that only made calls, to today's **smartphone** that sees, hears, learns, and talks. Robots moved from "dumb repeaters" to "thinking collaborators."

## 1.3 Standard Classification Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

| Term | Standard Value/Definition |
|---|---|
| **Generations of Robots** | **4 Generations** (1st: Playback, 2nd: Sensory, 3rd: Intelligent, 4th: Networked/AI) |
| **DOF (Degrees of Freedom)** | Mechanical **rigid-body** mobility count |
| **Payload** | Measured in **kg** (kilograms) |
| **Reach** | Measured in **mm** (millimetres) |
| **Repeatability** | Measured in **±0.02 mm** (ISO 9283 standard) |
| **First Industrial Robot** | **Unimate** (1961), Devolved by George Devol & Joseph Engelberger |
| **Three Laws of Robotics** | Formulated by **Isaac Asimov (1942)** in *I, Robot* |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Phased Evolution — Structured Logical Breakdown

The evolution of robotics is universally classified into **Four Generations** based on the **control architecture** and **intelligence hierarchy**. Each generational leap required breakthroughs in **actuators, sensors, control theory, and computing**.

### 🔹 Generation 1: Playback Robots (1954–1970)
- **Control:** Pre-programmed, open-loop (no feedback).
- **Capability:** Repeat a fixed sequence of motions blindly.
- **Sensing:** **None** — purely mechanical repetition.
- **Example:** **Unimate (1961)** at General Motors, die-casting and welding.
- **Limitation:** Cannot adapt to environmental change. A misplaced part = failure.

### 🔹 Generation 2: Sensory (Feedback) Robots (1970–1985)
- **Control:** **Closed-loop** servo control with external sensors.
- **Capability:** Adjust motion in real time using feedback.
- **Sensing:** **Tactile, force, vision, proximity sensors** introduced.
- **Example:** **ASEA IRB-6 (1974)**, Cincinnati Milacron T3 (1973).
- **Limitation:** Still requires structured environment; no autonomous decision-making.

### 🔹 Generation 3: Intelligent Robots (1985–2000)
- **Control:** **Microprocessor + PC-based** with onboard decision logic.
- **Capability:** Conditional branching, task-level commands, vision-guided assembly.
- **Sensing:** Multi-modal sensor fusion (vision + force + ultrasonic).
- **Example:** **SCARA robots** for PCB assembly, Fanuc Yellow Series.
- **Limitation:** Limited learning ability; no self-improvement.

### 🔹 Generation 4: Networked / AI Robots (2000–Present)
- **Control:** **AI/ML, IoT, Cloud, Edge-AI** distributed intelligence.
- **Capability:** Self-learning, predictive maintenance, human-robot collaboration (HRC).
- **Sensing:** 3D vision, LiDAR, tactile skins, IMUs, NLP-based interfaces.
- **Example:** **Boston Dynamics Spot, Universal Robots UR5e, ABB YuMi, Tesla Optimus**.
- **Limitation:** Still constrained by ethical, safety, and computational limits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Frequently Asked:** *"Distinguish between the generations of robots."* — A comparative table (see Section 5) is the highest-mark-yield revision tool.

## 2.2 Historical Timeline (Mandatory for KTU Board)

| Year | Milestone | Inventor / Organization |
|---|---|---|
| **~350 BC** | Aristotle's automata (mechanical puppets) | Greek philosophers |
| **1495** | Leonardo's Mechanical Knight | Leonardo da Vinci |
| **1738** | Digesting Duck (automaton) | Jacques de Vaucanson |
| **1921** | Term "Robot" coined (play *R.U.R.*) | **Karel Čapek** (Czech) |
| **1942** | **Three Laws of Robotics** | **Isaac Asimov** |
| **1954** | First programmable robot patent | **George Devol** |
| **1961** | **Unimate** deployed at GM | Engelberger & Devol |
| **1973** | **Cincinnati Milacron T3** (first commercially available) | Cincinnati Milacron |
| **1979** | **Stanford Cart** (first autonomous vehicle) | Stanford AI Lab |
| **1984** | **SCARA** robot invented | Hiroshi Makino (Yamanashi Univ.) |
| **1996** | Honda P2 humanoid prototype | Honda Motor Co. |
| **2002** | **Roomba** (consumer robot vacuum) | iRobot |
| **2013** | **Rethink Robotics Baxter** (first modern cobot) | Rodney Brooks |
| **2016** | **Hanson Sophia** (citizen robot, Saudi Arabia) | David Hanson |
| **2021+** | Tesla Optimus, Figure 01, AI-driven humanoid boom | Industry 4.0 |

## 2.3 Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics (Mandatory for 2-Mark Questions)

> [!NOTE]
> **Asimov's Three Laws (formulated 1942, used as the moral foundation of modern robotics ethics):**
>
> **Law 1:** A robot may not injure a human being or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm.
>
> **Law 2:** A robot must obey the orders given by human beings, except where such orders conflict with the **First Law**.
>
> **Law 3:** A robot must protect its own existence, as long as this protection does not conflict with the **First or Second Law**.
>
> *(Law 0 — added later):* A robot may not harm humanity, or, by inaction, allow humanity to come to harm.

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Conceptual Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Evolution of Robotics, formulas are replaced by canonical definitions.** Memorize the following table verbatim — it appears in **80% of KTU short-answer questions**.

| # | Concept | Canonical Definition (Examiner's Wording) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Robot (RIA Definition)** | *"A reprogrammable, multifunctional manipulator designed to move material, parts, tools, or specialized devices through variable programmed motions for the performance of a variety of tasks."* |
| 2 | **First Generation** | Playback / Pick-and-Place robots with no sensors |
| 3 | **Second Generation** | Sensory robots using closed-loop feedback control |
| 4 | **Third Generation** | Intelligent robots with microprocessor-based decision logic |
| 5 | **Fourth Generation** | AI-enabled, networked, collaborative robots (cobots) |
| 6 | **Father of Robotics** | **Joseph F. Engelberger** (industry) and **George Devol** (inventor) |
| 7 | **Three Laws** | Issac Asimov (1942), in the short-story collection *I, Robot* |
| 8 | **Term "Robot"** | Coined by **Karel Čapek** (1921), derived from Czech *robota* = "forced labor" |
| 9 | **Cobot** | Collaborative robot, designed for safe human-robot workspace sharing (ISO/TS 15066) |
| 10 | **ISO 9283** | International standard for **measuring robotic performance and repeatability** |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing:** Evolution of welding/painting robots reduced automotive assembly-line cost by **~30–40%** (GM, Ford, Tesla gigafactories).
- **Healthcare:** From da Vinci Surgical System (2000) to autonomous pharmacy robots.
- **Space:** Mars rovers (Sojourner → Perseverance) are direct descendants of Generation 3→4 evolution.
- **Agriculture:** John Deere's autonomous tractors stem from GPS + vision + AI fusion (Gen-4).
- **Disaster Response:** Fukushima (2011) and Chernobyl sarcophagus projects (2024) rely on Generation-4 teleoperated/AI robots.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Generational Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> Since "Evolution of Robotics" is a **conceptual/historical** topic, the "derivation" required by KTU 2024 is a **rigorous comparative analytical matrix** and a **chronological decoding exercise**. Both are presented below with exhaustive step-by-step reasoning.

## 3.1 Exhaustive Comparative Matrix: Four Generations of Robots

**Analytical Approach (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1:** Identify the **four engineering parameters** that define a robot's evolutionary level:
   (a) **Control Architecture** — what decides the robot's motion?
   (b) **Sensing Modality** — what information does the robot gather?
   (c) **Intelligence Level** — can it adapt/learn?
   (d) **Human-Robot Interaction** — does it work alone or with humans?

**Step 2:** Map each generation onto these four parameters.

**Step 3:** Cross-validate against canonical robotics textbooks (Spong, Hutchinson & Vidyasagar; Craig; Niku).

| Parameter | **Gen 1 (1954–70)** | **Gen 2 (1970–85)** | **Gen 3 (1985–2000)** | **Gen 4 (2000–Present)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Control Architecture** | Open-loop, mechanical cams, limit switches, **hydraulic playback** | Closed-loop **servo control**, analog feedback, joint encoders | Microprocessor-based, **PC-based controllers**, PLC integration | **AI/ML controllers**, cloud-edge distributed, ROS 2 architecture |
| **Sensing Modality** | **None** (blind) | Tactile, force, simple vision, proximity | Multi-modal: vision (CCD cameras), ultrasonic, IR, force-torque fusion | 3D depth cameras, **LiDAR, IMU, tactile skin arrays, NLP microphones** |
| **Intelligence Level** | Zero — fixed sequence | Low — conditional reaction | Medium — task-level planning, limited decision trees | High — **deep learning, reinforcement learning, generative AI** |
| **Human Interaction** | Caged, isolated | Caged, isolated | Caged, supervised | **Collaborative (cobot), shared workspace, voice-driven** |
| **Programming Method** | Mechanical stops, drum sequencer | Teach pendant (point-to-point) | Off-line programming, **graphical simulation** | **Natural language, imitation learning, vision-based teach** |
| **Decision Capability** | **None** | Reactive (error-correcting) | **Deliberative (rule-based)** | **Cognitive (probabilistic + neural)** |
| **Typical Application** | Die-casting, spot welding | Arc welding, paint spraying | PCB assembly, precision insertion | Surgery, autonomous mobility, human assistance |
| **Representative Robot** | Unimate 2000A | ASEA IRB-6 | Fanuc S-420, ABB IRB-2000 | **Universal Robots UR5e, Boston Dynamics Atlas, Tesla Optimus** |
| **Cost (Indicative)** | **~$30,000** (1961, inflation-adjusted) | **~$50,000** | **~$75,000–$150,000** | **$20,000 (UR5e) — $1M+ (humanoid)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked *"Compare any two generations,"* always structure your answer using the **5-row matrix** above (Control / Sensing / Intelligence / Application / Example). This single trick secures **6 out of 7 marks** in a 7-mark sub-part.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Decoding — A KTU Model Numerical Reasoning Question

**Question Pattern (Examiner's Style):** *"A factory wants to upgrade from a Gen-1 Unimate-style welder to a Gen-4 collaborative welding cobot. List 4 critical engineering changes required in the robotic cell."*

**Solution (Step-by-Step exhaustive working):**

**Step 1: Identify the baseline.**
The Gen-1 Unimate operates in an **open-loop, no-feedback, caged environment**. We compare this baseline to Gen-4 requirements.

**Step 2: Change #1 — Sensing Infrastructure.**
Gen-1 → **No sensors**.
Gen-4 → **Multi-modal sensor array** (3D vision for seam tracking, force-torque for arc-length control, thermal camera for weld-pool monitoring).
*Engineering Impact:* Adds **~$15,000–$30,000** per cell, but reduces **rework cost by 40%**.

**Step 3: Change #2 — Control Architecture.**
Gen-1 → Hydraulic playback controller (analog).
Gen-4 → **Digital twin + AI-driven motion planner running on ROS 2 / EtherCAT** with sub-millisecond cycle times.
*Engineering Impact:* Programming time drops from **8 hours to 30 minutes** (vision-based teach).

**Step 4: Change #3 — Safety Infrastructure.**
Gen-1 → Physical cage, light curtains.
Gen-4 → **Power-and-force limiting (PFL) actuators, ISO/TS 15066 compliance, collision-detection algorithms**.
*Engineering Impact:* Floor space reduces by **~60%** (cage removal).

**Step 5: Change #4 — Human-Robot Collaboration.**
Gen-1 → Operator fully separated.
Gen-4 → **Side-by-side cobot operation**, operator loads blanks while cobot welds.
*Engineering Impact:* Cycle time reduces by **~25%** and labor cost by **~50%**.

**Step 6: Summarize the ROI equation (conceptual).**

$$
\text{ROI} = \frac{\Delta \text{Labor Cost} + \Delta \text{Quality Cost} + \Delta \text{Downtime Cost}}{\text{CapEx}_{\text{new}} - \text{CapEx}_{\text{old}}}
$$

For a typical Gen-1 → Gen-4 upgrade, ROI break-even occurs in **18–24 months**, which is the standard industry benchmark KTU examiners expect.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*
**Define a robot as per the Robotics Institute of America (RIA) standards.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
According to the **Robotics Institute of America (RIA)**, a robot is defined as:

> *"A reprogrammable, multifunctional manipulator designed to move material, parts, tools, or specialized devices through variable programmed motions for the performance of a variety of tasks."*

**Key terms to be highlighted in the answer for full marks:**
1. **Reprogrammable** → distinguishes from hard automation (1 mark)
2. **Multifunctional** → can perform multiple tasks (1 mark)
3. **Variable programmed motions** → the motion sequence is software-driven, not mechanical (1 mark)

---

### **Q2.** *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]*
**State and explain Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **First Law:** A robot may not injure a human being or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm. *(1 mark)*
2. **Second Law:** A robot must obey the orders given by human beings, except where such orders conflict with the First Law. *(1 mark)*
3. **Third Law:** A robot must protect its own existence, as long as this protection does not conflict with the First or Second Law. *(1 mark)*

These laws were formulated by **Isaac Asimov in 1942** in his short-story collection *I, Robot* and form the ethical foundation of modern robotics.

---

## 📗 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Rule:** Answer **ONE** of the following two full questions (each carries **14 marks**). Each question has sub-parts (a) of 7 marks and (b) of 7 marks.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
*[KTU University Exam — May 2023]*

**(a)** Explain in detail the **First and Second Generations of robots** with suitable examples and clearly differentiate them based on control, sensing, and intelligence. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **historical evolution of robotics** from ancient automata to modern industrial robots, highlighting at least **6 key milestones**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q(a) (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Robotic generations classify robots based on the **control architecture, sensing capability, and intelligence** — introduced as the framework by the Japan Industrial Robot Association (JIRA) and widely adopted in KTU curriculum.

**First Generation — Playback Robots (3 marks):**
- **Era:** 1954–1970
- **Control:** Open-loop playback. No feedback mechanism.
- **Sensing:** **None** — the robot blindly replays a pre-taught motion sequence.
- **Intelligence:** Zero. Decision-making is absent.
- **Programming:** Mechanical stops, drum sequencer, or teach pendant with limit switches.
- **Example:** **Unimate (1961)** — installed at General Motors for die-casting and spot welding.
- **Limitation:** Any positional change in the part → robot fails. Environment must be highly structured.

**Second Generation — Sensory / Feedback Robots (3 marks):**
- **Era:** 1970–1985
- **Control:** **Closed-loop servo control** with positional feedback.
- **Sensing:** **Tactile, force, simple vision, proximity** sensors.
- **Intelligence:** Low-level reactive control — can detect and correct errors.
- **Programming:** Teach pendant (point-to-point or continuous path) + sensor-driven decision trees.
- **Example:** **ASEA IRB-6 (1974)**, Cincinnati Milacron T3 (1973).
- **Limitation:** Still requires structured environment; no autonomous planning.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q(b) (7 marks)

**Historical Milestones (1 mark each = 6 marks, Concluding remark = 1 mark):**

| Year | Milestone |
|---|---|
| **~350 BC** | Aristotle conceives mechanical automata; Greek engineers build water-powered statues. |
| **1495** | **Leonardo da Vinci** designs a mechanical knight (programmable automaton). |
| **1921** | Czech playwright **Karel Čapek** coins the term **"Robot"** in his play *R.U.R. (Rossum's Universal Robots)*. |
| **1942** | **Isaac Asimov** formulates the **Three Laws of Robotics** in *I, Robot*. |
| **1954** | **George Devol** patents the first programmable robotic arm. |
| **1961** | **Unimate** becomes the **first industrial robot** at a General Motors plant — the dawn of factory automation. |
| **1973** | Cincinnati Milacron **T3** is the first commercially available computer-controlled robot. |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The journey from Aristotle's automata to the Unimate illustrates a **5,000-year shift** from mechanical mimicry to reprogrammable, software-driven industrial automation — setting the stage for the intelligent robots of today.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**
*[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]*

**(a)** Explain the **Third and Fourth Generations of robots** with suitable examples. Discuss how AI and IoT have transformed modern collaborative robots (cobots). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **all four generations of robots** using a structured analytical table. Why are **cobots considered the future of Industry 4.0**? **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q(a) (7 marks)

**Third Generation — Intelligent Robots (3 marks):**
- **Era:** 1985–2000
- **Control:** **Microprocessor + PC-based** controllers (off-line programming).
- **Sensing:** Multi-modal sensor fusion — vision (CCD cameras), force/torque, ultrasonic.
- **Intelligence:** **Rule-based decision-making**, conditional branching, basic task-level planning.
- **Example:** **SCARA robots** (Hiroshi Makino, 1984) for PCB assembly; Fanuc S-420 series; ABB IRB-2000.
- **Application:** Precision insertion, electronics assembly, vision-guided pick-and-place.

**Fourth Generation — AI/Networked Robots (4 marks):**
- **Control:** Distributed intelligence — **AI, ML, IoT, Cloud-Edge computing**.
- **Sensing:** 3D depth cameras, **LiDAR, IMU, tactile skin arrays, NLP microphones**.
- **Intelligence:** Deep learning, reinforcement learning, **generative AI planners** (e.g., RT-2, PaLM-E).
- **Example:** **Universal Robots UR5e, Boston Dynamics Spot/Atlas, Tesla Optimus, Figure 01**.
- **Cobot Transformation:**
  - **AI-based collision detection** → ISO/TS 15066 compliance.
  - **Vision-based teach** → programming time reduced from 8 hours to 30 minutes.
  - **Force-limiting actuators** → safe side-by-side operation with humans (no cage).
  - **Cloud connectivity** → fleet learning, predictive maintenance, OTA updates.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q(b) (7 marks)

**Comparative Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Gen 1 | Gen 2 | Gen 3 | Gen 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Control | Open-loop | Closed-loop | Microprocessor | AI/Cloud |
| Sensing | None | Tactile/Force | Multi-modal | 3D Vision/LiDAR/AI |
| Intelligence | None | Reactive | Deliberative | Cognitive |
| HRI | Caged | Caged | Supervised | Collaborative |
| Example | Unimate | IRB-6 | SCARA | UR5e/Optimus |

**Why Cobots = Future of Industry 4.0 (3 marks):**
1. **Zero-Cage Operation** — Saves **~60% floor space**; no light curtains needed.
2. **Easy Programming** — Vision/voice teach reduces integration cost by **~70%**.
3. **AI-Powered Adaptability** — Learns from human demonstrations; supports **mass customization**.
4. **Human-Robot Synergy** — Combines human cognitive flexibility with robot precision/repeatability.
5. **Data-Driven Manufacturing** — Integrates with **MES/ERP** for closed-loop production analytics.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the "father of robotics":** It is **Joseph F. Engelberger** (industry) — not George Devol. Devol is the **inventor of the first patent**. Both are credited differently.
> 2. **Misattributing the term "Robot":** It was coined by **Karel Čapek** (Czech, 1921) — but his brother **Josef Čapek** originally suggested the word. KTU accepts Karel as the canonical answer.
> 3. **Skipping examples:** A 7-mark question on generations **without examples** (Unimate, IRB-6, SCARA, UR5e) loses a **guaranteed 1.5–2 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting Asimov's year:** Always mention **1942** + *I, Robot* collection.
> 5. **Treating "cobot" loosely:** A cobot is specifically a robot compliant with **ISO/TS 15066** — mentioning this standard fetches an **extra 0.5 mark bonus** in valuation.

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following 12 bullet points are the highest-frequency KTU 2024 exam items for "Evolution of Robotics." Memorize verbatim.**

- ✅ The **term "Robot"** was coined by **Karel Čapek (1921)** in the play *R.U.R.* — derived from Czech *robota* (forced labor).
- ✅ **Joseph F. Engelberger** is the **"Father of Robotics"** (industry deployment); **George Devol** is the **inventor** of the first programmable arm.
- ✅ **Unimate (1961)** at General Motors = **first industrial robot** (die-casting, spot welding).
- ✅ **Isaac Asimov's Three Laws (1942)** — in *I, Robot* — form the ethical foundation of modern robotics.
- ✅ **Generation 1:** Playback, no sensors, open-loop, **Unimate**.
- ✅ **Generation 2:** Sensory feedback, closed-loop, **ASEA IRB-6 (1974)**, Cincinnati Milacron T3.
- ✅ **Generation 3:** Microprocessor-based, multi-modal sensing, **SCARA (1984)**, Fanuc.
- ✅ **Generation 4:** AI/ML, IoT, cloud-edge, **cobots** — UR5e, Boston Dynamics Spot, Tesla Optimus.
- ✅ **Cobots** comply with **ISO/TS 15066** for safe human-robot collaboration.
- ✅ **Leonardo da Vinci (1495)** designed the first known **programmable mechanical knight**.
- ✅ **RIA Definition of Robot:** *"A reprogrammable, multifunctional manipulator designed to move material, parts, tools, or specialized devices through variable programmed motions…"*
- ✅ **Future direction:** Humanoid general-purpose robots, embodied AI, swarm robotics, AGI-integrated manipulators.

topics.title: Types of Robots and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT865 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Robots and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme Robotics and Automation (PEITT865), **robots are classified** based on several orthogonal taxonomies: (a) **Mechanical Configuration / Kinematic Structure**, (b) **Generation / Control Intelligence**, (c) **Drive Technology / Power Source**, and (d) **Application Domain / Working Environment**. The selection of a robot type for a given automation task is dictated by the **degrees of freedom (DOF)**, **workspace geometry**, **payload capacity**, **repeatability**, and the **nature of the task** (continuous path vs. point-to-point).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 2):** Students must be able to *classify robots based on configuration, generation, control, and drive*, and must be able to *map applications to robot types* across industrial, medical, space, underwater, and service domains.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **workshop toolbox**: just as you would not use a hammer to tighten a screw, you would not use a heavy 6-axis articulated arm to pick-and-place a tiny PCB component. Robot selection is a **tool-matching problem**. A *Cartesian gantry* is like a ruler sliding on rails (precise in straight lines), a *SCARA* is like a shoulder that pivots horizontally (great for top-down assembly), and a *6-axis articulated arm* is like a human arm reaching into a car chassis (ultimate flexibility). The **type of robot** is fundamentally a function of **what shape of space the robot must reach** and **how intelligently it must decide** while doing so.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Engineering Metric:** Robot repeatability is measured in **millimeters (mm)** or **micrometers (μm)**, while payload is measured in **kilograms (kg)** or **Newtons (N)**. Industrial repeatability typically lies in the range **±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm** for high-precision arms.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of Robot Classification (KTU High-Yield)

| Pillar | Question Answered | Key Categories |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical Configuration** | *What is the geometry of the arm?* | Cartesian, Cylindrical, Polar/Spherical, SCARA, Articulated, Parallel |
| **Control / Generation** | *How smart is the controller?* | Playback (Gen-1), Teach-in (Gen-2), CNC/Programmed (Gen-3), Adaptive/Sensor-based (Gen-4), AI/Cognitive (Gen-5) |
| **Application Domain** | *Where is it deployed?* | Industrial Manipulator, Mobile (AGV/AMR), Humanoid, Medical, Underwater (ROV), Aerial (UAV/Drone), Space |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification by Mechanical Configuration

A robot's mechanical configuration is determined by the **type of joints** and their arrangement. Each joint type contributes one DOF. The sum of all joint DOFs equals the robot's **degrees of freedom**.

#### 2.1.1 Cartesian (Gantry / Rectangular / XYZ) Robot

- **Structure:** Three linear (prismatic, **P**) joints arranged mutually perpendicular along **X, Y, Z**.
- **Total DOF:** Typically **3 DOF** (often extended with a wrist for 4–6 DOF).
- **Workspace Geometry:** A **rectangular cuboid** box.
- **Mathematical Workspace Volume:**

$$
V_{\text{cartesian}} = X_{\text{range}} \times Y_{\text{range}} \times Z_{\text{range}}
$$

- **Real-World Use:** Pick-and-place of heavy loads, CNC loading, palletizing, 3D printing, semiconductor wafer handling.
- **Why it works:** Prismatic joints are the *simplest and most rigid* joint type — they can carry the highest payload per unit of complexity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** A Cartesian robot is a **3D plotter** scaled up to factory size. Every motion is along one of three orthogonal axes, making kinematics trivial — the inverse kinematics has a **closed-form, unique solution**.

#### 2.1.2 Cylindrical Robot

- **Structure:** One revolute (**R**) joint at the base + two prismatic (**P**) joints (vertical lift, radial reach).
- **Configuration String:** **RPP** (Base rotation → Vertical lift → Radial extension).
- **Workspace Geometry:** A **hollow cylinder**.
- **Mathematical Workspace Volume:**

$$
V_{\text{cylindrical}} = \pi \cdot \left(R_{\max}^2 - R_{\min}^2\right) \cdot H
$$

where $R_{\max}$ and $R_{\min}$ are the maximum and minimum radial reach, and $H$ is the vertical lift height.

- **Real-World Use:** Machine tending in lathe operations, foundry casting handling, simple assembly.

#### 2.1.3 Polar (Spherical) Robot

- **Structure:** Two revolute joints + one prismatic joint → **RRP** configuration.
- **Workspace Geometry:** A portion of a **sphere** (hemispherical shell).
- **Mathematical Workspace Volume:**

$$
V_{\text{polar}} = \frac{2}{3}\pi \cdot \left(R_{\max}^3 - R_{\min}^3\right) \cdot \cos(\theta_{\text{tilt}})
$$

where $\theta_{\text{tilt}}$ is the angular bound of the prismatic (telescoping) arm.

- **Real-World Use:** Early Unimate robots in die-casting, forging, and spot welding; obsolete in modern greenfield plants.

#### 2.1.4 SCARA Robot (Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm)

- **Structure:** Two parallel revolute joints in the horizontal plane + one prismatic joint vertical → **RRP** with parallel axis constraint.
- **Key Property:** **Selective Compliance** — *compliant* (flexible) in the X-Y horizontal plane, *rigid* along the Z vertical axis.
- **Workspace Geometry:** A **sector of an annulus** in the horizontal plane.
- **Mathematical Workspace Area (top view):**

$$
A_{\text{SCARA}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \theta_{\text{base}} \cdot \left(L_1 + L_2\right)^2 - \frac{1}{2} \cdot \theta_{\text{base}} \cdot \left(\vert L_1 - L_2 \vert\right)^2
$$

where $L_1$ and $L_2$ are the two link lengths and $\theta_{\text{base}}$ is the angular sweep.

- **Real-World Use:** High-speed PCB assembly, small parts insertion (press-fit), pick-and-place on conveyor, screwdriving.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SCARA is the workhorse of electronics assembly** because its horizontal rigidity combined with vertical compliance allows fast, precise insertions without binding.

#### 2.1.5 Articulated (Jointed-Arm / Anthropomorphic) Robot

- **Structure:** All revolute joints — typically **6R** (six rotational joints) for full spatial dexterity.
- **Workspace Geometry:** A **complex, near-spherical** envelope with internal voids.
- **Key Metric:** The **Dexterous Workspace** is the subset reachable in all orientations.
- **Real-World Use:** Automotive welding, painting, machine tending, complex assembly, material removal.

#### 2.1.6 Parallel (Delta) Robot

- **Structure:** Three (or more) arms connected in parallel between a fixed base and a moving end-effector platform.
- **Key Property:** **Low moving mass** → extremely high accelerations (up to **30 g** for pick-and-place).
- **Real-World Use:** Ultra-high-speed packaging, food sorting, pharmaceutical pick-and-place.

### 2.2 Classification by Generation / Control Intelligence

| Generation | Name | Key Capability | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gen 1** | Playback Robot | Records and replays a taught trajectory; no sensory feedback | Early Unimate on assembly line |
| **Gen 2** | Teach-In / Sequence Robot | Taught sequence of points; limited I/O | Welding robots in 1980s |
| **Gen 3** | CNC / Programmed Robot | Offline programming via textual languages (e.g., VAL, RAPID) | ABB IRB series in CNC cells |
| **Gen 4** | Sensor-Adaptive Robot | Uses vision, force, tactile feedback to modify behavior | Bin-picking, force-controlled assembly |
| **Gen 5** | AI / Cognitive Robot | Machine-learning-based perception, planning, and decision-making | Modern collaborative robots (cobots) with vision AI |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Relationship | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Workspace volume, Cartesian | $V = X \cdot Y \cdot Z$ | m³ | Box geometry |
| 2 | Workspace volume, Cylindrical | $V = \pi (R_{\max}^2 - R_{\min}^2) H$ | m³ | Hollow cylinder |
| 3 | Workspace area, SCARA | $A = \tfrac{1}{2}\theta(L_1 + L_2)^2 - \tfrac{1}{2}\theta \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert^2$ | m² | Annular sector |
| 4 | Reach length, 2-link arm | $r = \sqrt{L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2}$ | m | Law of cosines |
| 5 | Payload–speed trade-off | $T_{\text{motor}} \propto m_{\text{payload}} \cdot g \cdot r$ | N·m | Torque ∝ load × arm |
| 6 | Degrees of freedom (Grübler) | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | integer | $n$ links, $j_1$ 1-DOF, $j_2$ 2-DOF joints |
| 7 | Repeatability vs. accuracy | $\text{Accuracy} = \text{Bias} + \text{Repeatability}$ | mm | Both in same units |
| 8 | Acceleration, delta robot | $a_{\max} \approx \dfrac{\tau_{\text{motor}}}{m_{\text{platform}}}$ | m/s² | High a due to low m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Grübler's Formula** is the cornerstone of mechanism analysis in KTU exams — it gives the **mobility** $M$ (DOF) of a planar mechanism. For spatial mechanisms, use $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$. **Memorize the planar form.**

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The classification system is not academic — it directly drives **capital expenditure (CAPEX)** decisions in Industry 4.0 factories. A SCARA costs roughly **₹8–15 lakh**, a 6-axis articulated arm **₹25–80 lakh**, and a delta robot **₹40–1.5 crore**. Choosing the wrong type causes either **over-engineering** (wasted CAPEX) or **bottleneck failures** (production losses). In KTU viva voce, students must justify robot selection using the **DOF–Workspace–Payload–Repeatability** trade-off framework.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Reach and Inverse Kinematics for a 2-Link Planar Arm

This derivation is the **most-asked analytical problem** in KTU Module 2 examinations. We will derive both **forward kinematics** (joint angles → end-effector position) and **inverse kinematics** (desired position → joint angles).

#### 3.1.1 Given Data

- A planar 2-link arm with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$.
- Joint angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ (both measured from the previous link).
- Target end-effector position $(x, y)$ in the base frame.

#### 3.1.2 Forward Kinematics Derivation

Using homogeneous transformation matrices, each link contributes a rotation $\theta_i$ and a translation $L_i$ along its local $x$-axis:

$$
A_i = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i & 0 & L_i\cos\theta_i \\ \sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i & 0 & L_i\sin\theta_i \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1:** Write the individual link transforms.

$$
A_1 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_1 & -\sin\theta_1 & 0 & L_1\cos\theta_1 \\ \sin\theta_1 & \cos\theta_1 & 0 & L_1\sin\theta_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
A_2 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_2 & -\sin\theta_2 & 0 & L_2\cos\theta_2 \\ \sin\theta_2 & \cos\theta_2 & 0 & L_2\sin\theta_2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Multiply $A_1 \cdot A_2$. Extract the position components from the resulting $4 \times 4$ matrix.

$$
x = L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

$$
y = L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

**Step 3 (Verification):** Apply the trigonometric identity $\cos(A+B) = \cos A\cos B - \sin A\sin B$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2(\cos\theta_1\cos\theta_2 - \sin\theta_1\sin\theta_2) \\
x &= (L_1 + L_2\cos\theta_2)\cos\theta_1 - L_2\sin\theta_1\sin\theta_2
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the standard form. Similarly for $y$. The forward kinematics derivation is complete.

#### 3.1.3 Inverse Kinematics Derivation

**Step 1:** Compute $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
r^2 &= [L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)]^2 + [L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)]^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Expand and apply $\sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1$ to all cross-terms.

$$
\begin{aligned}
r^2 &= L_1^2\cos^2\theta_1 + 2L_1L_2\cos\theta_1\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) + L_2^2\cos^2(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\
    &\quad + L_1^2\sin^2\theta_1 + 2L_1L_2\sin\theta_1\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) + L_2^2\sin^2(\theta_1+\theta_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Combine like terms using the cosine difference identity $\cos(A-B) = \cos A\cos B + \sin A\sin B$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
r^2 &= L_1^2(\cos^2\theta_1 + \sin^2\theta_1) + L_2^2(\cos^2(\theta_1+\theta_2) + \sin^2(\theta_1+\theta_2)) + 2L_1L_2\cos\theta_2 \\
r^2 &= L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2L_1L_2\cos\theta_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $\theta_2$ (the **elbow angle**).

$$
\cos\theta_2 = \frac{r^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2L_1L_2}
$$

$$
\theta_2 = \pm \arccos\!\left(\frac{x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2L_1L_2}\right)
$$

The **±** sign gives the **elbow-up** and **elbow-down** solutions (two configurations for the same $(x,y)$).

**Step 5:** Solve for $\theta_1$ using the intermediate variable $\phi = \operatorname{atan2}(y, x)$:

$$
\theta_1 = \operatorname{atan2}(y, x) - \operatorname{atan2}\!\left(L_2\sin\theta_2,\ L_1 + L_2\cos\theta_2\right)
$$

**Step 6 (Reachability check):** A point is reachable only if $r \le L_1 + L_2$ AND $r \ge \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$. This is the **annular workspace** of the arm.

$$
\vert L_1 - L_2 \vert \le r \le L_1 + L_2
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Selecting the Right Robot

| Application | Recommended Robot | Why? | Typical Repeatability |
|---|---|---|---|
| PCB pick-and-place | SCARA | Fast horizontal motion, vertical compliance | ±0.02 mm |
| Automotive body welding | 6-axis articulated | Reach into car chassis from many angles | ±0.05 mm |
| Heavy palletizing | Cartesian gantry or 4-axis articulated | High payload over large rectangular volume | ±0.1 mm |
| Pharma blister packing | Delta (parallel) | Ultra-high speed, low moving mass | ±0.05 mm |
| CNC machine tending | 6-axis articulated | Handles varied part geometries | ±0.05 mm |
| Spray painting | 6-axis articulated (explosion-proof) | Continuous path, complex curvature | ±0.2 mm |
| Bin-picking | 6-axis + vision (Gen-4) | Adaptive grasping from chaos | ±0.5 mm |
| Surgical assistant | 6-axis / 7-axis (surgical-grade) | Extreme precision, tremor filtering | ±0.01 mm |
| Warehouse logistics | Mobile robot (AMR) | Navigation in human spaces | ±10 mm |
| Underwater inspection | ROV / AUV | Pressure-rated, tether or autonomous | ±50 mm |

### 3.3 Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A 2-link planar manipulator has $L_1 = 4$ m, $L_2 = 3$ m. Compute the joint angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ to reach the point $(x, y) = (5, 2)$ m. Use the **elbow-down** configuration.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute $r^2 = x^2 + y^2 = 25 + 4 = 29$, so $r = \sqrt{29} \approx 5.385$ m.

**Step 2:** Verify reachability. $\vert L_1 - L_2 \vert = 1$ m and $L_1 + L_2 = 7$ m. Since $1 \le 5.385 \le 7$, the point is reachable. ✓

**Step 3:** Compute $\cos\theta_2$.

$$
\cos\theta_2 = \frac{r^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2} = \frac{29 - 16 - 9}{2 \cdot 4 \cdot 3} = \frac{4}{24} = 0.1667
$$

**Step 4:** $\theta_2 = \arccos(0.1667) \approx 80.41°$. For elbow-down, take the positive angle.

**Step 5:** Compute $\sin\theta_2 = \sqrt{1 - 0.1667^2} \approx 0.9860$.

**Step 6:** Compute $\theta_1$.

$$
\theta_1 = \operatorname{atan2}(2, 5) - \operatorname{atan2}(3 \cdot 0.9860,\ 4 + 3 \cdot 0.1667)
$$

$$
\theta_1 = 21.80° - \operatorname{atan2}(2.958,\ 4.500) = 21.80° - 33.33° = -11.53°
$$

**Final Answer:** $\theta_1 \approx -11.53°$, $\theta_2 \approx 80.41°$. (Negative $\theta_1$ indicates a clockwise rotation from the base $x$-axis.)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
