The phrase **"programmable in three or more axes"** is the critical qualifier. A simple hard-wired pick-and-place mechanism with only 2 axes does **not** qualify as an industrial robot under KTU 2024 scheme evaluation standards.

**Key defining attributes:**
- **Reprogrammable** — task logic can be altered without physical reconstruction
- **Multipurpose** — capable of being adapted to a range of tasks
- **Three or more axes** — minimum DOF for KTU/ISO classification
- **Automatically controlled** — operates without continuous human intervention

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Human Arm vs. The Robot Arm**
> Think of a human arm: your **shoulder** rotates and lifts (like a robot's *base joint*), your **elbow** bends (like a robot's *elbow joint*), and your **wrist** twists and turns (like a robot's *end-effector wrist*). The way these joints are arranged determines how nimbly you can reach into a toolbox versus paint a ceiling.
>
> Similarly, a robot's **mechanical configuration** (the geometric arrangement of its links and joints) determines its **workspace shape** (the volume of space it can reach) and its **best-suited application**.

**Intuitive takeaway:** Just as a crane, a forklift, and a human hand each excel at different jobs due to their *structure*, different robot *types* are engineered for specific industrial tasks (assembly, painting, welding, palletizing).

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Payload Capacity** — measured in **kg** or **N**
- **Reach / Workspace Radius** — measured in **mm** or **m**
- **Repeatability** — measured in **±mm** (typically ±0.02 mm to ±0.5 mm for industrial robots)
- **Number of Axes (DOF)** — integer count, **minimum 3** for ISO classification
- **Speed** — measured in **m/s** (linear) or **rad/s** (rotational)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Repeatability vs. Accuracy — A KTU Favorite Distinction:**
> - **Repeatability:** How closely the robot returns to a *previously taught* position (the measure KTU examiners love to test).
> - **Accuracy:** How closely the robot reaches a *commanded* position in absolute terms.
> Industrial robots are typically **highly repeatable but moderately accurate**.

---

### 1.4 Why "Types" Matter in KTU 2024 Examinations

> [!NOTE]
> The classification of industrial robots is a **high-frequency, high-yield topic** in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination). Questions commonly appear as:
> - 3-mark direct definitions or comparison table fill-ins
> - 7-mark descriptive answers with neat diagrams
> - 14-mark full questions with sub-parts on configuration, DOF, workspace, and applications
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Criteria for Industrial Robots

Industrial robots are classified along **four orthogonal axes** by the Robotics Industry Association (RIA) and ISO 8373:

1. **Geometric Configuration (Mechanical Structure)** ← *Most commonly asked in KTU*
2. **Method of Control** (Servo vs. Non-servo)
3. **Type of Drive System** (Hydraulic, Pneumatic, Electric)
4. **Application Domain** (Assembly, Welding, Painting, Material Handling)

---

### 2.2 Classification by Mechanical Configuration (Detailed Breakdown)

#### **Type 1: Cartesian / Rectangular / Gantry Robot (3 Linear Axes: L-L-L)**

- **Structure:** Three mutually perpendicular **prismatic (linear) joints** (L).
- **Joint sequence:** *Linear (X) → Linear (Y) → Linear (Z)*
- **Workspace shape:** Rectangular cuboid (a box).
- **Mechanical analogy:** A gantry crane or a 3D printer frame.
- **Advantages:** Simple kinematics, high accuracy, large workspace, easy to visualize off-line programming.
- **Disadvantages:** Requires large floor space envelope, slow, limited orientation capability of end-effector.
- **Typical applications:** Pick-and-place, CNC loading, assembly of flat components, adhesive dispensing, 3D printing, pick-and-place on conveyor lines.
- **DOF:** 3 (basic). Often upgraded to 4–5 by adding a wrist.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fun fact:** The largest Cartesian gantry robots in semiconductor fabs (e.g., ASML lithography handlers) can span over 10 meters and handle 500 kg payloads.

---

#### **Type 2: Cylindrical Coordinate Robot (R-L-L or L-L-R variations)**

- **Structure:** One **revolute (R) joint** at the base for rotation about a vertical axis, followed by one **prismatic (L) joint** for vertical motion, and one **prismatic (L) joint** for radial reach.
- **Joint sequence:** *Revolute (θ) → Linear (r) → Linear (z)*
- **Workspace shape:** A portion of a **cylinder** (cylindrical envelope).
- **Mechanical analogy:** A pillar crane or a turret lathe.
- **Advantages:** Reaches around obstacles radially, larger vertical reach than Cartesian, simple inverse kinematics.
- **Disadvantages:** Cannot reach overhead obstructions well, linear rails require maintenance, lower accuracy at extended reach.
- **Typical applications:** Machine tending (lathe/CNC), coating operations, simple assembly.

---

#### **Type 3: Spherical (Polar) Coordinate Robot (R-R-L)**

- **Structure:** One revolute joint at the base, one revolute joint for shoulder pitch, and one **prismatic (linear) joint** for radial extension.
- **Joint sequence:** *Revolute (θ) → Revolute (φ) → Linear (r)*
- **Workspace shape:** A portion of a **sphere** (spherical envelope).
- **Mechanical analogy:** A tank turret.
- **Advantages:** Larger workspace than cylindrical, good reach for heavy payloads.
- **Disadvantages:** Limited to a spherical envelope, complex kinematics, lower accuracy.
- **Typical applications:** Heavy material handling, forging, die casting, gas metal arc welding, nuclear hot-cell handling.

---

#### **Type 4: SCARA — Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm (R-R-L)**

- **Structure:** Two **revolute joints** for horizontal motion plus a **prismatic (linear) joint** for vertical motion. All horizontal joints are **parallel**, giving selective compliance.
- **Joint sequence:** *Revolute → Revolute → Linear (vertical)*
- **Workspace shape:** A horizontal **cylindrical disc** with a thin vertical band.
- **Mechanical analogy:** A horizontal arm that "tends the garden" — rigid vertically, compliant horizontally.
- **Advantages:** Extremely fast (cycle times < 0.3 s), excellent for vertical insertion tasks, very repeatable (±0.01 mm), compact footprint.
- **Disadvantages:** Limited to planar work surfaces, restricted vertical reach.
- **Typical applications:** PCB component insertion, screw-driving, small parts assembly, packaging, pick-and-place in electronics manufacturing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mantra:** *"SCARA is rigid in Z, compliant in X-Y"* — write this verbatim in the exam; it is **literally worth marks** as the examiner looks for this exact phrase.

---

#### **Type 5: Articulated (Revolute / Jointed-Arm) Robot (R-R-R-R-R-R)**

- **Structure:** All **revolute joints** — typically 6 (base rotation, shoulder, elbow, plus a 3-axis spherical wrist).
- **Joint sequence:** *Revolute → Revolute → Revolute → Revolute (wrist roll) → Revolute (wrist pitch) → Revolute (wrist yaw)*
- **Workspace shape:** A **complex, near-spherical** volume with internal hollows (singularities create dead zones).
- **Mechanical analogy:** A human arm.
- **Advantages:** Maximum dexterity, largest usable workspace, handles complex 3D paths (welding seams, painting curves), mature control algorithms.
- **Disadvantages:** Most complex kinematics (closed-form inverse kinematics can have up to 8 solutions), expensive, requires accurate calibration.
- **Typical applications:** Arc welding, spot welding, painting, deburring, polishing, waterjet cutting, complex assembly.
- **DOF:** Typically **6** (3 for position + 3 for orientation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry benchmark:** The **FANUC R-2000iC** and **KUKA KR 1000 Titan** (1300 kg payload) are flagship articulated robots.

---

#### **Type 6: Parallel / Delta Robot**

- **Structure:** End-effector connected to the base by **three or more parallel kinematic chains** (typically 3 or 6 DOF). Arms are arranged as parallelograms, keeping the end-effector platform always horizontal.
- **Workspace shape:** A **shallow dome or cylindrical** workspace directly under the base.
- **Mechanical analogy:** A flying insect or a tripod-mounted platform.
- **Advantages:** Extremely high speed (pick rates > 300 picks/min for small parts), high stiffness, low inertia, excellent for lightweight pick-and-place.
- **Disadvantages:** Limited workspace volume, complex forward kinematics, low payload (typically < 10 kg).
- **Typical applications:** High-speed packaging, food sorting, pharmaceutical pick-and-place, 3D printing (delta printers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Delta vs. SCARA — KTU Frequently Confused Pair:**
> - **SCARA** uses *serial* kinematic chains with R-R-L.
> - **Delta** uses *parallel* kinematic chains, with all three arms moving together.
> - Both are fast pick-and-place robots, but **Delta is faster and lighter**; **SCARA handles heavier parts and vertical forces**.

---

### 2.3 Classification by Control Method

| **Class** | **Description** | **Typical Use** |
|---|---|---|
| **Non-servo (Open-loop / "Bang-Bang")** | Pre-set stops; no feedback. Either pneumatic or electric with mechanical limits. | Simple pick-and-place |
| **Servo-controlled (Closed-loop)** | Continuous path or point-to-point control with position/velocity feedback. | Welding, painting, complex assembly |
| **Point-to-Point (PTP)** | Moves between taught points without controlling the path between them. | Spot welding, palletizing |
| **Continuous Path (CP)** | Records and replays the entire trajectory. | Spray painting, polishing, gluing |
| **Controlled Path (CP)** | Programmed with explicit path commands (linear/circular interpolation). | Welding seams, machining |

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all KTU-grade high-yield knowledge** for the topic. Memorize it as a single revision unit.

| **Robot Type** | **Joints** | **Coordinate System** | **Workspace Shape** | **Typical DOF** | **Strongest Application** | **Accuracy / Repeatability Class** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cartesian / Gantry** | L–L–L | Cartesian (X, Y, Z) | Rectangular cuboid | 3 (+ wrist) | Pick-and-place, 3D printing | High |
| **Cylindrical** | R–L–L | Cylindrical (r, θ, z) | Cylindrical sector | 3 (+ wrist) | Machine tending, coating | Medium |
| **Spherical / Polar** | R–R–L | Spherical (r, θ, φ) | Spherical sector | 3 (+ wrist) | Heavy forging, die casting | Medium |
| **SCARA** | R–R–L | Cylindrical (r, θ, z) | Thin disc / cylinder | 3–4 | PCB assembly, vertical insertion | Very high (±0.01 mm) |
| **Articulated (Revolute)** | R–R–R–R–R–R | Joint / Cartesian | Irregular sphere | 6 | Welding, painting, complex paths | High |
| **Parallel / Delta** | 3–6 parallel chains | Cartesian (constrained) | Shallow dome | 3–6 | High-speed packaging, sorting | Very high speed |

> **Where:**
> - L = Linear (Prismatic) joint
> - R = Revolute (Rotational) joint
> - DOF = Degrees of Freedom
> - All numerical values for accuracy/repeatability are qualitative; consult manufacturer datasheets for exact specifications.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive sector:** Articulated robots dominate body-in-white welding and paint shops (e.g., Tesla Model 3 body shop uses ~600 articulated robots).
- **Electronics manufacturing:** SCARA robots dominate PCB assembly lines (component placement).
- **Food & pharma:** Delta robots dominate high-speed packaging (up to 200 picks/min).
- **Semiconductor fabrication:** Cartesian robots dominate wafer handling (cleanroom compatibility + high accuracy).
- **Heavy industry:** Cylindrical and spherical robots dominate forging and foundry work (high payload capability).
- **Warehouse automation:** Articulated and Cartesian robots are used in ASRS (Automated Storage and Retrieval Systems).

> [!NOTE]
> A student's ability to **match robot type to application** is the single highest-scoring skill in this topic. If you can answer *"Which robot would you recommend for a chocolate packaging line?"* with reasoned justification, you will score full marks on KTU application-based sub-parts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The "types of industrial robots" topic is **descriptive rather than formula-heavy**, so this section presents a **structured analytical matrix** rather than algebraic derivations. For each robot type, we walk through the same four analytical lenses (Joints, DOF, Workspace, Forward Kinematics) so the student develops a uniform mental model.

---

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Standardized Decomposition of Each Robot Type

The KTU 2024 examiner expects you to analyze any robot type using the following **four-step framework**. Apply it consistently.

---

#### **Step 1: Cartesian Robot (L-L-L)**

**Joint identification:**

$$J_1 = \text{Prismatic along } X \text{ axis}, \quad J_2 = \text{Prismatic along } Y \text{ axis}, \quad J_3 = \text{Prismatic along } Z \text{ axis}$$

**End-effector position (forward kinematics):**

$$P_{ee} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} d_1 \\ d_2 \\ d_3 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $d_1, d_2, d_3$ are the prismatic joint displacements.

**Workspace volume:**

$$V_{workspace} = (X_{max} - X_{min}) \cdot (Y_{max} - Y_{min}) \cdot (Z_{max} - Z_{min})$$

**Reach envelope equation:** The end-effector can reach any point $(x, y, z)$ such that:

$$X_{min} \le x \le X_{max}, \quad Y_{min} \le y \le Y_{max}, \quad Z_{min} \le z \le Z_{max}$$

**Why this works (logic):** Each axis is decoupled from the others, so the reachable set is a simple Cartesian product of independent intervals.

---

#### **Step 2: Cylindrical Robot (R-L-L)**

**Joint identification:**

$$J_1 = \text{Revolute about vertical axis}, \quad J_2 = \text{Prismatic (radial reach)}, \quad J_3 = \text{Prismatic (vertical lift)}$$

**End-effector position (forward kinematics):**

$$P_{ee} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} r \cos\theta \\ r \sin\theta \\ z \end{bmatrix}$$

**Workspace constraints:**

$$0 \le r \le r_{max}, \quad 0 \le \theta \le 2\pi, \quad Z_{min} \le z \le Z_{max}$$

**Workspace volume:**

$$V_{workspace} = \pi r_{max}^2 \cdot (Z_{max} - Z_{min})$$

**Why this works (logic):** The radial reach $r$ and the height $z$ are independent linear parameters, while the base rotation $\theta$ sweeps the entire horizontal plane.

---

#### **Step 3: Spherical (Polar) Robot (R-R-L)**

**Joint identification:**

$$J_1 = \text{Revolute (base, azimuth)}, \quad J_2 = \text{Revolute (shoulder, elevation)}, \quad J_3 = \text{Prismatic (radial reach)}$$

**End-effector position (forward kinematics):**

$$P_{ee} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} r \sin\phi \cos\theta \\ r \sin\phi \sin\theta \\ r \cos\phi \end{bmatrix}$$

**Workspace constraints:**

$$0 \le r \le r_{max}, \quad 0 \le \phi \le \phi_{max}, \quad 0 \le \theta \le 2\pi$$

**Workspace volume (partial sphere):**

$$V_{workspace} = \frac{2\pi r_{max}^3}{3} (1 - \cos\phi_{max})$$

**Why this works (logic):** The two revolute joints define a direction vector in 3D space; the prismatic joint sets the reach distance along that direction.

---

#### **Step 4: SCARA Robot (R-R-L)**

**Joint identification:**

$$J_1 = \text{Revolute (shoulder)}, \quad J_2 = \text{Revolute (elbow)}, \quad J_3 = \text{Prismatic (vertical)}$$

**End-effector position (forward kinematics):**

$$P_{ee} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \cos\theta_1 + a_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ a_1 \sin\theta_1 + a_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ -d_3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Workspace constraints (horizontal plane):**

$$(x^2 + y^2) \le (a_1 + a_2)^2, \quad (x^2 + y^2) \ge (a_1 - a_2)^2$$

$$Z_{min} \le z \le Z_{max}$$

**Workspace volume (annular disc + thin vertical band):**

$$V_{workspace} = \pi [(a_1 + a_2)^2 - (a_1 - a_2)^2] \cdot (Z_{max} - Z_{min})$$

**Why this works (logic):** The two revolute joints form a planar 2R arm; the prismatic joint adds vertical motion but does not affect the horizontal footprint.

---

#### **Step 5: Articulated Robot (R-R-R + Spherical Wrist)**

**Joint identification:**

- $J_1$: Base rotation (about $Z$)
- $J_2$: Shoulder pitch (about $Y$)
- $J_3$: Elbow pitch (about $Y$)
- $J_4, J_5, J_6$: Wrist roll, pitch, yaw

**End-effector position (forward kinematics via DH parameters):**

Using the **Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) convention**, the end-effector pose is:

$$T_{6}^{0}(\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3, \theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6) = A_1(\theta_1) \cdot A_2(\theta_2) \cdot A_3(\theta_3) \cdot A_4(\theta_4) \cdot A_5(\theta_5) \cdot A_6(\theta_6)$$

where each $A_i$ is a $4 \times 4$ homogeneous transformation matrix of the form:

$$A_i = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i \cos\alpha_i & \sin\theta_i \sin\alpha_i & a_i \cos\theta_i \\ \sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i \cos\alpha_i & -\cos\theta_i \sin\alpha_i & a_i \sin\theta_i \\ 0 & \sin\alpha_i & \cos\alpha_i & d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Workspace shape:** Approximately **spherical**, with **internal singularities** (regions the end-effector cannot reach due to alignment of joint axes — the *elbow singularity* and *wrist singularity*).

**Why this works (logic):** Serial chaining of 6 rotation matrices enables arbitrary 3D positioning **and** orientation of the end-effector, which is what defines a "6-DOF" articulated robot.

---

#### **Step 6: Parallel / Delta Robot**

**Joint identification:**

- 3 actuated revolute joints at the base (actuated arms).
- 3 parallelogram passive linkages connecting each arm to the moving platform.
- Optional 4th vertical prismatic axis for rotation (4-DOF delta).

**End-effector position (forward kinematics — closed-form exists only in special cases):**

$$P_{ee} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix} = f(\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3)$$

This is generally a **nonlinear system** solved iteratively.

**Inverse kinematics (analytical, simple):**

$$r_i^2 = (x - x_{base,i})^2 + (y - y_{base,i})^2 + z^2 \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, 3$$

where $r_i$ is the length of the $i$-th arm.

**Workspace shape:** Shallow **circular dome** directly below the base, typically 300–1500 mm in diameter and 200–500 mm deep.

**Why this works (logic):** Each leg's length $r_i$ is constrained by the actuated revolute joint angle. Solving the three constraint equations simultaneously gives the end-effector pose.

---

### 3.2 Master Comparison Matrix (Engineering Decision Aid)

| **Criterion** | **Cartesian** | **Cylindrical** | **Spherical** | **SCARA** | **Articulated** | **Delta** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DOF (base)** | 3 | 3 | 3 | 3 | 6 | 3–4 |
| **Reach (typical)** | 1–10 m | 1–3 m | 1–3 m | 0.3–1 m | 0.5–3 m | 0.3–1.5 m |
| **Payload (typical)** | Up to 1000 kg | Up to 100 kg | Up to 200 kg | 1–20 kg | Up to 1300 kg | 0.1–10 kg |
| **Speed** | Medium | Medium | Slow | Very fast | Medium-fast | Ultra-fast |
| **Accuracy** | High | Medium | Medium | Very high | High | High |
| **Cost (relative)** | Medium | Low–Medium | Medium | Medium | High | Medium–High |
| **Footprint** | Large | Medium | Medium | Small | Medium | Small |
| **Best for** | Heavy loads, clean rooms | Machine tending | Forging, casting | Assembly | Welding, painting | High-speed sorting |

---

### 3.3 Numerical Example — Forward Kinematics for a Cylindrical Robot

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **solved numerical** of the type KTU may ask in the 7-mark sub-part (a).

**Problem:** A cylindrical robot has $r_{max} = 1.5$ m, base rotation range $\theta \in [0°, 180°]$, and vertical lift range $z \in [0, 1.2]$ m. Calculate:
1. The maximum reachable point's coordinates.
2. The volume of the workspace.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the maximum reach configuration:**

The maximum reach occurs at $r = r_{max} = 1.5$ m, $\theta = 90°$ (mid-range), and $z = z_{max} = 1.2$ m.

**Step 2 — Compute the end-effector coordinates:**

$$x = r \cos\theta = 1.5 \times \cos(90°) = 1.5 \times 0 = 0 \text{ m}$$

$$y = r \sin\theta = 1.5 \times \sin(90°) = 1.5 \times 1 = 1.5 \text{ m}$$

$$z = 1.2 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the workspace volume:**

The workspace is a half-cylinder (since $\theta$ spans $180°$ rather than $360°$):

$$V_{workspace} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \pi r_{max}^2 \cdot z_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \pi \cdot (1.5)^2 \cdot 1.2$$

$$V_{workspace} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \pi \cdot 2.25 \cdot 1.2 = 1.35\pi \approx 4.24 \text{ m}^3$$

**Final Answer:**

$$P_{max} = (0 \text{ m}, 1.5 \text{ m}, 1.2 \text{ m}) \quad \text{with} \quad V_{workspace} \approx 4.24 \text{ m}^3$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following **Mermaid diagrams** use *block-level functional architecture* representations rather than physical drawings (per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol for industrial/mechanical topics). Each robot type is rendered as a *joint-link graph* — the standard robotics textbook schematic style.

---

### 4.1 Master Joint-Link Graph — All Six Robot Types

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Cartesian["Cartesian Robot L-L-L"]
        baseC[Base] --> jC1[J1: Linear X]
        jC1 --> jC2[J2: Linear Y]
        jC2 --> jC3[J3: Linear Z]
        jC3 --> eeC[End Effector]
    end

    subgraph Cylindrical["Cylindrical Robot R-L-L"]
        baseY[Base] --> jY1[J1: Revolute theta]
        jY1 --> jY2[J2: Prismatic r]
        jY2 --> jY3[J3: Prismatic z]
        jY3 --> eeY[End Effector]
    end

    subgraph Spherical["Spherical Robot R-R-L"]
        baseS[Base] --> jS1[J1: Revolute theta]
        jS1 --> jS2[J2: Revolute phi]
        jS2 --> jS3[J3: Prismatic r]
        jS3 --> eeS[End Effector]
    end

    subgraph SCARA["SCARA Robot R-R-L"]
        baseSC[Base] --> jSC1[J1: Revolute]
        jSC1 --> jSC2[J2: Revolute]
        jSC2 --> jSC3[J3: Prismatic Z]
        jSC3 --> eeSC[End Effector]
    end

    subgraph Articulated["Articulated Robot 6R"]
        baseA[Base] --> jA1[J1: Base Rotate]
        jA1 --> jA2[J2: Shoulder]
        jA2 --> jA3[J3: Elbow]
        jA3 --> jA4[J4: Wrist Roll]
        jA4 --> jA5[J5: Wrist Pitch]
        jA5 --> jA6[J6: Wrist Yaw]
        jA6 --> eeA[End Effector]
    end

    subgraph Delta["Delta Parallel Robot"]
        baseD[Fixed Base] --> armD1[Arm 1: R + Parallelogram]
        baseD --> armD2[Arm 2: R + Parallelogram]
        baseD --> armD3[Arm 3: R + Parallelogram]
        armD1 --> platD[Moving Platform EE]
        armD2 --> platD
        armD3 --> platD
    end

    style Cartesian fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style Cylindrical fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style Spherical fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style SCARA fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style Articulated fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f
    style Delta fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff6f00
```

---

### 4.2 Workspace Shape Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Shapes["Workspace Envelope Shapes"]
        sCart[Rectangular Cuboid] --> sCyl[Cylindrical Sector]
        sCyl --> sSph[Spherical Sector]
        sSph --> sSCARA[Thin Annular Disc]
        sSCARA --> sArt[Irregular Sphere with Singularities]
        sArt --> sDelta[Shallow Dome]
    end

    style sCart fill:#e1f5ff
    style sCyl fill:#fff3e0
    style sSph fill:#f3e5f5
    style sSCARA fill:#e8f5e9
    style sArt fill:#fce4ec
    style sDelta fill:#fff8e1
```

---

### 4.3 Application-to-Robot-Type Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Choose Robot Type] --> q1{Task Location?}
    q1 -->|3D large volume| q2{Requires Orientation?}
    q1 -->|Flat surface 2.5D| SCARA[SCARA Robot]
    q1 -->|Conveyor pick high speed| Delta[Delta Robot]
    q1 -->|Heavy payload machine tending| CylSp[Cylindrical or Spherical]

    q2 -->|Yes, complex 3D path| Art[Articulated 6-Axis]
    q2 -->|No, just XYZ| Cart[Cartesian Gantry]

    SCARA --> check1[Use for assembly, PCB]
    Delta --> check2[Use for packaging, sorting]
    CylSp --> check3[Use for forging, casting]
    Art --> check4[Use for welding, painting]
    Cart --> check5[Use for CNC loading, 3D printing]

    style start fill:#fff9c4
    style Art fill:#fce4ec
    style Cart fill:#e1f5ff
    style SCARA fill:#e8f5e9
    style Delta fill:#fff8e1
    style CylSp fill:#fff3e0
```

---

### 4.4 Functional Architecture of a Typical Industrial Robot System

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Input["Input Layer"]
        teach[Teach Pendant / HMI]
        prog[Offline Program CAD/CAM]
    end

    subgraph Control["Control Layer"]
        ctrl[Robot Controller CPU]
        interp[Trajectory Interpolator]
        kin[Forward and Inverse Kinematics Module]
    end

    subgraph Drive["Drive Layer"]
        servo[Servo Drive]
        amp[Power Amplifier]
    end

    subgraph Mechanical["Mechanical Layer"]
        motor[Servo Motors]
        gear[Gear Reducers]
        links[Links and Joints]
        ee[End Effector Tool]
    end

    subgraph Feedback["Feedback Layer"]
        enc[Encoders on each joint]
        force[Force Torque Sensor]
        vision[Vision System]
    end

    teach --> ctrl
    prog --> ctrl
    ctrl --> interp
    interp --> kin
    kin --> servo
    servo --> amp
    amp --> motor
    motor --> gear
    gear --> links
    links --> ee

    enc --> ctrl
    force --> ctrl
    vision --> ctrl

    style Control fill:#e3f2fd
    style Mechanical fill:#fff3e0
    style Feedback fill:#f3e5f5
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are constructed strictly per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE** (End Semester Examination) regulations: 3-mark short answers and 14-mark long answers with internal choice and 7+7 sub-part splits.

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Set B]**
**Define an industrial robot as per ISO 8373. List any four characteristics that distinguish it from a fixed automation system.**

**Model Answer:**

> An **industrial robot** is an *automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes*, which can be fixed in place or mobile, used in industrial automation applications (ISO 8373:2021).
>
> **Four distinguishing characteristics:**
> 1. **Reprogrammability** — task can be changed by software alone, no hardware rebuild.
> 2. **Multipurpose capability** — adaptable to diverse tasks (welding, painting, assembly).
> 3. **Three or more programmable axes (DOF)** — minimum threshold for classification.
> 4. **Automatic / autonomous control** — operates without continuous human intervention.
>
> Fixed automation, in contrast, is **hard-wired for a single task** and cannot be economically reprogrammed.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by "Selective Compliance" in a SCARA robot? Why is this property useful for assembly tasks?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Selective compliance** in a SCARA robot refers to the property of being **stiff in the vertical (Z) direction but compliant (flexible) in the horizontal (X-Y) plane**.
>
> This is achieved by the parallel arrangement of the two revolute joints' axes, which allows the arm to absorb small horizontal misalignments during assembly.
>
> **Why useful for assembly:**
> 1. During **vertical insertion** of pegs into holes (e.g., PCB component placement), the compliant horizontal arm tolerates minor positional errors.
> 2. This reduces jamming and part damage.
> 3. Eliminates the need for ultra-precise fixturing, lowering tooling costs.
> 4. Enables high-speed assembly cycles (often < 0.5 s per part).
>
> Hence SCARA dominates **PCB assembly, screw-driving, and small-parts insertion**.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Reminder:** Part B questions offer an **internal choice** between two 14-mark alternatives. You must attempt **only one** of the two options.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

#### **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify industrial robots based on their **mechanical configuration**. With neat diagrams, explain the **Cartesian, Cylindrical, and Spherical** coordinate robots, clearly stating their joint sequences, workspace shapes, and two industrial applications each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a labeled diagram, describe the **SCARA robot** in detail. Explain its kinematic structure, the meaning of "selective compliance," and justify why SCARA is preferred for **printed circuit board (PCB) assembly** over Cartesian and articulated robots.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Classification of industrial robots by mechanical configuration:**

| **Configuration** | **Joints** | **Workspace** |
|---|---|---|
| Cartesian | L-L-L | Rectangular cuboid |
| Cylindrical | R-L-L | Cylindrical sector |
| Spherical (Polar) | R-R-L | Spherical sector |
| SCARA | R-R-L (horizontal) | Thin disc |
| Articulated | R-R-R-R-R-R | Complex near-spherical |
| Parallel (Delta) | 3 parallel R arms | Shallow dome |

**1. Cartesian Robot (L-L-L):**

- **Structure:** Three prismatic (linear) joints arranged mutually perpendicular along X, Y, and Z axes.
- **Forward Kinematics:** $P_{ee} = [d_1, d_2, d_3]^T$ where $d_i$ are joint displacements.
- **Workspace:** A rectangular cuboid defined by $X_{min} \le x \le X_{max}$, $Y_{min} \le y \le Y_{max}$, $Z_{min} \le z \le Z_{max}$.
- **Applications:** (i) CNC machine loading/unloading, (ii) 3D printing / additive manufacturing, (iii) Pick-and-place on conveyors, (iv) Adhesive dispensing.
- **Advantages:** Simple, high accuracy, easy offline programming.

**2. Cylindrical Robot (R-L-L):**

- **Structure:** One revolute joint at the base (rotation about vertical axis), one prismatic joint for vertical height, and one prismatic joint for radial reach.
- **Forward Kinematics:** $P_{ee} = [r \cos\theta, r \sin\theta, z]^T$.
- **Workspace:** A cylindrical sector of volume $V = \pi r_{max}^2 \cdot z_{max}$.
- **Applications:** (i) Machine tending (lathes, mills), (ii) Spray coating, (iii) Simple assembly, (iv) Foundry core setting.
- **Advantages:** Larger vertical reach than Cartesian; good for radial sweeping tasks.

**3. Spherical (Polar) Robot (R-R-L):**

- **Structure:** Two revolute joints (base rotation + shoulder pitch) plus one prismatic joint for radial reach.
- **Forward Kinematics:** $P_{ee} = [r \sin\phi \cos\theta, r \sin\phi \sin\theta, r \cos\phi]^T$.
- **Workspace:** A partial spherical envelope.
- **Applications:** (i) Heavy forging manipulation, (ii) Die casting extraction, (iii) Gas metal arc welding, (iv) Nuclear hot-cell sample handling.
- **Advantages:** High payload, large reachable volume; good for working around central obstructions.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**SCARA Robot — Kinematic Structure:**

SCARA stands for **Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm**. It has:
- **J1:** Revolute joint at the base (shoulder rotation about vertical axis).
- **J2:** Revolute joint at the elbow (rotation about vertical axis parallel to J1).
- **J3:** Prismatic joint for vertical (Z-axis) motion.
- Optional **J4:** Revolute joint about the vertical axis for end-effector rotation.

**Forward Kinematics:**

$$P_{ee} = \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \cos\theta_1 + a_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ a_1 \sin\theta_1 + a_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ -d_3 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $a_1, a_2$ are link lengths and $d_3$ is the vertical descent.

**Selective Compliance — Meaning:**

- The two revolute joints have **parallel vertical axes**, so the arm is **rigid in the vertical (Z) direction** but **laterally compliant** in the horizontal (X-Y) plane.
- This means small horizontal forces cause the arm to deflect slightly, but vertical loads are borne stiffly.
- A small downward force on the end-effector does not cause the arm to sag.

**Why SCARA is preferred for PCB Assembly:**

1. **Vertical insertion compatibility:** Most PCB components (DIPs, headers, pin connectors) require **vertical insertion** — the rigid Z-axis of SCARA is ideal.
2. **Tolerance to horizontal misalignment:** The horizontal compliance allows small position errors in the X-Y plane without jamming or damaging delicate component leads.
3. **Speed:** SCARA achieves cycle times of **0.3–0.5 seconds per pick-and-place**, faster than Cartesian or articulated robots.
4. **Compact footprint:** Fits in the tight cell layout of a pick-and-place machine.
5. **High repeatability (±0.01 mm):** Meets the precision demands of surface-mount and through-hole component placement.

**Comparison with alternatives:**

- **Cartesian robot** for PCB assembly would be slower and require a larger footprint.
- **Articulated robot** is overkill — 6 DOF and complex control for a task that only needs 4 DOF at most. Higher cost.

Hence SCARA dominates PCB assembly in modern SMT (surface-mount technology) lines.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)**

#### **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Articulated (Revolute) Robot** in detail. With a kinematic diagram, describe its 6-DOF structure, state the role of the spherical wrist, and list four typical industrial applications.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Parallel (Delta) robot**. Explain its kinematic principle, advantages over serial robots for high-speed pick-and-place, and provide two real-world application scenarios.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Articulated Robot (Revolute / Jointed-Arm):**

**Definition:** An articulated robot has **all revolute (rotational) joints** — typically **6 axes** for full position-and-orientation dexterity.

**6-DOF Kinematic Structure:**

| **Axis** | **Function** | **Typical Range** |
|---|---|---|
| **J1** | Base rotation (about vertical Z) | ±180° to ±360° |
| **J2** | Shoulder pitch (about horizontal Y) | ±90° to ±150° |
| **J3** | Elbow pitch (about horizontal Y) | ±150° to ±180° |
| **J4** | Wrist roll (about forearm axis) | ±360° |
| **J5** | Wrist pitch (about perpendicular) | ±120° |
| **J6** | Wrist yaw (about tool axis) | ±360° |

**The Spherical Wrist (J4, J5, J6):**

- The last three joints form a **spherical wrist** — all three axes intersect at a single point called the **wrist center (WC)**.
- This geometric property **decouples position and orientation** of the end-effector:
  - The first three joints (**J1, J2, J3**) position the wrist center in 3D space.
  - The last three joints (**J4, J5, J6**) orient the tool about the wrist center.
- This decoupling simplifies **inverse kinematics** — solve position first, then orientation.

**Forward Kinematics (DH Convention):**

$$T_{6}^{0} = A_1(\theta_1) \cdot A_2(\theta_2) \cdot A_3(\theta_3) \cdot A_4(\theta_4) \cdot A_5(\theta_5) \cdot A_6(\theta_6)$$

**Workspace:** Approximately spherical in shape, with internal **singularities** (configurations where the robot loses one or more DOF — e.g., when J5 = 0°).

**Four Typical Applications:**

1. **Arc welding** of car bodies — follows complex 3D weld seams.
2. **Spray painting** — continuous path control along curved surfaces.
3. **Spot welding** — point-to-point motion with high precision.
4. **Material handling / palletizing** — picks boxes from a conveyor and stacks them on a pallet.
5. (Bonus) **Deburring, polishing, waterjet cutting.**

**Advantages:** Maximum dexterity, largest usable workspace, mature control algorithms, suited for complex 3D paths.

**Disadvantages:** Most complex kinematics, expensive, requires accurate calibration, internal singularities.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Parallel (Delta) Robot:**

**Definition:** A parallel robot has its **end-effector (moving platform) connected to a fixed base by three or more parallel kinematic chains** working simultaneously. The most common variant is the **3-DOF Delta robot**, designed by Reymond Clavel in 1985.

**Kinematic Structure:**

- **Three actuated revolute joints** at the base (one for each arm).
- Each arm consists of an **upper arm** (rotated by the actuator) connected via a **forearm** (parallelogram linkage) to the moving platform.
- The parallelogram structure keeps the moving platform **always horizontal** (parallel to the base).
- Optional **4th vertical prismatic axis** through the center for additional DOF (rotation about vertical axis).

**Kinematic Principle:**

- All three arms move in concert (parallel action).
- Forward kinematics: solve the position of the moving platform from the three joint angles — generally **nonlinear, iterative**.
- Inverse kinematics: from desired platform position, compute the three joint angles — **analytical and unique** (relatively simple):

$$r_i^2 = (x - x_{base,i})^2 + (y - y_{base,i})^2 + z^2 \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, 3$$

This simplicity of inverse kinematics is one of the Delta's key strengths.

**Advantages over Serial Robots for High-Speed Pick-and-Place:**

1. **Low moving mass:** The motors stay on the **fixed base** — only the lightweight parallelograms and platform move. This drastically reduces inertia and enables accelerations of up to **30 g**.
2. **High stiffness:** The closed kinematic structure is inherently stiffer than an open-chain arm of equivalent reach.
3. **High speed:** Pick rates of **> 300 picks/min** for small parts (1–10 g).
4. **High accuracy:** Repeatability of ±0.05 mm for small workspaces.
5. **Simple control:** Inverse kinematics is straightforward.

**Two Real-World Application Scenarios:**

1. **Food packaging line:** Delta robots pick chocolates, biscuits, or vegetables from a conveyor and place them in trays at high speed (> 200 picks/min), with wash-down-compatible stainless steel construction.
2. **Pharmaceutical sorting:** Delta robots pick blister packs, vials, or syringes from a moving belt and place them into cartons, often integrated with vision systems for orientation.
3. (Bonus) **3D printing (delta printers):** Used in consumer-grade and industrial FFF 3D printers for fast vertical-axis-free printing of small to medium parts.
4. (Bonus) **Electronics pick-and-place:** Surface-mount device (SMD) component feeders in electronics manufacturing.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU 2024 Examinations for this topic:**
>
> 1. **Conflating SCARA's compliance direction.** Many students wrongly say "SCARA is compliant vertically and stiff horizontally." The *correct* statement is **stiff in Z, compliant in X-Y**. This single error can cost **2–3 marks**.
>
> 2. **Confusing DOF with axes.** The number of **axes** equals the number of **joints** equals the number of **DOF** *only if every joint contributes a DOF*. Some textbooks list a 5-axis articulated robot — this has 5 DOF, not 6.
>
> 3. **Mixing up the joint sequence of cylindrical and spherical robots.** A cylindrical robot is **R-L-L**; a spherical robot is **R-R-L**. Mixing these up loses **2 marks** on the joint identification sub-part.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the spherical wrist decoupling property** in articulated robots. Examiners specifically test this — always mention the **wrist center** and the **decoupling of position and orientation**.
>
> 5. **Drawing a 2R planar arm and calling it a SCARA.** A 2R arm has no vertical motion. A proper SCARA must show **R-R-L** (the third L being the vertical prismatic).
>
> 6. **Vague workspace descriptions.** Don't just say "the workspace is a sphere" — specify the **partial / sector / annulus** nature and write the **equation** (volume or boundary).
>
> 7. **Ignoring applications.** A robot type described without its **two real-world applications** is incomplete. KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this omission.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize This Block Before the Exam**

- **Industrial robot definition:** Automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose, **3+ axes** (ISO 8373).
- **6 Main Types by Configuration:**
  1. **Cartesian (L-L-L)** → rectangular workspace → pick-and-place, 3D printing
  2. **Cylindrical (R-L-L)** → cylindrical sector → machine tending
  3. **Spherical / Polar (R-R-L)** → spherical sector → forging, casting
  4. **SCARA (R-R-L)** → thin disc, **stiff in Z, compliant in X-Y** → PCB assembly
  5. **Articulated (R-R-R + spherical wrist)** → near-spherical → welding, painting
  6. **Delta (parallel)** → shallow dome → high-speed packaging
- **Classification criteria:** (1) Configuration, (2) Control method (servo vs. non-servo; PTP vs. CP), (3) Drive (hydraulic, pneumatic, electric), (4) Application.
- **Spherical wrist property:** All three wrist axes intersect at one point → **decouples position and orientation**.
- **Repeatability vs. Accuracy:** Repeatability = return to *taught* point; Accuracy = reach *commanded* point. Robots are **highly repeatable, moderately accurate**.
- **SCARA = "Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm"** — must mention in every SCARA answer.
- **Delta robot inventor:** Reymond Clavel, 1985, EPFL.
- **High-speed pick-and-place leaders:** Delta > SCARA > Cartesian (in terms of cycle time).
- **Maximum payload (industrial):** Articulated robot holds the record (KUKA KR 1000 Titan, 1300 kg).
- **Most common DOF in industry:** 6-DOF articulated robots dominate by unit count.
- **Workspace equations to remember:**
  - Cartesian: $V = (X_{max}-X_{min})(Y_{max}-Y_{min})(Z_{max}-Z_{min})$
  - Cylindrical: $V = \pi r_{max}^2 \cdot z_{max}$
  - Spherical: $V = \frac{2\pi r_{max}^3}{3}(1 - \cos\phi_{max})$
  - SCARA: $V = \pi[(a_1+a_2)^2 - (a_1-a_2)^2] \cdot z_{max}$
- **High-yield one-liner for the exam:** *"SCARA — rigid in Z, compliant in X-Y; Delta — parallel, ultra-fast, low payload; Articulated — 6-DOF, full dexterity, spherical wrist decouples position and orientation."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications in Manufacturing, Assembly, and Inspection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT865 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Applications in Manufacturing, Assembly, and Inspection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for the course *PEITT865 – Robotics and Automation*, **Applications in Manufacturing, Assembly, and Inspection** refers to the systematic deployment of industrial robotic manipulators within the three foundational pillars of modern discrete-part production systems. According to the International Federation of Robotics (IFR) classification and the ISO 8373:2021 standard, these applications constitute the dominant share of industrial robot installations worldwide and form the cornerstone of **Computer-Integrated Manufacturing (CIM)** and **Industry 4.0** paradigms.

$$ \text{Industrial Robot Application Triad} = \{ f_{\text{Manufacturing}}(t),\ f_{\text{Assembly}}(t),\ f_{\text{Inspection}}(t) \} $$

where each function is a discrete, repeatable, programmable operation executed by a reprogrammable multi-axis manipulator operating within a structured work envelope.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight – Module 3 Anchor Definition**
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly categorises these three domains because they represent *value-adding* operations where robots have demonstrated measurable superiority over humans in **3D tasks (Dull, Dirty, Dangerous)** and in tasks demanding **sub-millimetre repeatability** (typically $\pm 0.02\ \text{mm}$ to $\pm 0.1\ \text{mm}$).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Specialised Factory Worker" Analogy

Imagine a factory floor as a busy **kitchen** in a five-star restaurant. Three types of workers run the kitchen:

1. **The "Heavy Cook" (Manufacturing Application)** – Handles hot woks, sharp knives, and toxic fumes. The cook doesn't *create* the recipe but executes the same stirring and frying motion 10,000 times a day without burning out. This is exactly what a robotic welder or paint sprayer does — it performs a hazardous, repetitive fabrication process with mechanical precision.

2. **The "Pastry Decorator" (Assembly Application)** – Places tiny sugar flowers on a cake, one petal at a time, with millimetre accuracy, never crushing the icing. This mirrors a robotic assembly arm inserting microchips or tightening screws on a printed circuit board.

3. **The "Food Critic" (Inspection Application)** – Stands back, observes the finished dish under bright lights, and decides whether it meets the Michelin standard. A robotic vision inspection cell performs the same quality-verdict role using cameras, lasers, and AI inference.

In short: **Manufacturing = Build it. Assembly = Put it together. Inspection = Check it.**

### 1.3 Core Operational Metrics and Standard Benchmarks

Every KTU examination question on this topic hinges on a handful of *numerical constants* that define the boundary between feasible and infeasible robotic deployment. The following table consolidates the high-priority benchmarks:

| Operational Metric | Symbol | Typical Industrial Range | Physical Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Repeatability | $R$ | $\pm 0.02\ \text{mm}$ to $\pm 0.1\ \text{mm}$ | Radial scatter of end-effector upon repeated homing |
| Accuracy | $A$ | $\pm 0.1\ \text{mm}$ to $\pm 1.0\ \text{mm}$ | Deviation between commanded and actual pose |
| Payload Capacity | $P$ | $3\ \text{kg}$ to $1000+\ \text{kg}$ | Maximum end-effector load at rated acceleration |
| Degrees of Freedom | $n_{DOF}$ | $4$ to $7$ | Number of independent joint axes |
| Cycle Time | $T_c$ | $1\ \text{s}$ to $60\ \text{s}$ | Time to complete one full operational cycle |
| Working Envelope | $V$ | $0.5\ \text{m}^3$ to $50\ \text{m}^3$ | Reachable Cartesian volume |
| MTBF | $\bar{T}_{MTBF}$ | $20{,}000$ to $80{,}000\ \text{h}$ | Mean Time Between Failures |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard to Remember:** The KTU 2024 question bank frequently references **ISO 9283:1998** as the governing standard for *performance criteria of industrial robots* — specifically the definitions of *pose accuracy*, *pose repeatability*, *path accuracy*, and *path repeatability*. Memorise these four terms verbatim.

### 1.4 Why These Three Domains Dominate KTU Examinations

The KTU 2024 Scheme has consolidated these topics under Module 3 because they represent the **highest cognitive-level integration** of kinematics (Modules 1 & 2) with real-world industrial problems. The typical Part B (14-mark) question demands that the student:

1. Identify the **application class** from a case study,
2. Specify the **robot configuration** (Cartesian/SCARA/Articulated/Delta),
3. Compute the **cycle time, throughput, or utilisation**,
4. Justify the **end-effector and sensor selection**,
5. Discuss **economic and safety justification**.

The remaining sections of this note address each of these deliverables with the depth required for a full-marks ESE response.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Application Class 1 — Manufacturing Applications

Manufacturing applications are operations that **transform raw stock into finished or semi-finished geometry**. They are characterised by high energy input, contact with the workpiece, and exposure to thermal/chemical hazards.

#### 2.1.1 Robotic Welding

Welding is the single largest application of industrial robots globally, accounting for approximately **29% of all robot installations** (IFR World Robotics Report 2023).

**Operational Sub-classes:**
- **Spot Welding (Resistance Welding):** Two copper electrodes squeeze metal sheets and pass a high current (~10 kA) for ~0.2 s. Robots must deliver precise electrode force and tip alignment.
- **Arc Welding (MIG/MAG/TIG):** A continuous consumable or non-consumable electrode deposits filler metal under inert gas shielding. The robot must maintain a constant **torch angle** ($\alpha$) and **stick-out distance** ($d_s$).
- **Laser Welding:** Focused laser beam (typically 1–10 kW fibre laser) provides deep-penetration, low-distortion welds.

**Why a robot excels:** Weld seams demand a constant **travel speed** ($v_t$, typically $5$ to $150\ \text{cm/min}$) and a constant **torch-to-workpiece distance** (TWD, typically $10$ to $15\ \text{mm}$). A skilled human welder cannot hold these tolerances for a full 8-hour shift.

**Robot Configuration Used:** **Articulated 6-axis** (e.g., FANUC R-2000iC, ABB IRB 6700) — required to orient the torch at any angle and reach into car-body sub-assemblies.

#### 2.1.2 Robotic Painting and Coating

Painting involves the controlled atomisation of paint via **airless, electrostatic, or HVLP (High Volume Low Pressure)** spray guns.

**Critical Control Variables:**
- **Trigger distance** ($d_t$): Distance from nozzle to workpiece, usually $200$ to $300\ \text{mm}$.
- **Gun velocity** ($v_g$): $400$ to $1000\ \text{mm/s}$.
- **Trigger lag** ($\Delta t_{\text{on/off}}$): The over-spray at start/stop points — robots minimise this from ~150 mm (human) to ~25 mm.
- **Film build** ($\delta_f$): Wet film thickness, typically $20$ to $80\ \mu m$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Economic Justification (commonly asked in KTU Part B):** Robotic painting reduces paint wastage from ~40% (manual) to ~10% (robotic), giving a payback period of typically **2 to 4 years** for a painting cell costing ₹1.5–3 Crore.

#### 2.1.3 Robotic Machining (Drilling, Milling, Deburring, Grinding)

While not as common as welding/painting, robotic machining has grown with the advent of **high-payload (>50 kg) high-stiffness robots** that can replace CNC machines for large components (e.g., aerospace fuselage panels).

**Governing Equation for Robotic Drilling — the Cutting Force Model:**

$$ F_c = K_c \cdot A_c = K_c \cdot (f \cdot d) $$

where $F_c$ is the cutting force (N), $K_c$ is the specific cutting energy (N/mm²), $A_c$ is the uncut chip cross-section (mm²), $f$ is the feed per revolution (mm/rev), and $d$ is the drill diameter (mm).

The robot must counteract this force without exceeding its **allowable pose deviation** ($\Delta x_{\max}$), given its Cartesian stiffness $K_R$:

$$ \Delta x = \frac{F_c}{K_R} \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_{c,\max} = K_R \cdot \Delta x_{\max} $$

For a typical 6-axis articulated robot, $K_R$ varies from $0.5$ to $5\ \text{N/}\mu\text{m}$ depending on the joint configuration (Cartesian stiffness is *configuration-dependent*).

#### 2.1.4 Material Handling and Palletising

Material handling is the **largest application by unit count** — it includes:
- **Pick and Place:** Moving parts between conveyors, fixtures, and machines.
- **Machine Tending:** Loading/unloading CNC mills, presses, injection moulding machines.
- **Palletising:** Stacking cartons/bags on shipping pallets in a pre-defined pattern.

**Palletising Pattern Mathematics:**

The number of layers in a pallet stack is given by:

$$ n_L = \left\lfloor \frac{H_{\text{pallet}}}{H_{\text{case}}} \right\rfloor $$

where $H_{\text{pallet}}$ is the usable pallet stack height (typically $1.5$ to $1.8\ \text{m}$) and $H_{\text{case}}$ is the case height.

The cases per layer depends on the pattern. For a **column-stacked pattern** with case footprint $a \times b$ on a pallet of size $A \times B$:

$$ n_{\text{cases/layer}} = \left\lfloor \frac{A}{a} \right\rfloor \cdot \left\lfloor \frac{B}{b} \right\rfloor $$

### 2.2 Application Class 2 — Assembly Applications

Assembly is the process of **joining two or more sub-components** to form a higher-level assembly. Robotic assembly is the most challenging application class because it demands:
- **Sub-millimetre positional accuracy** (often $\le 0.05\ \text{mm}$),
- **Force feedback** to prevent part damage,
- **Compliance** to absorb positional errors.

#### 2.2.1 The Peg-in-Hole Problem

The canonical robotic assembly research problem. A cylindrical peg of diameter $d_p$ must be inserted into a hole of diameter $d_h$, with a **clearance**:

$$ c = d_h - d_p $$

The **clearance ratio** $c/d_p$ determines the strategy:
- $c/d_p \ge 0.02$: Easy insertion; pure position control suffices.
- $0.005 \le c/d_p < 0.02$: Remote Centre Compliance (RCC) device required.
- $c/d_p < 0.005$: Active force control with search strategies (spiral, Lissajous).

**Insertion Force during chamfer crossing:**

The axial force required to push the chamfered peg past the hole edge is:

$$ F_{\text{chamfer}} = \mu \cdot F_N + F_N \cdot \tan(\theta_c) $$

where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction ($\sim 0.1$ to $0.3$ for steel), $F_N$ is the normal engagement force, and $\theta_c$ is the chamfer angle (typically $15°$ to $45°$).

#### 2.2.2 SCARA Robots — The Assembly Standard

The **Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm (SCARA)** is the dominant configuration for assembly because it offers:
- High stiffness in the **vertical (Z) direction**,
- Compliance in the **horizontal (X-Y) plane**,
- Fast cycle times (typically $0.3$ to $0.5\ \text{s}$ for pick-and-place).

The **SCARA reach envelope** is a horizontal annulus from inner radius $r_{\min}$ to outer radius $r_{\max}$:

$$ r_{\min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert, \qquad r_{\max} = L_1 + L_2 $$

where $L_1$ and $L_2$ are the two link lengths.

#### 2.2.3 Electronic and PCB Assembly

Key operations include:
- **Pick-and-place of SMD (Surface Mount Devices):** Requires vision-guided positioning with $\le 25\ \mu m$ placement accuracy.
- **Soldering:** Wave soldering, reflow soldering, or selective robotic soldering with controlled tip temperature (~$350°\text{C}$ for lead-free SAC alloys).
- **Conformal coating:** Dispensing a thin polymer layer for environmental protection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry 4.0 Connection:** Modern PCB assembly uses **collaborative robots (cobots)** like the Universal Robots UR5e alongside human operators. Cobots are limited to $250\ \text{N}$ contact force per ISO/TS 15066.

### 2.3 Application Class 3 — Inspection Applications

Inspection is the **verification of manufactured quality** against a specification. Robotic inspection is the fastest-growing application segment due to advances in **machine vision, AI, and sensor fusion**.

#### 2.3.1 Machine Vision-Based Inspection

The standard inspection architecture consists of:
1. **Illumination** (LED ring, dome, backlight, structured light),
2. **Optics** (lens, aperture, focal length),
3. **Image sensor** (CCD or CMOS, typically 5–20 MP),
4. **Processing** (edge detection, blob analysis, template matching, deep learning),
5. **Decision output** (pass/fail, defect classification, dimensional readout).

**Key Performance Equation — Pixel Resolution:**

$$ R_{\text{pixel}} = \frac{\text{FOV}_x}{N_x} $$

where $\text{FOV}_x$ is the field of view in the X-direction and $N_x$ is the number of pixels along X. For inspecting a $0.1\ \text{mm}$ defect, you need $R_{\text{pixel}} \le 0.05\ \text{mm}$ (Nyquist sampling criterion).

#### 2.3.2 Robot-Mounted Coordinate Measurement

A robot can carry a **touch-trigger probe** or **laser scanner** to perform dimensional inspection. The measurement uncertainty is the **root-sum-square (RSS)** of multiple error sources:

$$ u_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{u_{\text{robot}}^2 + u_{\text{probe}}^2 + u_{\text{temp}}^2 + u_{\text{calib}}^2} $$

A typical **touch-trigger probe** has an accuracy of $\pm 2\ \mu m$ (e.g., Renishaw OMP60), and a **laser line scanner** can achieve $z$-axis accuracy of $\pm 10\ \mu m$ at 100 mm stand-off.

#### 2.3.3 Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)

Robots are deployed for **ultrasonic testing (UT)**, **eddy current testing**, and **radiographic testing** of:
- Welds in pressure vessels and pipelines,
- Aircraft fuselage and wing skins,
- Nuclear reactor components.

The robot must maintain a **constant normal probe-to-surface orientation** and a **constant scan speed** to ensure repeatability of the NDT signal.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Consolidated Reference

The following consolidated table is the **examiner's cheat sheet** for this topic. Every formula has appeared in previous KTU question papers or is directly derivable from them.

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Glossary | Domain of Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_c = t_{\text{travel}} + t_{\text{process}} + t_{\text{wait}}$ | $T_c$ cycle time; $t_{\text{travel}}$ motion time; $t_{\text{process}}$ process dwell; $t_{\text{wait}}$ interlock | All applications |
| 2 | $\eta = T_{\text{active}}/T_{\text{available}}$ | $\eta$ utilisation; $T_{\text{active}}$ active time | Cell efficiency |
| 3 | $\Phi = 3600 / T_c$ | $\Phi$ throughput (parts/h) | All applications |
| 4 | $T_{\text{travel}} = \sum_i (L_i / v_i)$ | $L_i$ path segment, $v_i$ joint velocity | Cycle time calc. |
| 5 | $F_c = K_c \cdot f \cdot d$ | Cutting force model | Machining |
| 6 | $\Delta x = F_c / K_R$ | Robot deflection | Machining |
| 7 | $n_L = \lfloor H_{\text{pallet}}/H_{\text{case}} \rfloor$ | Layer count | Palletising |
| 8 | $F_{\text{ins}} = \mu F_N + F_N \tan\theta_c$ | Chamfer crossing | Assembly |
| 9 | $R_{\text{pixel}} = \text{FOV}/N$ | Vision resolution | Inspection |
| 10 | $u_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{\sum u_i^2}$ | RSS uncertainty | Inspection |
| 11 | $c = d_h - d_p$ | Hole clearance | Assembly |
| 12 | $v_t = \pi d n$ | Welding travel speed | Welding |
| 13 | $P_{\text{disp}} = Q \cdot \Delta p$ | Pump power | Painting |
| 14 | $\text{ROI} = \frac{\Delta C_{\text{labour}} - C_{\text{robot}}}{C_{\text{robot}}}$ | Payback analysis | Economic case |
| 15 | $n_{\text{cycles/MTBF}} = \bar{T}_{MTBF} / T_c$ | Robot life | Reliability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Examination Tip:** KTU questions on this topic almost always combine a **process-specific formula** (e.g., welding travel speed) with a **robot-performance formula** (e.g., cycle time). The marks are split 50:50 between *process knowledge* and *robotics knowledge*. Do not answer a welding question as if it were a pure mechanical-engineering welding question — always bring the robot's kinematics, payload, and workspace into your answer.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The integration of robotics into these three application classes has reshaped global manufacturing economics:

- **Automotive sector** (BMW, Tesla, Maruti Suzuki): A single body shop has 400–800 robots performing ~95% of all welds, reducing cycle time per car from ~80 hours (1960s) to ~20 hours (2020s).
- **Electronics (Foxconn, Apple supply chain):** PCB assembly lines have >$50$ pick-and-place machines per line, placing >$200{,}000$ components per hour.
- **Aerospace (Airbus, Boeing):** Robotic drilling of the A350 fuselage reduced assembly time by ~50% versus manual drilling, with hole positional accuracy of $\pm 0.3\ \text{mm}$.
- **Pharmaceutical inspection:** Vision-based inspection at $120$ m/min line speed on tablet blister packaging lines, rejecting tablets with surface defects $<0.5\ \text{mm}$.

The next section develops the **derivation logic** that examiners expect to see when a numerical cycle-time or throughput problem appears in the KTU ESE paper.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Logic:** The KTU valuation key awards 60% of marks for *correct methodology*, 20% for *correct numerical substitution*, and 20% for *correct final answer with units*. The derivations below show the full methodology in textbook style.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cycle Time of a Point-to-Point Pick-and-Place Robot

**Problem Setup (Sample KTU-style):**
A SCARA robot with link lengths $L_1 = 300\ \text{mm}$ and $L_2 = 200\ \text{mm}$ must pick a component from station A and place it at station B. The straight-line distance between A and B is $D = 400\ \text{mm}$. The end-effector gripper close/open time is $t_{\text{grip}} = 0.15\ \text{s}$ each. The maximum joint speed is $v_{\max} = 2000\ \text{mm/s}$. The part settles at the placement station in $t_{\text{settle}} = 0.10\ \text{s}$. The robot waits for the conveyor index in $t_{\text{wait}} = 0.30\ \text{s}$. Find the cycle time $T_c$.

**Step 1 — Decompose the cycle into its sub-intervals.**

A pick-and-place cycle has the following sequential sub-intervals:

$$ T_c = t_{\text{travel},\ A\to B} + t_{\text{settle}} + t_{\text{grip, open}} + t_{\text{travel},\ B\to A} + t_{\text{grip, close}} + t_{\text{wait}} $$

**Step 2 — Compute the travel time from A to B (assuming trapezoidal velocity profile).**

For a straight-line move of distance $D = 400\ \text{mm}$ with maximum velocity $v_{\max} = 2000\ \text{mm/s}$, the move is too short to reach $v_{\max}$ if the acceleration time is significant. Assume acceleration $a = 5000\ \text{mm/s}^2$.

The distance required to reach $v_{\max}$ from rest is:

$$ d_{\text{accel}} = \frac{v_{\max}^2}{2a} = \frac{(2000)^2}{2 \times 5000} = \frac{4 \times 10^6}{10{,}000} = 400\ \text{mm} $$

This is exactly equal to $D$. So the robot **just reaches** $v_{\max}$ at the end of the move with **triangular velocity profile**.

$$ t_{\text{travel},\ A\to B} = 2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{D}{a}} = 2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{400}{5000}} = 2 \cdot \sqrt{0.08} $$

$$ = 2 \cdot 0.2828 = 0.5657\ \text{s} $$

**Step 3 — By symmetry, the return travel $B \to A$ has the same duration:**

$$ t_{\text{travel},\ B\to A} = 0.5657\ \text{s} $$

**Step 4 — Sum all sub-intervals:**

$$ T_c = 0.5657 + 0.10 + 0.15 + 0.5657 + 0.15 + 0.30 $$

$$ T_c = 1.8314\ \text{s} \approx 1.83\ \text{s} $$

**Step 5 — Compute the throughput in parts per hour:**

$$ \Phi = \frac{3600}{T_c} = \frac{3600}{1.83} \approx 1967\ \text{parts/hour} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The throughput is a function of the *total motion* in the cycle, *not* just the distance. Students who answer 2000/1.83 = 1092 or make unit mistakes lose 2 marks. Always carry units through the calculation.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Robot Workspace and Reachability Constraint

**Problem Setup:**
A 6-axis articulated robot has link lengths $a_1 = 500\ \text{mm}$, $a_2 = 800\ \text{mm}$, $a_3 = 600\ \text{mm}$, with the wrist considered as a point. The end-effector adds $d_4 = 100\ \text{mm}$ to the reach. Determine (a) the maximum reach, (b) the dead-zone radius, and (c) whether a part located at $r = 1200\ \text{mm}$ height and $\rho = 1100\ \text{mm}$ radial distance is reachable.

**Step 1 — Compute the maximum reach (fully extended).**

When all three links are collinear and aligned with the base axis, the end-effector reaches:

$$ R_{\max} = a_1 + a_2 + a_3 + d_4 = 500 + 800 + 600 + 100 = 2000\ \text{mm} $$

**Step 2 — Compute the dead-zone (unreachable sphere) radius.**

The dead zone is the sphere around the base that the robot cannot reach (because the wrist would have to pass through the shoulder/elbow joints).

$$ R_{\min} = \sqrt{a_2^2 + (a_3 + d_4)^2} - a_1 $$

$$ = \sqrt{800^2 + (600+100)^2} - 500 = \sqrt{640{,}000 + 490{,}000} - 500 $$

$$ = \sqrt{1{,}130{,}000} - 500 = 1063.0 - 500 = 563\ \text{mm} $$

**Step 3 — Compute the radial distance of the target.**

The target is at $\rho = 1100\ \text{mm}$ radial and $z = 1200\ \text{mm}$ height (assuming cylindrical coordinates around the base). For reachability, we must have:

$$ \rho + z^2 \quad \text{within bounds}, \quad \text{or} \quad \sqrt{\rho^2 + z^2} \le R_{\max} \text{ and } \sqrt{\rho^2 + z^2} \ge R_{\min} $$

$$ \sqrt{1100^2 + 1200^2} = \sqrt{1{,}210{,}000 + 1{,}440{,}000} = \sqrt{2{,}650{,}000} \approx 1627.9\ \text{mm} $$

Since $563\ \text{mm} \le 1627.9\ \text{mm} \le 2000\ \text{mm}$, the target is **reachable** within the workspace.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Palletising Cycle Throughput

**Problem Setup:**
A palletising robot must stack cardboard cases of dimensions $400 \times 300 \times 250\ \text{mm}$ on a standard Euro pallet of size $1200 \times 800\ \text{mm}$. The pallet stack height is limited to $1500\ \text{mm}$. Each pick cycle takes $T_c = 6\ \text{s}$ (including motion, grasp, place, return). Determine: (a) the number of cases per layer, (b) the number of layers, (c) the number of cases per pallet, (d) the time to build one full pallet.

**Step 1 — Compute the cases per layer.**

Try orientation 1: case footprint $400 \times 300$ on pallet $1200 \times 800$.

$$ n_1 = \left\lfloor \frac{1200}{400} \right\rfloor \cdot \left\lfloor \frac{800}{300} \right\rfloor = 3 \times 2 = 6\ \text{cases/layer} $$

Try orientation 2: case footprint $300 \times 400$ on pallet $1200 \times 800$.

$$ n_2 = \left\lfloor \frac{1200}{300} \right\rfloor \cdot \left\lfloor \frac{800}{400} \right\rfloor = 4 \times 2 = 8\ \text{cases/layer} $$

Orientation 2 gives more cases per layer, so we choose orientation 2.

**Step 2 — Compute the number of layers.**

$$ n_L = \left\lfloor \frac{1500}{250} \right\rfloor = 6\ \text{layers} $$

**Step 3 — Compute the cases per pallet.**

$$ n_{\text{total}} = 8 \times 6 = 48\ \text{cases/pallet} $$

**Step 4 — Compute the time per pallet.**

$$ T_{\text{pallet}} = n_{\text{total}} \cdot T_c = 48 \times 6 = 288\ \text{s} = 4.8\ \text{minutes} $$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Insertion Force and Force-Controlled Assembly

**Problem Setup:**
A peg of diameter $d_p = 19.95\ \text{mm}$ must be inserted into a hole of diameter $d_h = 20.00\ \text{mm}$. The chamfer angle is $\theta_c = 30°$, the coefficient of friction is $\mu = 0.15$, and the applied normal force is $F_N = 10\ \text{N}$. Compute (a) the clearance, (b) the clearance ratio, (c) the chamfer-crossing force, and (d) recommend the assembly strategy.

**Step 1 — Compute the clearance.**

$$ c = d_h - d_p = 20.00 - 19.95 = 0.05\ \text{mm} $$

**Step 2 — Compute the clearance ratio.**

$$ \frac{c}{d_p} = \frac{0.05}{19.95} \approx 0.00251 = 0.251\% $$

**Step 3 — Compute the chamfer-crossing force.**

The chamfer component of the resistance is $F_N \tan\theta_c$ and the friction is $\mu F_N$:

$$ F_{\text{chamfer}} = \mu F_N + F_N \tan\theta_c = F_N (\mu + \tan\theta_c) $$

$$ = 10 \cdot (0.15 + \tan 30°) = 10 \cdot (0.15 + 0.5774) = 10 \cdot 0.7274 $$

$$ = 7.274\ \text{N} $$

**Step 4 — Recommend the strategy.**

Since $c/d_p = 0.00251 < 0.005$, the tolerance is **tight**. A **passive Remote Centre Compliance (RCC) device** alone is insufficient. The recommended strategy is:
1. Use a **force-controlled search pattern** (spiral or Lissajous) with a 6-axis force/torque sensor.
2. Apply a **2-step insertion**: search for the hole at low contact force ($<2\ \text{N}$), then ramp up to insertion force.
3. Provide **active compliance** via admittance control with admittance $A = 0.01\ \text{m/N}$.

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Robot Configuration

The following matrix maps **application class** to the **most suitable robot configuration** and the **dominant sensor requirement**. Memorise this for the KTU ESE.

| Application Sub-class | Recommended Robot | Drive Type | End-Effector | Primary Sensor | Critical Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Spot welding (automotive) | 6-axis articulated (heavy) | Servo-electric | Welding gun | None / current feedback | Reach into car body |
| Arc welding | 6-axis articulated | Servo-electric | MIG/MAG torch | Through-arc seam tracking | Torch orientation |
| Painting | 6-axis or 7-axis (hollow wrist) | Hydraulic or servo | Spray gun | None | Reach around object |
| PCB pick-and-place | SCARA or Delta | Servo-electric | Vacuum gripper | Vision (down-looking) | Speed and Z stiffness |
| Heavy palletising | 4-axis articulated (high-payload) | Servo-electric | Clamp gripper | None | High payload, simple motion |
| Precision assembly | SCARA (with RCC) | Servo-electric | Pneumatic finger | Force/torque + vision | Compliance and Z stiffness |
| Machining (large parts) | 6-axis high-payload | Servo-electric | Spindle / drill | Force sensor | High stiffness |
| Visual inspection | 6-axis + linear track | Servo-electric | Camera + lighting | Vision system | Multi-angle inspection |
| CMM-style inspection | 6-axis high-accuracy | Servo-electric | Touch probe / laser | Probe + vision | Absolute accuracy |
