## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**Differentiate between inductive and capacitive proximity sensors based on (i) target material, (ii) sensing principle, and (iii) typical applications.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Inductive | Capacitive |
|-----------|-----------|------------|
| **Target material** | Metallic only (ferrous and non-ferrous) | Any material with $\varepsilon_r > 1$ (metals, plastics, wood, liquids) |
| **Sensing principle** | Eddy current loss in a metallic target alters the LC oscillator amplitude | Change in capacitance between active and reference electrode due to target approach |
| **Typical range** | 0.8 mm – 60 mm | 1 mm – 50 mm |
| **Applications** | Metal part counting, machine tool position feedback, gear-tooth sensing | Liquid level detection, plastic part detection, paper stack monitoring |

Both are non-contact, solid-state devices with no moving parts, making them highly reliable in industrial environments. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**Explain the working principle of a piezoelectric force sensor. State two of its advantages and one limitation.**

**Model Answer:**

A **piezoelectric force sensor** uses the property of certain crystals (e.g., quartz, PZT — lead zirconate titanate) to develop an electric charge on their surface when subjected to mechanical stress. The charge $q$ generated is directly proportional to the applied force $F$:

$$q = d \cdot F$$

where $d$ is the piezoelectric charge coefficient (C/N). A charge-amplifier circuit converts this small charge into a proportional output voltage.

**Working sequence:**
1. Force applied to sensor housing.
2. Crystal stack experiences compressive stress.
3. Dipoles within crystal align, producing surface charge.
4. Electrodes collect the charge.
5. Charge amplifier outputs a voltage proportional to force.

**Advantages:**
- Very high bandwidth (up to MHz range) — excellent for **dynamic** measurements like impact, vibration, and shock.
- High stiffness → minimal mechanical deformation of the sensor.

**Limitation:**
- **Cannot measure static (DC) forces** because accumulated charge leaks away over time, causing signal drift. A static force must be measured with a strain-gauge load cell instead. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Expected Pattern 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the construction and working of different types of **hydraulic actuators** with suitable diagrams. Compare the performance of hydraulic cylinders and hydraulic motors.

**Model Solution:**

**Construction of a Hydraulic System:**
A complete hydraulic actuation system comprises the following components:
1. **Oil reservoir (tank)** — stores hydraulic oil and allows air to separate.
2. **Pump** (gear / vane / piston) — driven by an electric motor, pressurises the oil.
3. **Filter** — removes particulates to protect downstream components.
4. **Pressure relief valve** — safety device limiting maximum system pressure.
5. **Directional control valve (DCV)** — routes fluid to either side of the actuator; commonly 4/3-way (4 ports, 3 positions).
6. **Actuator** — cylinder (linear) or motor (rotary).
7. **Pressure gauges and sensors** — for monitoring and feedback.
8. **Return line and cooler** — returns fluid to tank and dissipates heat.

**Hydraulic Cylinder (Linear Actuator):**
- A **single-acting cylinder** has oil entering one side; the rod is returned by an external spring or load.
- A **double-acting cylinder** has oil entering alternately on both sides; rod extends and retracts under positive pressure.
- Construction: cylindrical barrel with honed inner surface, piston with seals, piston rod (chrome-plated), end caps, and port connections.

**Thrust force equations:**

$$F_{ext} = P \cdot A_{piston}$$

$$F_{ret} = P \cdot (A_{piston} - A_{rod})$$

**Hydraulic Motor (Rotary Actuator):**
- **Gear motor** — simple, low-to-medium pressure, fixed displacement.
- **Vane motor** — sliding vanes in an eccentric rotor; smooth, low noise.
- **Piston (axial or radial) motor** — high efficiency, high pressure; used in heavy machinery and mobile hydraulics.

**Torque equation (theoretical):**

$$T = \frac{\Delta p \cdot V_d}{2\pi}$$

where $\Delta p$ = pressure drop across motor and $V_d$ = displacement volume per revolution (m³/rev).

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Hydraulic Cylinder | Hydraulic Motor |
|-----------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Motion type | Linear | Rotary |
| Key equation | $F = P \cdot A$ | $T = \Delta p \cdot V_d / 2\pi$ |
| Typical speed | 0.01 – 0.5 m/s | 50 – 3000 rpm |
| Typical force/torque | Up to several MN | Up to several kN·m |
| Application | Press, jack, lift | Excavator swing, winch |

**Diagrams:** (sketch the cylinder cross-section and gear motor cross-section — 1 mark for neat diagram)

**(7 marks)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **electric, hydraulic, and pneumatic actuators** based on at least eight important parameters. Give one industrial application of each.

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Electric | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
|-----------|----------|-----------|-----------|
| Energy source | Electrical | Pressurised oil | Compressed air |
| Power density | Low–Medium | Very High | Low–Medium |
| Precision | Very High | High | Low–Medium |
| Speed | Fast (high rpm) | Medium (controlled) | Very Fast |
| Force capability | Low–Medium | Very High | Low–Medium |
| Stiffness / rigidity | High | Very High | Low (compressible) |
| Cleanliness | Very clean (no leak) | Prone to oil leak | Very clean (exhaust is air) |
| Cost of system | Medium | High | Low |
| Maintenance | Low | High (oil, filter) | Low–Medium |
| Noise | Low | Medium | High (needs silencer) |
| Position holding | Excellent (with brake) | Excellent | Poor (needs brake/valve) |
| Typical pressure | — | 40 – 350 bar | 2 – 8 bar |
| Example application | CNC servo axis | Excavator boom | Pick-and-place gripper |

**Key Insights for KTU:**
- Choose **electric** when precision, cleanliness, and computer control are required.
- Choose **hydraulic** when very high force/torque in a compact envelope is required.
- Choose **pneumatic** when speed, simplicity, and low cost dominate over precision.

**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Expected Pattern 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **machine vision system** for industrial inspection. Discuss at least **four** applications of machine vision in modern automation.

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram of a Machine Vision System:**

```
Object  →  Lighting  →  Lens  →  Image Sensor  →  A/D Converter
                                                    ↓
Display ←  HMI ← Decision ← Algorithm ← Digital Image Processor
                              ↓
                          Pass / Fail Output → PLC → Reject Actuator
```

**Working Steps:**

1. **Illumination:** Controlled LED ring, dome light, or laser line provides consistent, shadow-free lighting on the object. Lighting is the single most important factor in vision system performance.
2. **Image capture:** A lens projects the illuminated object onto a 2D **CCD or CMOS image sensor** organised as a matrix of pixels (e.g., 640 × 480, 1920 × 1080).
3. **Digitisation:** The analog pixel voltages are digitised by an A/D converter (typically 8- or 10-bit) and stored in frame memory.
4. **Image processing:** A DSP / FPGA / embedded PC runs algorithms such as:
   - Thresholding and segmentation
   - Edge detection (Sobel, Canny)
   - Blob analysis, template matching
   - Optical Character Recognition (OCR) and barcode reading
5. **Decision logic:** The processor compares measured features (size, position, colour, defect area) with tolerance limits and outputs a Pass / Fail signal.
6. **Communication:** The decision is sent to a PLC which triggers a reject actuator (pusher, air jet) or accepts the part.

**Four Applications:**

1. **Automated visual inspection** of PCB solder joints for defects.
2. **Pick-and-place guidance** for robotic arms to locate randomly oriented parts.
3. **Barcode / QR / Data Matrix reading** in logistics and packaging lines.
4. **Surface defect detection** on rolled steel, glass, and painted panels.
5. *(Optional 5th)* Traffic monitoring and ADAS in autonomous vehicles.

**(7 marks)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the construction and working of:
**(i) Inductive Proximity Sensor (3 marks)**
**(ii) Capacitive Proximity Sensor (2 marks)**
**(iii) Ultrasonic Proximity Sensor (2 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Inductive Proximity Sensor (3 marks):**

**Construction:**
- A **ferrite-core coil** wound on a former, connected to a capacitor to form an LC tank circuit.
- The LC tank is part of a **Colpitts / Hartley oscillator** operating at 5 kHz to 10 MHz.
- A detector circuit monitors oscillation amplitude.
- An output stage (NPN / PNP transistor, NO / NC) drives the load.
- All components are encapsulated in a threaded metal or plastic housing with a sensing face at one end.

**Working:**
- With no target, the LC oscillator produces a sustained high-frequency alternating magnetic field radiating from the sensing face.
- When a **ferrous or non-ferrous metal target** enters the magnetic field, **eddy currents** are induced in the target.
- These eddy currents draw energy from the oscillator, **damping the oscillation amplitude**.
- The detector senses this amplitude drop and switches the output transistor.
- Sensing range: typically 0.8 – 60 mm; only metals are detected.

**(ii) Capacitive Proximity Sensor (2 marks):**

**Construction:**
- Two electrodes — one active and one reference — form a capacitor at the sensing face.
- The active electrode is connected to a high-frequency RC oscillator.
- A detector and output switching stage complete the circuit.

**Working:**
- With no target, the oscillator's feedback is balanced and the circuit oscillates at a set frequency.
- When any object (metal, plastic, wood, liquid) approaches, it acts as a **third electrode / dielectric**, **increasing the capacitance** of the active electrode to ground.
- This detunes the oscillator, reducing its amplitude.
- The detector switches the output when the amplitude change exceeds a set threshold.
- Can detect both conductive and non-conductive materials.

**(iii) Ultrasonic Proximity Sensor (2 marks):**

**Construction:**
- A **piezoelectric transducer** (e.g., PZT) that can both transmit and receive ultrasound.
- An electronic driver excites the transducer with a short burst (typically a few cycles) at its resonant frequency (e.g., 40 kHz, 200 kHz, 500 kHz).
- A timer / microcontroller measures the round-trip time between transmitted burst and received echo.

**Working:**
- The transducer emits an ultrasonic pulse into the air.
- The pulse travels at the speed of sound (**c ≈ 343 m/s**), strikes the object, and returns as an echo.
- The distance to the object is computed as $D = c \cdot t / 2$.
- Works for any material that reflects sound — solids, liquids, granular media — and is **independent of colour and transparency**.

**(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Examiners Penalise (Module 3):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in ultrasonic time-of-flight** — students often write $D = c \cdot t$ instead of $D = c \cdot t / 2$. The echo travels to the target **and back**, so divide by 2.
> 
> 2. **Confusing sensor range with sensor output** — proximity sensors have a **rated switching distance** (Sn). Operating distance is typically 0.81 × Sn for inductive, 0.7 × Sn for capacitive, with correction factors for different target metals. Examiners deduct marks if the student assumes the rated range is always the actual range.
> 
> 3. **Mixing up single-acting and double-acting cylinders** — single-acting has one port and a return spring; double-acting has two ports. Many students draw only one port in the diagram and lose a mark.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting the rod-side area reduction in retracting force** — for a double-rod or single-rod cylinder, $F_{ret} = P \cdot A_{rod\,side}$, not $P \cdot A_{piston}$.
> 
> 5. **Not stating units in numerical answers** — a numerical answer without units is incomplete. Always write **N, kN, m/s, mm, V, mA, kHz**.
> 
> 6. **Conflating back-EMF constant and torque constant** — in SI units $k_e = k_t$, but in CGS or other systems they may differ. Always state the units being used.
> 
> 7. **Drawing vision-system block diagrams without the lighting block** — examiners specifically look for "Lighting → Lens → Sensor → Processor → Output" sequence. Missing lighting costs 1 mark.
> 
> 8. **Using pneumatic formulas in a hydraulic problem and vice versa** — both use $F = P \cdot A$, but the **pressure ranges are very different** (bar vs kPa). Always state the system type first.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (must memorise before KTU exam):**
> 
> **Sensors:**
> - ☐ Inductive proximity sensors detect **metals only** via eddy-current damping of an LC oscillator.
> - ☐ Capacitive proximity sensors detect **any material** ($\varepsilon_r > 1$) via change in capacitance $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$.
> - ☐ Optical proximity sensors use **light interruption or reflection**; affected by colour and transparency.
> - ☐ Ultrasonic proximity sensors use **time-of-flight** of sound waves; $D = c t / 2$ with $c = 343$ m/s; works on all surfaces.
> - ☐ Vision sensors (CCD/CMOS) capture images; image processing (edge, blob, OCR) extracts features.
> - ☐ Strain-gauge force sensors use a **Wheatstone bridge**; $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$ with $GF = 2.0$ for constantan.
> - ☐ Piezoelectric force sensors use $q = d \cdot F$; good for **dynamic** forces only, not static.
> 
> **Actuators:**
> - ☐ **Electric actuators** = DC, stepper, servo motors; precise, clean, lower force.
> - ☐ **Hydraulic actuators** = cylinder/motor with oil at 40–350 bar; very high force, position-holding, messy.
> - ☐ **Pneumatic actuators** = cylinder/motor with air at 2–8 bar; fast, clean, low force, compressible.
> - ☐ Cylinder thrust: $F = P \cdot A_{piston}$; rod-side area $A_r = \pi (D^2 - d^2)/4$.
> - ☐ Cylinder velocity: $v = Q / A_{piston}$.
> - ☐ Motor torque: $T = k_t \Phi I_a$; back-EMF: $E_b = k_e \Phi \omega$; power: $P = T \omega$.
> - ☐ Stepper step angle: $\theta_{step} = 360° / N_{steps}$ (typically 1.8° for hybrid 200-step motors).
> - ☐ Hydraulic power: $P = p \cdot Q$ (with $p$ in Pa, $Q$ in m³/s).
> 
> **Numbers to Memorise:**
> - ☐ $c_{air} = 343$ m/s
> - ☐ $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m
> - ☐ $GF_{constantan} = 2.0$
> - ☐ $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa = 1.01325 bar
> - ☐ Standard pneumatic pressure ≈ 6 bar
> - ☐ Standard hydraulic pressure ≈ 100–200 bar
> - ☐ Standard NPN / PNP sensor supply = 24 V DC
> 
> **Three-Step Strategy for KTU Answers:**
> 1. **Define** the sensor/actuator in one sentence (1 mark).
> 2. **Draw** a labelled block diagram or cross-section (1–2 marks).
> 3. **Explain** the working principle with the correct formula and units (remaining marks).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Machine Learning Algorithms and Their Applications in Robotics (e.g., Vision, Decision-Making)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT865 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Machine Learning Algorithms and Their Applications in Robotics (e.g., Vision, Decision-Making)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Machine Learning (ML)** is a sub-field of Artificial Intelligence (AI) that enables computational systems to **learn patterns, relationships, and decision policies directly from data** without being explicitly programmed for every scenario. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **PEITT865 – Robotics and Automation**, ML is positioned as the **"perception–cognition engine"** that empowers a robot to:

1. Interpret high-dimensional sensor streams (cameras, LiDAR, IMU, force/torque).
2. Generalize behaviors across unseen environments.
3. Adapt control policies in real time through experience.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim-aligned):**  
> *Machine Learning in robotics refers to a class of algorithms that build a mathematical model $f_\theta : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ based on sample data $(x_i, y_i)$ drawn from an unknown joint distribution $P(X, Y)$, with the objective of minimizing an expected risk functional $\mathcal{R}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim P}[\mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x), y)]$ such that the robot performs perception, planning, and control tasks with bounded generalization error.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine teaching a **toddler to recognize a cup**:
- You do not give the toddler a 200-page rulebook describing geometry, color, handle position, or material.
- You simply **show 50 cups** (and 50 non-cups), say "cup / not cup," and the child's brain **self-discovers** the visual concept.

Machine Learning replicates this biologically: instead of hard-coded geometric rules, the robot "sees" thousands of labelled images and a **statistical function approximator** (linear, neural, tree-based) discovers the decision boundary.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Classical Robotics:**  
> - *Classical:* Hand-engineered features (e.g., Canny edges, Hough transforms, PID gains).  
> - *ML-based:* Features are **learned** from data (e.g., CNN features, RL policies).

### 1.3 Three Canonical Paradigms of ML in Robotics

| Paradigm | Robot Learns From | Robotics Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Supervised Learning** | Labelled $(x, y)$ pairs | Grasp pose prediction, object classification |
| **Unsupervised Learning** | Unlabelled $x$ only | Scene segmentation, anomaly detection in motor currents |
| **Reinforcement Learning (RL)** | Reward signal $r_t$ from environment | Locomotion, manipulation, autonomous navigation |

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU 2024 syllabus, **Reinforcement Learning is given highest weightage** because it directly produces **action policies $\pi(a \mid s)$** for embodied agents.

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Bolded Constants)

- **Accuracy:** $\text{Acc} = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$
- **Precision:** $\text{Prec} = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$
- **Recall (Sensitivity):** $\text{Rec} = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$
- **F1-Score:** $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Prec}\cdot\text{Rec}}{\text{Prec}+\text{Rec}}$
- **Intersection over Union (IoU)** — critical for robotic vision: $\text{IoU} = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$
- **Mean Squared Error (MSE):** $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Supervised Learning — Mathematical Foundation

**Goal:** Approximate an unknown target function $f^* : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ from a hypothesis class $\mathcal{H}$.

**Linear Regression (the simplest parametric model):**

$$\hat{y} = w^\top x + b$$

where $w \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is the weight vector and $b \in \mathbb{R}$ is the bias.

**Loss Function (Mean Squared Error):**

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}}(w,b) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(y_i - (w^\top x_i + b)\right)^2$$

**Why it works for robotics:** Predicting continuous outputs like **joint torques, end-effector velocities, or grasp force magnitudes** from sensor readings.

**Logistic Regression (binary classification, e.g., "graspable" vs. "non-graspable"):**

$$P(y=1 \mid x; w) = \sigma(w^\top x) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-w^\top x}}$$

**Cross-Entropy Loss:**

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y}_i)\right]$$

### 2.2 Unsupervised Learning

**K-Means Clustering** — used in robotics to discover **object categories** in point clouds or to compress **LiDAR scans**.

Algorithm steps:
1. Initialize $K$ centroids $\{\mu_1, \mu_2, \dots, \mu_K\}$.
2. **Assignment step:** Assign each point $x_i$ to nearest centroid: $c_i = \arg\min_k \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$.
3. **Update step:** Recompute centroid: $\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert}\sum_{i \in C_k} x_i$.
4. Iterate until convergence of objective $J = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\mathbb{1}\{c_i = k\}\Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$.

**Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** — reduces dimensionality of high-D vision data:

$$X_{\text{centered}} = X - \bar{X}, \quad \Sigma = \frac{1}{N}X_{\text{centered}}^\top X_{\text{centered}}, \quad X_{\text{PCA}} = X_{\text{centered}} W_k$$

where $W_k$ contains the top $k$ eigenvectors of covariance $\Sigma$.

### 2.3 Reinforcement Learning (RL) — The Core of Robotic Decision-Making

**Markov Decision Process (MDP) Tuple:** $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma \rangle$
- $\mathcal{S}$: state space (robot pose, sensor readings)
- $\mathcal{A}$: action space (joint commands, velocities)
- $P(s' \mid s, a)$: transition probability
- $R(s, a)$: reward function
- $\gamma \in [0,1)$: discount factor (typically **0.99**)

**Policy:** $\pi(a \mid s)$ — probability of taking action $a$ in state $s$.

**Value Function:** Expected cumulative discounted reward.

$$V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\left[\sum_{t=0}^{\infty}\gamma^t R(s_t, a_t) \,\Big\vert\, s_0 = s\right]$$

**Action-Value (Q) Function:**

$$Q^\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\left[\sum_{t=0}^{\infty}\gamma^t R(s_t, a_t) \,\Big\vert\, s_0 = s, a_0 = a\right]$$

**Bellman Optimality Equation:**

$$Q^*(s,a) = R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a')$$

**Q-Learning Update Rule (off-policy, model-free):**

$$Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha\left[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s,a)\right]$$

where $\alpha$ is the learning rate (typically **0.1 to 0.5**).

### 2.4 Deep Learning Backbone for Robotic Vision

**Convolutional Neural Network (CNN):** Hierarchical feature extractor.

A convolution layer applies $K$ learnable filters $W_k \in \mathbb{R}^{h \times w \times C}$:

$$Z_{i,j,k} = \sigma\left(\sum_{u=0}^{h-1}\sum_{v=0}^{w-1}\sum_{c=0}^{C-1} W_{u,v,c}^{(k)} \cdot X_{i+u, j+v, c} + b_k\right)$$

**Real-world utility in robotics:**
- **Object detection** (YOLO, Faster-RCNN) for bin-picking.
- **Semantic segmentation** (U-Net, DeepLab) for terrain traversability.
- **Pose estimation** (PoseNet) for visual servoing.
- **Depth estimation** (Monodepth) as virtual LiDAR.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Application in Robotics | Symbol Glossary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\hat{y} = w^\top x + b$ | Joint torque regression | $w$ weights, $b$ bias |
| 2 | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Training loss for continuous control | $N$ samples |
| 3 | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Graspable/not classification | Sigmoid activation |
| 4 | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -[y\log\hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})]$ | Vision classifier loss | Cross-entropy |
| 5 | $V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[\sum_{t}\gamma^t R_t]$ | Locomotion policy evaluation | $\gamma$ discount |
| 6 | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha[r + \gamma\max_{a'}Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]$ | Q-learning update | $\alpha$ learning rate |
| 7 | $J_{K\text{-means}} = \sum_i \sum_k \mathbb{1}\{c_i=k\}\Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$ | Point cloud clustering | $\mu_k$ centroids |
| 8 | $\text{IoU} = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Bounding box evaluation | $A, B$ regions |
| 9 | $Z_{i,j,k} = \sigma(W_k * X + b_k)$ | CNN feature extraction | $*$ convolution |
| 10 | $\nabla_w \mathcal{L} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w}$ | Backpropagation update | Gradient |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In real robotic systems, the same ML model rarely works in isolation. A **Perception → Localization → Planning → Control** stack uses supervised learning for vision, unsupervised learning for SLAM loop closure, and RL for low-level control. This **multi-ML fusion** is frequently asked in KTU Module-4 descriptive answers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Form Solution for Linear Regression

We want to find $w^*$ that minimizes $\mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - w^\top x_i)^2$ (ignoring bias for clarity, absorbed into $x$).

**Step 1 — Vectorize the loss.** Let $X \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times d}$ be the data matrix (rows = samples) and $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^N$ the target vector.

$$\mathcal{L}(w) = \frac{1}{N}(\mathbf{y} - Xw)^\top(\mathbf{y} - Xw)$$

**Step 2 — Expand the quadratic form.**

$$\mathcal{L}(w) = \frac{1}{N}\left[\mathbf{y}^\top\mathbf{y} - 2w^\top X^\top \mathbf{y} + w^\top X^\top X w\right]$$

**Step 3 — Take the gradient w.r.t. $w$.** Using standard matrix calculus identities $\frac{\partial}{\partial w}(w^\top A w) = 2Aw$ (when $A$ is symmetric) and $\frac{\partial}{\partial w}(b^\top w) = b$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = \frac{1}{N}\left[-2X^\top \mathbf{y} + 2X^\top X w\right]$$

**Step 4 — Set the gradient to zero for the optimum.**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; X^\top X w^* = X^\top \mathbf{y}$$

**Step 5 — Solve the Normal Equation.**

$$\boxed{\,w^* = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top \mathbf{y}\,}$$

**Engineering Note:** The inverse $(X^\top X)^{-1}$ exists only when $X^\top X$ is **positive definite**, i.e., when columns of $X$ are linearly independent. In robotic datasets with high-collinearity (e.g., consecutive time-series IMU readings), the **Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse** $w^* = X^{+}\mathbf{y}$ is preferred.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Gradient Descent Update Rule

For cases where the closed-form is intractable (e.g., neural networks), we use **iterative optimization**.

**Step 1 — Initialize** $w^{(0)}$ randomly (e.g., $w^{(0)} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.01^2)$).

**Step 2 — At each iteration $t$, compute the gradient** of the loss over a mini-batch $\mathcal{B}_t$ of size $B$:

$$g^{(t)} = \nabla_w \mathcal{L}_{\mathcal{B}_t}(w^{(t)}) = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}_t}\nabla_w \mathcal{L}_i(w^{(t)})$$

**Step 3 — Update the weights** in the direction of steepest descent:

$$w^{(t+1)} = w^{(t)} - \eta \cdot g^{(t)}$$

where $\eta > 0$ is the **learning rate** (typical values: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-1}$).

**Step 4 — Convergence check:** Stop when $\Vert g^{(t)} \Vert_2 < \epsilon$ or after a fixed budget of $T$ iterations.

**Robotic Application:** Training a CNN for **object detection in bin-picking** — the loss $\mathcal{L}$ includes a localization MSE term and a classification cross-entropy term, summed across $B$ mini-batch images.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Q-Learning Bellman Update

We start from the **Bellman Optimality Equation** for the action-value function:

$$Q^*(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_{s'}\left[R(s,a) + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a')\right]$$

**Step 1 — Sample transition.** Robot executes action $a$ in state $s$, observes reward $r$ and next state $s'$.

**Step 2 — Form the Temporal Difference (TD) target.** Because we do not know $P(s' \mid s,a)$ analytically, we use a **sample-based estimate**:

$$\text{TD Target: } \; y_t = r_t + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a'; \theta)$$

**Step 3 — Form the TD error** (the "surprise"):

$$\delta_t = y_t - Q(s, a; \theta)$$

**Step 4 — Stochastic gradient ascent to maximize expected reward** is equivalent to:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{(s,a,r,s')\sim \mathcal{D}}\left[\frac{1}{2}\left(r + \gamma\max_{a'}Q(s',a';\theta) - Q(s,a;\theta)\right)^2\right]$$

**Step 5 — Take gradient w.r.t. $\theta$** and apply gradient descent on this loss:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \alpha\left[r + \gamma\max_{a'}Q(s',a';\theta_t) - Q(s,a;\theta_t)\right]\nabla_\theta Q(s,a;\theta_t)$$

**Step 6 — For tabular Q-learning** (state and action discretized into a table), $\nabla_\theta Q(s,a;\theta)$ becomes the indicator $\mathbb{1}\{s,a\}$, yielding the textbook update:

$$\boxed{\,Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha\left[r + \gamma\max_{a'}Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)\right]\,}$$

**Robotic Application:** A mobile robot learns to navigate a 2D grid. Each cell = state, each cardinal direction = action, $r = +1$ for goal, $r = -1$ for collision, $r = -0.01$ per step (encourages short paths).

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Cross-Entropy Loss Gradient (Logistic Regression)

For sigmoid output $\hat{y} = \sigma(z)$ with $z = w^\top x$:

**Step 1 — Define binary cross-entropy loss for a single sample:**

$$\mathcal{L} = -[y\log\hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})]$$

**Step 2 — Use the identity** $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z} = \hat{y} - y$$

**Step 3 — Apply chain rule** $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z} \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial w} = (\hat{y} - y) \cdot x$:

$$\boxed{\,\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = (\hat{y} - y)\,x\,}$$

**Engineering Implication:** This beautifully simple gradient is the foundation of **backpropagation** in deep networks — it tells us *how much each weight contributed to the error* relative to its input activation.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: ML Algorithms in Robotics

| Algorithm | Type | Robotics Use-Case | Strength | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Linear Regression | Supervised | Torque prediction | Closed-form, fast | Cannot model nonlinearity |
| Logistic Regression | Supervised | Grasp success classifier | Probabilistic output | Linear boundary |
| Decision Tree / RF | Supervised | Terrain classification | Handles mixed features | Overfits without pruning |
| SVM | Supervised | Object recognition | Strong margin in high-D | Slow on big data |
| K-Means | Unsupervised | Point-cloud clustering | Simple, scalable | Must choose $K$ |
| PCA | Unsupervised | Vision feature compression | Dimensionality reduction | Linear only |
| Q-Learning | RL | Grid navigation | Model-free | Tabular blow-up |
| DQN | Deep RL | Atari, simple manipulation | Scales to high-D state | Sample inefficient |
| PPO | Policy-Gradient RL | Quadruped locomotion | Stable training | Hyperparameter sensitive |
| CNN | Deep Learning | Object detection | Hierarchical vision | Needs lots of labelled data |
| RNN / LSTM | Deep Learning | Time-series IMU | Captures temporal context | Slow sequential training |
| Transformer | Deep Learning | Multi-modal sensor fusion | Attention over modalities | Computationally heavy |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Machine Learning Taxonomy for Robotics

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Machine Learning for Robotics] --> B[Supervised Learning]
    A --> C[Unsupervised Learning]
    A --> D[Reinforcement Learning]
    A --> E[Deep Learning]

    B --> B1[Linear Regression]
    B --> B2[Logistic Regression]
    B --> B3[Support Vector Machines]
    B --> B4[Random Forest]

    C --> C1[K-Means Clustering]
    C --> C2[PCA]
    C --> C3[DBSCAN]
    C --> C4[Autoencoders]

    D --> D1[Q-Learning]
    D --> D2[Deep Q-Network]
    D --> D3[Policy Gradient]
    D --> D4[Actor Critic]

    E --> E1[CNN for Vision]
    E --> E2[RNN for Time Series]
    E --> E3[Transformers]
    E --> E4[Diffusion Models]
```

### 4.2 Robotic Perception → Decision Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Camera Image] --> P1[CNN Feature Extractor]
    S2[LiDAR Point Cloud] --> P2[PointNet Encoder]
    S3[IMU Stream] --> P3[LSTM Temporal Model]
    S4[Joint Encoders] --> P4[MLP]

    P1 --> F[Sensor Fusion Layer]
    P2 --> F
    P3 --> F
    P4 --> F

    F --> DEC[Decision Module]
    DEC --> A1[Motion Planner]
    DEC --> A2[Grasp Selector]
    DEC --> A3[Behavior Policy]

    A1 --> R[Robot Actuators]
    A2 --> R
    A3 --> R
```

### 4.3 Reinforcement Learning Agent–Environment Loop

```mermaid
graph TD
    ENV[Environment / Robot World] -->|State s_t| AG[RL Agent]
    AG -->|Action a_t| ENV
    ENV -->|Reward r_t| AG
    AG --> POL[Policy π]
    AG --> VAL[Value Function Q]
    POL --> AG
    VAL --> AG
    AG -->|Update Q s a| MEM[Replay Buffer]
    MEM --> AG
```

### 4.4 Convolutional Neural Network Block for Robotic Vision

```mermaid
graph TD
    IN[Input Image HxWx3] --> C1[Conv2D + ReLU]
    C1 --> P1[MaxPool]
    P1 --> C2[Conv2D + ReLU]
    C2 --> P2[MaxPool]
    P2 --> C3[Conv2D + ReLU]
    C3 --> F[Flatten]
    F --> FC1[Fully Connected + ReLU]
    FC1 --> FC2[Fully Connected + Softmax]
    FC2 --> OUT[Object Class / Grasp Pose]
```

### 4.5 Decision-Making Flow in a Mobile Robot

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Start Mission] --> PER[Perceive Environment]
    PER --> LOC[Localize Robot]
    LOC --> PLAN[Plan Path using Learned Policy]
    PLAN --> CHK{Obstacle Detected?}
    CHK -->|Yes| RE[Re-plan with RL Policy]
    CHK -->|No| EXE[Execute Action]
    RE --> EXE
    EXE --> UPD[Update Q-values]
    UPD --> PER
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**  
Define Machine Learning. List and briefly explain the three main paradigms of machine learning relevant to robotics.

**Model Answer:**  
Machine Learning is a sub-field of Artificial Intelligence that enables systems to learn from data and improve their performance over a task $T$ with respect to a metric $M$ based on experience $E$ (Mitchell's formal definition). In robotics, the three paradigms are:
1. **Supervised Learning** — Robot learns an input-output mapping from labelled $(x,y)$ pairs (e.g., grasp-success prediction).
2. **Unsupervised Learning** — Robot discovers structure in unlabelled sensor data (e.g., K-means clustering of LiDAR returns into objects).
3. **Reinforcement Learning** — Robot learns a policy $\pi(a \mid s)$ by interacting with the environment and receiving reward signals (e.g., learning to walk).

*(3 marks — definitions with robotics-specific examples expected.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**  
Write the Q-learning update rule. Explain the role of $\alpha$, $\gamma$, and the term $\max_{a'} Q(s', a')$.

**Model Answer:**  

$$Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha\left[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s,a)\right]$$

- **$\alpha$ (learning rate):** Controls how aggressively new information overrides old Q-value. $\alpha \in (0,1]$.
- **$\gamma$ (discount factor):** Weighs the importance of future rewards. $\gamma = 0$ makes the agent myopic; $\gamma \to 1$ makes it far-sighted.
- **$\max_{a'} Q(s', a')$:** The best expected return achievable from the next state, embodying the *Bellman optimality* principle.

---

### Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE — Answer ANY ONE of the following:**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture and working of a **Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)** for robotic vision. Discuss how it differs from a fully connected network and justify why CNNs are preferred for image-based perception in robots. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **Reinforcement Learning framework** for a mobile robot navigating an unknown environment. Define the components of the MDP tuple and explain the Q-learning algorithm step-by-step. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

A CNN is a hierarchical feature extractor composed of three building blocks:
1. **Convolutional Layer** — Applies $K$ learnable filters $W_k$ over local receptive fields, producing feature maps $Z_k = \sigma(W_k * X + b_k)$. The convolution operator *captures spatial locality*, exploiting the fact that nearby pixels are more correlated than distant ones — a property violated by fully connected (FC) networks.
2. **Pooling Layer** — Performs down-sampling (typically max-pooling) to introduce **translation invariance** and reduce computation. E.g., a $2 \times 2$ max-pool reduces a feature map of size $H \times W$ to $H/2 \times W/2$.
3. **Fully Connected Layer** — At the end of the network, FC layers map the flattened high-level features to class scores (via softmax) or regression outputs (e.g., bounding box coordinates).

**Why CNNs over FC for robotic vision:**
- **Parameter sharing** — A single filter is reused across the entire image, drastically reducing parameters ($K \cdot h \cdot w \ll H \cdot W \cdot d$).
- **Translation equivariance** — Critical for object detection as robots move.
- **Hierarchical features** — Early layers detect edges; mid layers detect parts; deep layers detect whole objects.

For a robot, this means **robust, real-time object recognition** in cluttered scenes with orders-of-magnitude fewer parameters than an equivalent FC network.

**Model Solution (b):**

The RL framework models the robot as an **agent** interacting with a **stochastic environment** over discrete time steps $t = 0, 1, 2, \dots$.

**MDP Tuple Components:**
- $\mathcal{S}$: State space — e.g., $(x, y, \theta, \text{LiDAR scan})$ of the robot.
- $\mathcal{A}$: Action space — e.g., $\{\text{forward}, \text{turn-left}, \text{turn-right}, \text{stop}\}$.
- $P(s' \mid s, a)$: Transition probability — environment dynamics (often unknown to the robot).
- $R(s, a)$: Reward function — e.g., $r = +100$ at goal, $r = -10$ at collision, $r = -0.1$ per step.
- $\gamma \in [0,1)$: Discount factor (typically 0.99).

**Q-Learning Algorithm Steps:**
1. Initialize $Q(s, a)$ arbitrarily (e.g., zero) for all $s, a$.
2. **For each episode:**
   - Initialize state $s_0$ from environment.
   - **For each time step $t$:**
     - Choose action $a_t$ using $\epsilon$-greedy policy (with probability $\epsilon$ pick random action; else $a_t = \arg\max_a Q(s_t, a)$).
     - Execute $a_t$, observe reward $r_t$ and next state $s_{t+1}$.
     - Update: $Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha\left[r_t + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, a_t)\right]$.
     - Set $s_t \leftarrow s_{t+1}$.
3. **Until convergence:** $Q$ converges to $Q^*$ under standard stochastic approximation conditions (all $(s, a)$ visited infinitely often, $\sum_t \alpha_t = \infty$, $\sum_t \alpha_t^2 < \infty$).

The **learned policy** is then $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_a Q^*(s, a)$, which the robot executes greedily at deployment.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Discuss the application of **supervised learning algorithms** in robotic perception. Compare Linear Regression, Logistic Regression, and Support Vector Machines (SVM) with suitable robotic examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **K-Means clustering algorithm** in detail. How can a humanoid robot use it to segment an unknown scene into distinct objects? Discuss its limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

Supervised learning underpins much of **robotic perception** — converting raw sensor data into actionable predictions.

- **Linear Regression** $\hat{y} = w^\top x + b$: Predicts continuous outputs. *Robotic example:* Predicting the required joint torque of a manipulator arm from current sensor readings (force, position, velocity). Trained on dataset of $N$ historical $(x_i, y_i)$ pairs; solution is the normal equation $w^* = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top \mathbf{y}$.

- **Logistic Regression** $P(y=1 \mid x) = \sigma(w^\top x)$: Binary classification. *Robotic example:* Classifying a detected region in an RGB image as **"graspable" or "non-graspable"** based on geometric and visual features. Trained using **cross-entropy loss** with gradient $(\hat{y} - y)x$.

- **Support Vector Machine (SVM):** Finds the **maximum-margin hyperplane** separating two classes. *Robotic example:* Surface classification for a planetary rover (sand vs. rock vs. compact soil) using 12-dimensional texture features. Kernel trick (RBF kernel) allows non-linear boundaries.

**Comparative Summary Table (key for marks):**

| Aspect | Linear Reg. | Logistic Reg. | SVM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Output | Continuous | Probability | Class label |
| Loss | MSE | Cross-entropy | Hinge |
| Solver | Closed-form | Iterative | Quadratic programming |
| Robot Use | Torque pred. | Grasp classifier | Terrain classifier |

**Model Solution (b):**

**K-Means Algorithm:**

**Input:** Dataset $\{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N\} \subset \mathbb{R}^d$, number of clusters $K$.

**Output:** Partition $\{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_K\}$ and centroids $\{\mu_1, \dots, \mu_K\}$.

**Step 1 — Initialization.** Choose $K$ initial centroids (randomly or via K-Means++).

**Step 2 — Assignment Step.** For each data point, assign it to the cluster of the nearest centroid:

$$c_i = \arg\min_{k \in \{1, \dots, K\}} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$$

**Step 3 — Update Step.** Recompute each centroid as the mean of assigned points:

$$\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert}\sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$$

**Step 4 — Convergence Check.** Repeat Steps 2–3 until centroid positions stabilize (change below threshold $\epsilon$) or maximum iterations reached.

**Objective Function Being Minimized:**

$$J = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\mathbb{1}\{c_i = k\}\,\Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$$

**Humanoid Robot Application — Scene Segmentation:**
1. A humanoid robot equipped with an **RGB-D camera** captures a point cloud of a tabletop scene containing cups, bottles, and books.
2. Each point is reduced to a 6-D feature $(x, y, z, r, g, b)$.
3. The robot runs **K-Means with $K = 4$**, automatically partitioning the cloud into 4 spatial groups (e.g., cup-cluster, bottle-cluster, book-cluster, table-surface).
4. Each cluster is treated as a **distinct candidate object** for subsequent manipulation.

**Limitations (must be mentioned for full marks):**
- Must pre-specify $K$ (often unknown a priori).
- Sensitive to initial centroid placement — K-Means++ mitigates but does not eliminate.
- Assumes **spherical, equal-variance clusters**; fails on elongated or non-convex shapes (DBSCAN or Gaussian Mixture Models are alternatives).
- Sensitive to outliers (a single rogue point can shift centroids).
- Convergence is to a **local minimum**, not global.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing paradigms:** Writing "supervised learning learns from rewards" — a classic 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Omitting the $\gamma$ term in Q-learning:** Half the derivation marks are lost.
> 3. **Forgetting the role of $K$ in K-Means:** Students often say "K-Means automatically determines cluster count" — INCORRECT.
> 4. **Not relating ML to robotics:** Generic answers without robotic examples (manipulation, SLAM, locomotion) are penalized up to 30%.
> 5. **Skipping diagrams:** KTU 2024 scheme explicitly awards 2 marks for **neat labelled diagrams** in Part B; a missing diagram = 2 marks lost.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **ML = learning from data**; three paradigms = **Supervised / Unsupervised / Reinforcement**.
- ✅ **Supervised:** Linear Regression for continuous, Logistic/SVM for classification, loss = MSE / Cross-Entropy.
- ✅ **Linear Regression closed-form:** $w^* = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top \mathbf{y}$.
- ✅ **Gradient Descent:** $w \leftarrow w - \eta \nabla_w \mathcal{L}$; $\eta$ = learning rate.
- ✅ **Unsupervised:** K-Means minimizes $J = \sum_i \sum_k \mathbb{1}\{c_i=k\}\Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$; PCA projects onto top eigenvectors.
- ✅ **MDP = $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$** — foundation of RL.
- ✅ **Q-Learning Update:** $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha[r + \gamma \max_{a'}Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]$.
- ✅ **Bellman Equation:** $V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[\sum_t \gamma^t R(s_t, a_t)]$.
- ✅ **CNN** = Conv → Pool → Conv → Pool → FC; preferred for vision due to **parameter sharing** and **translation invariance**.
- ✅ **Robotic Applications:** Object detection (CNN), grasping (Logistic Reg.), localization (PCA), navigation (Q-Learning), locomotion (Deep RL).
- ✅ **Metrics to remember:** Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, **IoU** (for vision), MSE, Cross-Entropy.
- ✅ **$\epsilon$-greedy** policy balances exploration vs exploitation in RL.
- ✅ **Markov property:** Future depends only on current $(s, a)$, not history — a key assumption in RL derivations.
- ✅ **Sample efficiency** is the biggest challenge of RL in real robots — hence sim-to-real transfer is an active research area.
- ✅ In KTU 2024, **diagrams carry 2 marks** in Part B; always include a labelled block diagram of the algorithm/architecture.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Human-Robot Interaction (HRI): Fundamentals of HRI, Collaborative Robots (Cobots), Safety in HRI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT865 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Human-Robot Interaction (HRI): Fundamentals of HRI, Collaborative Robots (Cobots), Safety in HRI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Human-Robot Interaction (HRI) — The Big Picture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> *Human-Robot Interaction (HRI)* is an **interdisciplinary field of study** dedicated to understanding, designing, and evaluating robotic systems for use by or with humans. It merges knowledge from **robotics, cognitive psychology, ergonomics, artificial intelligence, and human factors engineering** to create robots that communicate, coordinate, and coexist safely with human operators.

### 🔍 Intuitive Analogy — "The Polite Co-Worker"

Imagine you are sharing a workshop bench with a new assistant. A **good human assistant** will:
- **Watch** what you are doing (perception)
- **Anticipate** when you need a tool (cognition/prediction)
- **Hand it over** without bumping your hand (safe action)
- **Respond** to your verbal commands and gestures (multimodal communication)

A **robot designed with HRI principles** behaves exactly like this *polite co-worker* — it senses, predicts, acts safely, and communicates naturally with you, instead of being locked behind a cage like a traditional factory robot.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why HRI Matters in KTU Context**
> With Industry 5.0 emphasizing **human-centric, sustainable, and resilient manufacturing**, HRI is the cornerstone that allows robots to leave their cages and work *alongside* humans rather than *instead of* them.

---

## 1.2 The Three Primary Categories of HRI

| Category | Description | Typical Setup | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Remote HRI (Teleoperation)** | Human controls robot from a distance | Joystick, haptic suit, VR headset | Bomb disposal, space rovers |
| **Proximate HRI** | Human and robot share the *same workspace* but may not directly touch | Shared floor space with safety zones | Factory cobots assisting assembly |
| **Social HRI** | Robot acts as a *social entity* (companion, tutor) | No shared task, but interaction is the goal | Pepper robot in hospitals, NAO in classrooms |

> [!TIP]
> **Module 4 Focus**: KTU's PEITT865 concentrates heavily on **Proximate HRI**, which directly bridges into **Cobots** and **Safety**.

---

## 1.3 Collaborative Robots (Cobots) — The Caged Robot's Kinder Cousin

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition — Cobot**
> A **Collaborative Robot (Cobot)** is a robot explicitly designed to *physically interact with humans in a shared workspace*, built with inherent safety features such as **force limiting, rounded geometry, lightweight materials, and compliant joints**, in compliance with international safety standards like **ISO 10218** and **ISO/TS 15066**.

### 🔍 Intuitive Analogy — "The Helper in the Kitchen"

Think of a **traditional industrial robot arm** as a powerful, fast, but completely blind **electric drill** — give it the wrong command or step too close, and it can injure you badly. A **cobot**, in contrast, behaves like a **gentle sous-chef** — it might bump your hand while passing the salt, but the built-in force sensors make it instantly *yield* the moment it feels resistance, like a careful dance partner following your lead.

### 📜 Brief Historical Note (Frequently Asked in KTU)

The term **"Cobot"** was coined in **1996** by professors **J. Edward Colgate and Michael Peshkin** at **Northwestern University, USA**. Originally, it referred to a *passive device* that guided a human's motion. The modern meaning (an active robot safe for collaboration) evolved after the introduction of **KUKA LBR iiwa (2013)** and **Universal Robots UR-series**, which became the world's first commercially successful active cobots.

---

## 1.4 Fundamental Communication Modalities in HRI

HRI communication happens through **five primary modalities**, which KTU expects students to enumerate:

1. **Verbal / Spoken Language** — Natural Language Processing (NLP), speech recognition
2. **Non-Verbal Visual** — Gestures, gaze, body posture, facial expressions
3. **Haptic / Tactile** — Touch, force feedback, vibration cues
4. **Proximity & Motion** — Detecting approach, retreating, following
5. **Physiological / Bio-signals** — EEG, EMG, heart-rate (advanced HRI)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric for Trust in HRI** — *Operator Acceptance* is often quantified through surveys like the **Godspeed Questionnaire**, which scores robots on *Anthropomorphism, Animacy, Likeability, Perceived Intelligence,* and *Perceived Safety*.

---

## 1.5 Safety in HRI — The Non-Negotiable Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight — Safety Philosophy**
> Safety in HRI is **not an add-on** but a **design constraint from the very first sketch** of the robot. The governing principle is **Inherently Safe Design** — eliminating hazards rather than just warning about them.

The safety stack in HRI consists of four layers:

1. **Inherent Safety Design** — rounded edges, low inertia, compliant materials
2. **Safeguarding** — physical barriers, light curtains (used in *non-collaborative* zones)
3. **Complementary Protective Measures** — emergency stops, speed limits
4. **Information for Use** — user training, warning labels

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Many students confuse **Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS)** with **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)**. Remember — SRMS is *temporary* (robot halts when human approaches), while PFL is *continuous* (robot moves and limits contact force throughout collaboration).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the HRI System — The Perception–Cognition–Action Loop

Every HRI system, whether a cobot on a factory floor or a service robot in a mall, operates on a continuous **Perception → Cognition → Action** loop. Understanding this loop is essential for KTU derivations.

### 🔁 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Sensing (Perception Layer)**
The robot gathers data about the human and environment using:
- **Vision sensors** (RGB-D cameras, LiDAR) → detect human pose, distance
- **Force/Torque sensors** at joints → detect contact forces
- **Tactile skin sensors** → detect touch location and pressure
- **Microphones** → detect speech commands

**Step 2 — Understanding (Cognition Layer)**
The robot interprets sensor data using:
- **Human intent prediction** (ML models — Hidden Markov Models, LSTMs)
- **Human tracking algorithms** (Kalman filters, particle filters)
- **Trust modeling** (Bayesian networks estimating operator confidence)

**Step 3 — Acting (Action Layer)**
The robot executes a response:
- **Motion planning** with safety constraints (e.g., Dynamic Movement Primitives)
- **Compliance control** (impedance/admittance control yielding to human force)
- **Communication output** (LED indicators, voice, screen feedback)

**Step 4 — Feedback to Human**
The human perceives the robot's action and the loop closes, repeating continuously.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU**: A question on *"Explain the HRI architecture"* (often a 7-mark sub-part) expects exactly this **three-layered loop** with one or two examples of sensors or algorithms per layer.

---

## 2.2 Collaborative Robots (Cobots) — Engineering Principles

### 🛠️ Five Hallmark Engineering Features of a Cobot

1. **Compliant Actuation** — Use of *series elastic actuators (SEA)* or torque-sensed joints to measure and limit applied force in real time.
2. **Lightweight Construction** — Carbon-fiber links, aluminum housings → low inertia → low impact energy.
3. **Rounded Geometry & No Pinch Points** — Smooth contours minimize sharp-contact injuries.
4. **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** — Active electronic limits on joint torque, velocity, and momentum.
5. **Redundant Sensing** — Multiple sensors cross-checking each other (fault tolerance).

### 📐 The Four Collaborative Operation Modes (ISO/TS 15066) — **KTU MUST-KNOW**

| # | Mode | Robot Behaviour | Human Proximity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS)** | Robot halts safely when human enters collaborative workspace, resumes when human leaves | Human can be *very close*, but robot is stationary |
| **2** | **Hand-Guiding (HG)** | Human physically grasps robot and moves it manually; robot follows passively | Direct *physical contact*, robot is passive |
| **3** | **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)** | Robot continuously adjusts speed based on human distance | Human may be close, robot slows down |
| **4** | **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** | Robot moves continuously but limits contact force below biomechanical thresholds | Human may *touch* the robot safely |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Favourite**: Questions like *"Differentiate the four collaborative modes"* (often 7–8 marks) appear frequently. Memorize the table above.

---

## 2.3 Safety in HRI — Quantitative Framework

### 🛡️ International Safety Standards Stack

| Standard | Scope | Key Contribution |
|---|---|---|
| **ISO 12100** | General machinery safety | Risk assessment & reduction philosophy |
| **ISO 10218-1** | Robot safety (manufacturer) | Robot design requirements, inherent safety |
| **ISO 10218-2** | Robot system integration | Cell-level integration safety |
| **ISO/TS 15066** | **Collaborative robots (Technical Specification)** | Biomechanical limits, PFL thresholds, collaborative operation modes |
| **ISO 13849-1** | Safety-related control systems | Performance Levels (PL a–e) |

### ⚖️ Risk Assessment — The Triad

$$\text{Risk} = f(\text{Severity}, \text{Probability}, \text{Frequency of Exposure})$$

KTU commonly tests this as:
> *"Explain the risk assessment process for a collaborative robot cell."*

The standardized process is **4-staged**:
1. **Hazard Identification** — physical, electrical, ergonomic, psychological
2. **Risk Estimation** — Severity × Probability × Frequency
3. **Risk Evaluation** — Compare against acceptable threshold
4. **Risk Reduction** — Apply the *Three-Step Method* (inherent safe design → safeguarding → information for use)

---

## 2.4 ⭐ KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ⭐

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas you need to solve Module 4 numerical / analytical KTU questions are listed below. Memorize the units and variables.**

### 🔸 A. Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM) — *The Most Tested Formula*

$$\boxed{S_p(t_0) = S_h + S_r + S_s + C}$$

Where:
- $S_p(t_0)$ = **Protective Separation Distance** at time $t_0$ (in **mm**)
- $S_h$ = contribution from human movement toward robot (mm)
- $S_r$ = contribution from robot reaction time lag (mm)
- $S_s$ = contribution from robot stopping distance (mm)
- $C$ = intrusion distance (human reach into sensing zone, mm)

The robot must maintain:

$$S_p \;\geq\; v_H \cdot (t_r + t_s) + v_R \cdot t_s + C$$

Where $v_H$ = human approach speed, $v_R$ = robot speed, $t_r$ = robot reaction time, $t_s$ = stopping time.

### 🔸 B. Kinetic Energy of a Moving Robot (for PFL Impact Analysis)

$$\boxed{E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2}$$

This kinetic energy determines the *severity of impact* during accidental contact. **ISO/TS 15066** mandates quasi-static contact limits below **~140 N** pressure and **~350 N** force on most body regions.

### 🔸 C. Power and Force Limiting Threshold

$$\boxed{F_{max} \leq F_{biomech,region}}$$

Where $F_{biomech,region}$ is the *maximum tolerable force* for a specific body region (e.g., hand: ~140 N quasi-static; chest: ~210 N).

### 🔸 D. Joint Torque Limit for PFL Robots

$$\boxed{\tau_{limit} = J(q)^T \cdot F_{max}}$$

Where $J(q)^T$ is the **transpose of the Jacobian matrix** at configuration $q$, and $F_{max}$ is the Cartesian force limit.

### 🔸 E. Trust Dynamics in HRI (Lyapunov-inspired simplified form)

$$\boxed{\dfrac{dT(t)}{dt} = \alpha \cdot P(t) - \beta \cdot D(t)}$$

Where $T$ = trust level, $P$ = positive interaction factor, $D$ = distrust/failure factor, $\alpha,\beta$ = scaling constants.

### 🔸 F. Stopping Time (Braking Dynamics)

$$\boxed{t_s = \dfrac{v_0}{a_{brake}}}$$

Where $v_0$ = initial velocity, $a_{brake}$ = deceleration.

### 🔸 G. Stopping Distance

$$\boxed{S_s = v_0 \cdot t_r + \tfrac{1}{2} a_{brake} \cdot t_s^2}$$

### 🔸 H. Combined Velocity Approximation

$$\boxed{v_{rel} = \sqrt{v_H^2 + v_R^2}}$$

Used in SSM calculations when robot and human move at angles.

### 🔸 I. Body Region Impact Energy Limit (ISO/TS 15066)

| Body Region | Max Quasi-Static Force | Max Pressure |
|---|---|---|
| Hand / Finger | **140 N** | 140 N/cm² |
| Palm | **140 N** | 140 N/cm² |
| Forearm | **180 N** | 140 N/cm² |
| Chest | **210 N** | 110 N/cm² |
| Face | **65 N** | 110 N/cm² |
| Skull / Forehead | **130 N** | 110 N/cm² |

> [!TIP]
> These numbers are **memorize-worthy** for KTU viva and Part-A questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Protective Separation Distance (SSM Mode)

This is the **most analytically important derivation** for KTU Module 4.

### 🎯 Problem Statement
A cobot is operating in **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)** mode. The human operator approaches at speed $v_H = 1600$ mm/s. The robot moves toward the human at $v_R = 250$ mm/s. The robot's reaction time is $t_r = 0.05$ s and stopping time is $t_s = 0.20$ s. The intrusion distance is $C = 100$ mm. Compute the **minimum protective separation distance** that must be maintained to avoid contact.

### 📐 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — State the governing equation.**

We use the ISO/TS 15066 protective separation formula:

$$S_p(t_0) = v_H \cdot (t_r + t_s) + v_R \cdot t_s + C$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given numerical values.**

The human approaches during the *entire* reaction + stopping window:

$$S_h = v_H \cdot (t_r + t_s) = 1600 \cdot (0.05 + 0.20)$$

$$S_h = 1600 \cdot 0.25 = 400 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate robot stopping distance contribution $S_s$.**

$$S_s = v_R \cdot t_s = 250 \cdot 0.20 = 50 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step 4 — Add intrusion distance $C$.**

$$C = 100 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step 5 — Sum all components.**

$$S_p = 400 + 50 + 100 = 550 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step 6 — State the engineering conclusion.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion**: The human must maintain **at least 550 mm** separation from the cobot's nearest moving point. The robot's safety controller must monitor distance in real time and trigger an emergency stop if $d < S_p$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Kinetic Energy & PFL Threshold Check

### 🎯 Problem Statement
A cobot arm has an effective moving mass of $m = 12$ kg and moves at maximum speed $v = 250$ mm/s. Check whether it meets the **biomechanical limit** for safe contact with the human hand (max tolerable force = **140 N**, equivalent to a stopping distance that absorbs the kinetic energy below threshold).

### 📐 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Calculate kinetic energy.**

$$E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$$

Convert velocity: $v = 250$ mm/s $= 0.25$ m/s.

$$E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 12 \cdot (0.25)^2$$

$$E_k = 6 \cdot 0.0625 = 0.375 \;\text{J}$$

**Step 2 — Convert kinetic energy to equivalent contact force over a deformable cushion of $d_{cushion} = 10$ mm $= 0.01$ m.**

$$F_{eq} = \dfrac{E_k}{d_{cushion}} = \dfrac{0.375}{0.01} = 37.5 \;\text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with biomechanical limit.**

$$F_{eq} = 37.5 \;\text{N} \;<\; F_{biomech,hand} = 140 \;\text{N} \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion**: The cobot's kinetic energy on contact produces a force of **37.5 N**, well below the 140 N hand biomechanical limit. Hence the design **passes** the ISO/TS 15066 PFL safety check. Margin of safety = $(140 - 37.5) / 140 \approx 73.2\%$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Trust Dynamics in HRI (Conceptual)

### 🎯 Problem Statement
A human works with a cobot for 1 hour. During this period, the cobot performs 5 successful handovers (positive events) and 1 minor collision (negative event). Using $\alpha = 0.08$ (positive weight) and $\beta = 0.20$ (distrust weight per failure), compute the net change in trust level.

### 📐 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the trust dynamics equation.**

$$\dfrac{dT}{dt} \approx \alpha \cdot P(t) - \beta \cdot D(t)$$

**Step 2 — Discretize for finite events.**

$$\Delta T = \alpha \cdot n_{success} - \beta \cdot n_{failures}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**

$$\Delta T = (0.08)(5) - (0.20)(1)$$

$$\Delta T = 0.40 - 0.20 = +0.20$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion**: The operator's trust in the robot **increases by 0.20 units** over the hour. This shows that in early-stage HRI, **positive interactions contribute incrementally**, but a *single failure has greater marginal impact* than a single success ($\beta > \alpha$), reflecting the asymmetry seen in real human psychology.

---

## 3.4 KTU Analytical Comparison Matrix — Traditional Robot vs Cobot

| Parameter | Traditional Industrial Robot | Collaborative Robot (Cobot) |
|---|---|---|
| Workspace | **Caged / Isolated** | **Shared with humans** |
| Speed | **Very high** (up to several m/s) | **Limited** (typically $\leq$ 250 mm/s in PFL) |
| Payload | Up to **>1000 kg** | Typically $\leq$ **16 kg** |
| Mass of moving parts | High inertia | **Low inertia** |
| Sensors | Position encoders only | **Force, torque, vision, tactile** |
| Safety strategy | Physical barriers + light curtains | **Inherent safe design + PFL/SSM** |
| Cost (entry-level) | \$50k–\$300k | \$25k–\$75k |
| Programming | Expert-level | **Lead-through / Hand-guiding** |
| Standards | ISO 10218-1/2 | ISO 10218 + **ISO/TS 15066** |
| Stop on human contact | Catastrophic | **Yields in milliseconds** |
| Application | Welding, painting, heavy lifting | Assembly, pick-and-place, polishing, machine tending |

---

## 3.5 KTU Hierarchical Safety Levels (Performance Levels — PL)

| Performance Level (PL) | Probability of Dangerous Failure per Hour | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **PL a** | $\geq 10^{-5}$ to $< 10^{-4}$ | Low-risk auxiliary |
| **PL b** | $\geq 10^{-6}$ to $< 10^{-5}$ | Light guarding |
| **PL c** | $\geq 10^{-6}$ to $< 10^{-5}$ | Standard interlocks |
| **PL d** | $\geq 10^{-7}$ to $< 10^{-6}$ | **Cobot safety functions (typical)** |
| **PL e** | $< 10^{-7}$ to $\geq 10^{-8}$ | Highest safety integrity |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU trick**: Most cobot safety-rated functions (e-stop, force limiting) are certified to **PL d Cat 3**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HRI Functional Architecture — Perception–Cognition–Action Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Human Operator] -->|Approach, gesture, speech| P1[Perception Layer]
    P1 -->|RGB-D Camera| S1[Vision Sensor]
    P1 -->|Joint Torque| S2[Force Sensor]
    P1 -->|Audio| S3[Mic]
    S1 --> C1[Cognition Layer]
    S2 --> C1
    S3 --> C1
    C1 -->|Intent Prediction| ML[ML Models LSTM]
    C1 -->|Tracking| KF[Kalman Filter]
    C1 -->|Trust Update| TM[Trust Model]
    ML --> A1[Action Layer]
    KF --> A1
    TM --> A1
    A1 -->|Motion Plan| MC[Motion Controller]
    A1 -->|Compliance| CC[Impedance Controller]
    A1 -->|Feedback| FB[LED and Voice]
    MC --> R[Robot Actuators]
    CC --> R
    FB --> H
    R -->|Physical Motion| H
```

## 4.2 Cobot Collaborative Operation Modes — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Robot Task Request] --> Q1{Human in Workspace?}
    Q1 -->|No| NM[Non-Collaborative Mode High Speed]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Which Collaborative Mode?}
    Q2 -->|SRMS| M1[Robot Halts Immediately Safety Rated Monitored Stop]
    Q2 -->|HG| M2[Robot Goes Passive Awaits Human Hand Guide]
    Q2 -->|SSM| M3[Robot Slows Based on Separation Distance]
    Q2 -->|PFL| M4[Robot Limits Force and Torque Continuously]
    M1 --> Safe[Safe State]
    M2 --> Safe
    M3 --> Calc{S_p Greater Than Threshold?}
    Calc -->|Yes| Continue[Continue Motion]
    Calc -->|No| M1
    M4 --> ForceCheck{Contact Force Less Than Biomech Limit?}
    ForceCheck -->|Yes| Continue
    ForceCheck -->|No| M1
    Continue --> End[Task Execution]
```

## 4.3 HRI Safety System — Layered Defense Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Layer 0 Inherent Safe Design Lightweight Rounded Compliant] --> L1
    L1[Layer 1 Safety Rated Control Functions PL d] --> L2

    L3[Layer 3 Power and Force Limiting PFL] --> L4

```

```mermaid
    H -->|Proximity| V4[LiDAR and Depth Camera]
    H -->|Physiological| V5[EEG and EMG]

    V2 --> RP
    V3 --> RP
    V4 --> RP
    V5 --> RP

    RA -->|Sound| H

```
