# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 🎯 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define Human-Robot Interaction (HRI). List any two communication modalities used in HRI with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

HRI is an interdisciplinary field that studies the design, evaluation, and understanding of robotic systems operating with or alongside humans. It integrates robotics, cognitive psychology, and human factors engineering.

Two communication modalities:
1. **Verbal communication** — speech recognition, NLP-based command parsing (e.g., "Pick up the red part").
2. **Haptic communication** — force/torque feedback where the robot conveys information through touch or detects human intent via guided motion.

> [!TIP]
> **Marks distribution (KTU pattern)**: 1 mark for definition + 0.5 per modality + 0.5 per example = 3 marks.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What are Collaborative Robots (Cobots)? State any two distinguishing features.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Collaborative Robot (Cobot) is a robot designed for direct physical interaction with humans within a defined collaborative workspace, in compliance with ISO 10218 and ISO/TS 15066 standards.

Two distinguishing features:
1. **Inherent safety design** — rounded edges, lightweight links, low moving inertia.
2. **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** — active electronic limitation of contact forces below biomechanical injury thresholds.

---

## 5.2 🎯 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **four types of collaborative robot operations** as per ISO/TS 15066. For each mode, describe the robot's behaviour and human-robot proximity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cobot arm with effective moving mass **m = 15 kg** operates at maximum speed **v = 200 mm/s** in PFL mode. Compute the kinetic energy and verify whether the contact force is safe with respect to the human hand biomechanical limit of **140 N**, assuming a deformable cushion distance of **d = 12 mm**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Answer for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**The four collaborative operation modes per ISO/TS 15066 are:**

**(1) Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS) — 1.5 marks**
- The robot performs a *safety-rated monitored stop* when a human enters the collaborative workspace.
- The robot remains stationary until the human withdraws; then it auto-resumes.
- Application: Light assembly with intermittent human intervention.

**(2) Hand-Guiding (HG) — 1.5 marks**
- The human physically *grasps* the robot's end-effector and guides it through motions.
- The robot is in a passive, gravity-compensated state.
- Application: Teaching new paths, heavy-lift assistance.

**(3) Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM) — 2 marks**
- The robot dynamically adjusts its speed based on the *separating distance* from the human.
- The minimum protective separation distance $S_p$ must satisfy $S_p \geq v_H \cdot (t_r + t_s) + v_R \cdot t_s + C$.
- Application: Wide workspaces with intermittent close approach.

**(4) Power and Force Limiting (PFL) — 2 marks**
- The robot operates continuously but limits contact *force* and *pressure* below biomechanical thresholds.
- The robot may physically contact the human without causing injury.
- Application: Tight collaboration like polishing, screwing, insertion tasks.

---

#### 📝 Model Answer for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — State the kinetic energy formula.**

$$E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$$

**Step 2 — Convert velocity to SI.**

$$v = 200 \;\text{mm/s} = 0.20 \;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate kinetic energy.**

$$E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 15 \cdot (0.20)^2$$

$$E_k = 7.5 \cdot 0.04 = 0.30 \;\text{J}$$

**Step 4 — Convert kinetic energy to equivalent contact force.**

Using work-energy equivalence over cushion distance:

$$F_{eq} = \dfrac{E_k}{d} = \dfrac{0.30}{0.012} = 25 \;\text{N}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with biomechanical limit.**

$$F_{eq} = 25 \;\text{N} \;<\; F_{limit} = 140 \;\text{N}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion**: The cobot is **safe for hand contact**, with a safety margin of $(140 - 25)/140 \approx 82.1\%$. The design complies with ISO/TS 15066 PFL criteria.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss the **fundamentals of Human-Robot Interaction** with a block diagram of the Perception–Cognition–Action loop. Explain at least one sensor technology and one algorithm per layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM) scenario, a human approaches a cobot at $v_H = 1500$ mm/s. The robot moves toward the human at $v_R = 200$ mm/s. The robot's reaction time is $t_r = 0.08$ s and stopping time is $t_s = 0.25$ s. The intrusion distance is $C = 150$ mm. Calculate the **minimum protective separation distance** $S_p$. If the actual measured distance is 600 mm, determine whether the robot should continue or halt. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Answer for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):** HRI is the study of how humans and robots interact. Modern HRI is built on a three-layered architecture.

**Perception Layer (2 marks):**
- Sensors: RGB-D cameras for *human pose estimation*; force/torque sensors at joints for *contact detection*.
- Example device: Microsoft Kinect / Intel RealSense for skeleton tracking.

**Cognition Layer (2 marks):**
- Algorithms: *Hidden Markov Models* or *LSTMs* for *intent prediction*; *Kalman Filters* for human tracking.
- Output: Predicted future state of human (position, action).

**Action Layer (2 marks):**
- Control: *Impedance control* and *admittance control* for compliant motion.
- Safety: Emergency stop, speed scaling, trajectory replanning.

**Conclusion (0 marks — implicit):** The loop closes as the human perceives the robot's response.

---

#### 📝 Model Answer for Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — State the SSM formula.**

$$S_p = v_H \cdot (t_r + t_s) + v_R \cdot t_s + C$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the human approach contribution.**

$$S_h = v_H \cdot (t_r + t_s) = 1500 \cdot (0.08 + 0.25)$$

$$S_h = 1500 \cdot 0.33 = 495 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the robot stopping distance contribution.**

$$S_s = v_R \cdot t_s = 200 \cdot 0.25 = 50 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step 4 — Add intrusion distance.**

$$S_p = 495 + 50 + 150 = 695 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with actual measured distance.**

$$d_{actual} = 600 \;\text{mm} \;<\; S_p = 695 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Conclusion**: Since $d_{actual} < S_p$, the robot must **halt immediately** via safety-rated monitored stop. Continuing motion would violate the protective separation threshold and risk contact.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students commonly lose marks in HRI Module 4 questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing SRMS with PFL** — SRMS stops the robot *temporarily*; PFL allows *continuous* contact with force limits. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting to state the standard** — Always cite *ISO/TS 15066* for cobot modes and *ISO 10218* for industrial robot safety. Generic answers lose marks.
> 3. **Missing units in numericals** — SSM distances are in **mm**, force in **N**, energy in **J**. A numerical without units can lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping the comparison step** — In numerical answers, always write *explicitly*: $F_{eq} < F_{limit}$ → Safe ✓ (this is what examiners look for).
> 5. **Wrong Jacobian usage in torque limiting** — The formula $\tau_{limit} = J(q)^T F_{max}$ uses the *transpose* of the Jacobian; writing $J(q)$ directly is wrong.
> 6. **Omitting the block diagram in HRI fundamentals** — KTU awards 1–2 marks purely for a *correct, labelled* Perception–Cognition–Action block diagram.

---

## 5.4 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density summary for last-minute KTU revision**

- **HRI** = interdisciplinary field blending robotics + psychology + ergonomics + AI.
- **Three categories**: Remote (teleop), Proximate (shared workspace), Social (companion).
- **Perception–Cognition–Action** loop is the canonical HRI architecture.
- **Cobot** = robot safe for direct human collaboration, designed per **ISO/TS 15066**.
- **Four cobot modes**: SRMS, HG, SSM, PFL — memorize the behaviour table.
- **SSM key equation**: $S_p = v_H(t_r + t_s) + v_R t_s + C$.
- **PFL key check**: $F_{eq} = E_k/d \leq F_{biomech}$ per body region.
- **Biomechanical limits**: Hand 140 N, Chest 210 N, Face 65 N, Skull 130 N.
- **Kinetic energy**: $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$ (SI units).
- **Stopping distance**: $S_s = v_0 t_r + \tfrac{1}{2} a_{brake} t_s^2$.
- **Standards stack**: ISO 12100 → ISO 10218-1/2 → ISO/TS 15066 → ISO 13849-1.
- **Risk = f(Severity, Probability, Frequency)**.
- **Three-step risk reduction**: Inherent design → Safeguarding → Information.
- **Trust dynamics**: Failures have *greater* impact than successes ($\beta > \alpha$).
- **Communication modalities**: Verbal, Non-verbal visual, Haptic, Proximity, Physiological.
- **Performance Levels**: PL a (lowest) → PL e (highest); cobots typically certified **PL d Cat 3**.
- **Cobot examples**: KUKA LBR iiwa, Universal Robots UR5e/UR10e, FANUC CRX, ABB GoFa.
- **Hand-guiding** uses *gravity compensation* + admittance control.
- **SRMS** is the *easiest to implement*, **PFL** is the *most permissive* of all four modes.
- **Intrusion distance $C$** in SSM accounts for *sensor uncertainty* and *human reach error*.
- **History**: Cobot coined by Colgate & Peshkin in **1996** at Northwestern University.
- **Module 4 weightage in KTU ESE**: ~15–20% of total marks; expect 1 Part-A + 1 Part-B question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Operating System (ROS): Advanced ROS Concepts, ROS Packages for Manipulation and Navigation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT865 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Robot Operating System (ROS): Advanced ROS Concepts, ROS Packages for Manipulation and Navigation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is the Robot Operating System (ROS)?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *The Robot Operating System (ROS) is an open-source, meta-operating system for robots that provides hardware abstraction, low-level device control, implementation of commonly-used functionality, message-passing between processes, and package management. It is not a traditional OS, but a framework of libraries and tools running on top of a host operating system (typically Ubuntu Linux).*

ROS is best understood as the **"nervous system + standard communication bus"** of a robot. Just as the human nervous system connects the brain to muscles and sensors through a standardized signaling mechanism, ROS connects perception modules, decision algorithms, and actuation drivers through a **distributed publish/subscribe message-passing middleware**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Control Tower

Imagine a busy international airport:

| Airport Component | ROS Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Air Traffic Control Tower | **ROS Master (roscore)** | Central registry; tracks which flights (nodes) exist, what routes (topics) they publish/subscribe to |
| Pilots in different aircraft | **Nodes** | Independent processes performing specific tasks (read sensor, compute path, send motor command) |
| Radio frequencies | **Topics** | Named buses over which asynchronous, many-to-many data streams flow |
| Scheduled flight requests | **Services** | Synchronous, request/reply RPC calls |
| Long-haul mission updates | **Actions** | Asynchronous, long-duration goal-feedback-result tasks (e.g., "navigate to room B") |
| Ground crew paperwork manifest | **Parameter Server** | Shared, globally readable configuration values |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ROS 1 vs ROS 2 — Syllabus Highlight:**
> KTU 2024 acknowledges both generations. ROS 1 (final LTS: ROS Noetic, 2025) uses XML-RPC + TCPROS. **ROS 2** (Foxy, Humble, Jazzy, Rolling) replaces the master with **DDS (Data Distribution Service)** middleware, supports real-time control, multi-robot systems natively, and is the **industry and academic standard from 2024 onward**. This module primarily focuses on **ROS 1 architecture** for exam purposes, with conceptual references to ROS 2.

---

## 1.2 Why "Advanced" ROS Concepts?

Beginner ROS covers: nodes, topics, services, messages, `catkin`/`colcon` build, simple `roslaunch` files, and basic `rviz` visualization.

**Advanced ROS concepts** in KTU Module 4 escalate to engineering-grade robotics:

1. **TF (Transform) System** — managing time-synchronized, multi-frame spatial relationships.
2. **URDF (Unified Robot Description Format)** — XML robot kinematic specification.
3. **Xacro (XML Macros)** — parameterised URDF generation.
4. **`actionlib`** — pre-emptable, feedback-rich goal protocols.
5. **ROS Bags** — recording and playback of sensor streams for offline debugging.
6. **`rosbag2` & MCAP** — modern container formats (ROS 2).
7. **Multi-Master & Multi-Machine ROS** — distributed deployment.
8. **Lifecycle Nodes** (ROS 2) — managed state machines for safety-critical systems.
9. **Gazebo / Ignition Simulator integration** — physics-aware digital twin.
10. **MoveIt!** — the de-facto manipulation stack.
11. **Navigation2 / Navigation Stack** — the de-facto mobile robot navigation stack.

### The Engineering "Why"

A real mobile manipulator (think a warehouse AMR with a 7-DoF arm) has:
- 1 base mobile platform → needs **navigation**.
- 1 manipulator arm → needs **motion planning & manipulation**.
- 6 cameras + 2 LiDARs + IMU + force/torque → needs **TF, URDF, synchronized sensor fusion**.

None of this is "beginner ROS". Hence Module 4 explicitly elevates to these advanced subsystems.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Glossary (KTU 2024 Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants & Standards to memorize for KTU 2024:**
> - **Publish Rate:** typically **10 Hz** for sensor data, **100 Hz** for motor commands, **1 Hz** for diagnostics.
> - **TF Buffer Length:** default **10 seconds** (configurable via `tf2_buffer`).
> - **DDS QoS Default (ROS 2):** Reliability = RELIABLE, Durability = VOLATILE, History = KEEP_LAST with depth 10.
> - **MoveIt! Planning Group Naming:** declared in SRDF (Semantic Robot Description Format).
> - **Navigation Costmap Resolution:** typically **0.05 m / cell** (2 cm) for indoor AMRs.

### Standard ROS Acronyms (High-Yield)

- **ROS** — Robot Operating System
- **URDF** — Unified Robot Description Format
- **SRDF** — Semantic Robot Description Format
- **TF** — Transform (coordinate frame)
- **TF2** — Second-generation transform library (default since ROS Hydro)
- **DDS** — Data Distribution Service (ROS 2 middleware)
- **SLAM** — Simultaneous Localization and Mapping
- **AMCL** — Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization
- **DWA** — Dynamic Window Approach (local planner)
- **TEB** — Timed Elastic Band (local planner)
- **PID** — Proportional-Integral-Derivative controller
- **IK** — Inverse Kinematics
- **FK** — Forward Kinematics
- **PRM** — Probabilistic Roadmap
- **RRT** — Rapidly-exploring Random Tree
- **STOMP** — Stochastic Trajectory Optimization for Motion Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The TF (Transform) System — Spatial Reasoning Backbone

### 2.1.1 What TF Solves

A robot has many coordinate frames:
- `map` — global, world-fixed
- `odom` — drift-corrected but locally consistent
- `base_link` — robot chassis origin
- `base_laser` — LiDAR sensor mounting point
- `arm_base_link`, `shoulder_link`, `elbow_link`, `wrist_link`, `gripper_link` — arm joints
- `camera_optical_frame` — camera optics origin (Z-forward convention)

Each frame has a **6-DoF pose** (3 position + 3 orientation) relative to its parent in the TF tree. TF maintains a **time-series buffer** of these transforms.

### 2.1.2 TF Tree Topology

ROS enforces a **strict tree** (no cycles): every frame has exactly one parent except `map` (root). Example for a mobile manipulator:

```
map
 └── odom
      └── base_link
           ├── base_laser
           ├── camera_link
           │    └── camera_optical_frame
           └── arm_base_link
                └── shoulder_link
                     └── elbow_link
                          └── wrist_link
                               └── gripper_link
```

### 2.1.3 How TF Lookup Works

A node requests: *"Where is `gripper_link` relative to `map` at time t?"*

TF traverses the tree upward applying homogeneous transforms:

$$
T_{A}^{C} = T_{B}^{C} \cdot T_{A}^{B}
$$

Where $T_{A}^{C}$ is the transform from frame $A$ to frame $C$, and $T_{A}^{B}$ is from $A$ to $B$.

### 2.1.4 TF Broadcast & Listener Pattern (Theory)

**Broadcaster:** a sensor driver publishes $T_{\text{parent}}^{\text{child}}$ at fixed rate $f_{\text{tf}}$.

**Listener:** any node can call `tf2_ros::Buffer.lookupTransform()` to obtain the composite transform at a given timestamp.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `tf2` (not legacy `tf`)?**
> `tf2` is the second-generation library, mandatory since ROS Hydro (2013). It offers:
> - Compiled, template-based (no Python overhead)
> - Built-in interpolation for missing timestamps
> - Thread-safe buffer
> - Python and C++ API parity

---

## 2.2 URDF — Unified Robot Description Format

### 2.2.1 What URDF Encodes

URDF is an **XML file** describing:
- **Visual geometry** (meshes for RViz rendering)
- **Collision geometry** (simpler primitives for physics)
- **Inertial properties** (mass, COM, inertia tensor $I_{xx}, I_{yy}, I_{zz}$)
- **Joints** — revolute, continuous, prismatic, fixed, planar, floating

### 2.2.2 URDF Mathematical Model

Each link is represented by:
- A reference frame
- An inertia tensor in the link's coordinate system

$$
I = \begin{bmatrix} I_{xx} & I_{xy} & I_{xz} \\ I_{xy} & I_{yy} & I_{yz} \\ I_{xz} & I_{yz} & I_{zz} \end{bmatrix}
$$

For a uniform solid cuboid of mass $m$ and dimensions $a \times b \times c$:

$$
I_{xx} = \frac{1}{12} m (b^{2} + c^{2})
$$

$$
I_{yy} = \frac{1}{12} m (a^{2} + c^{2})
$$

$$
I_{zz} = \frac{1}{12} m (a^{2} + b^{2})
$$

### 2.2.3 DH Parameters vs URDF — Important Distinction

| Aspect | URDF | DH Parameters |
|---|---|---|
| Format | XML | Table of 4 parameters per joint |
| Joint axes | Specified as `<axis xyz="...">` | $a, \alpha, d, \theta$ convention |
| Parallel joints | **Not natively supported** (workaround via fixed dummy links) | Requires extension |
| Standard for ROS | ✅ Required by MoveIt! & Gazebo | ❌ Not directly readable by ROS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Frequently Tested:**
> "Differentiate URDF and DH parameters. Why does ROS use URDF instead of DH?" → DH cannot represent branched trees, parallel linkages, or non-kinematic elements like sensors/cameras.

---

## 2.3 Actionlib — Goal-Feedback-Result Protocol

### 2.3.1 Why Actions? (The Gap Between Topics and Services)

- **Topics** → fire-and-forget, no return value (bad for "go to kitchen" goal).
- **Services** → synchronous, blocks caller, no preemption (bad for long tasks).
- **Actions** → asynchronous, pre-emptable, periodic feedback, terminal result.

### 2.3.2 State Machine

An action server cycles through states:

$$
\text{PENDING} \rightarrow \text{ACTIVE} \rightarrow (\text{PREEMPTED} \mid \text{SUCCEEDED} \mid \text{ABORTED})
$$

Five message types per action:
1. **Goal** — sent by client
2. **Cancel** — client requests preemption
3. **Feedback** — periodic status from server
4. **Result** — final outcome (sent once)
5. **Status** — current state (continuously published)

### 2.3.3 The Preemption Equation

If $t_{\text{elapsed}} > t_{\text{max}}$ or obstacle detected, the server issues a `PREEMPTED` transition:

$$
\text{state}_{k+1} = \begin{cases} \text{SUCCEEDED} & \text{if goal achieved} \\ \text{PREEMPTED} & \text{if client.cancel()} \\ \text{ABORTED} & \text{if unrecoverable error} \\ \text{ACTIVE} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

---

## 2.4 ROS Packages for Manipulation — MoveIt!

### 2.4.1 What is MoveIt!?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *MoveIt! is the state-of-the-art ROS software framework for robot manipulation, integrating motion planning, manipulation, 3D perception, kinematics, control, and navigation. It is built around the MoveGroup node and uses the OMPL (Open Motion Planning Library) by default.*

### 2.4.2 MoveIt! Architecture (5 Layers)

1. **MoveGroup Node** — central orchestrator (C++ pluginlib).
2. **Planning Pipeline** — OMPL, CHOMP, STOMP, Pilz industrial planner.
3. **Kinematics Solvers** — KDL, IKFast (analytical), TRAC-IK (robust numerical), BioIK.
4. **Collision Checking** — FCL (Flexible Collision Library) over meshes + primitives.
5. **Execution** — `FollowJointTrajectory` action to the robot controller.

### 2.4.3 Inverse Kinematics — Closed-Form vs Numerical

Closed-form (analytical) IK:

For a 2-link planar arm with link lengths $L_1, L_2$ and end-effector at $(x, y)$:

$$
\theta_2 = \arccos\!\left(\frac{x^{2} + y^{2} - L_1^{2} - L_2^{2}}{2 L_1 L_2}\right)
$$

$$
\theta_1 = \arctan2(y, x) - \arctan2\!\left(L_2 \sin\theta_2,\; L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2\right)
$$

Numerical (Jacobian-based) IK (used by KDL/TRAC-IK):

$$
\Delta q = J^{+}(q)\, \Delta x
$$

Where $J^{+}$ is the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse:

$$
J^{+} = J^{T} (J J^{T})^{-1}
$$

### 2.4.4 Path Planning Algorithms Used by MoveIt!

| Algorithm | Type | Strength |
|---|---|---|
| **RRT (Rapidly-exploring Random Tree)** | Sampling-based | Probabilistically complete, fast for high-DoF |
| **RRT*** | Asymptotically optimal | Converges to optimal as $n \to \infty$ |
| **PRM (Probabilistic Roadmap)** | Multi-query | Build roadmap once, query many times |
| **CHOMP** | Trajectory optimization | Smooth, gradient-based |
| **STOMP** | Stochastic optimization | Robust to local minima |

---

## 2.5 ROS Packages for Navigation — The Navigation Stack

### 2.5.1 Navigation Stack Components

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Core Diagram to memorize — Navigation Stack Topology:**
> 
> `move_base` is the central coordinator. It subscribes to:
> - `/tf` (sensor-to-base transforms)
> - `/odom` (wheel odometry)
> - `/scan`, `/pointcloud` (sensor data)
> - `/map` (static map from `map_server`)
> - `/initialpose`, `/goal` (AMCL & RViz)
> 
> It publishes:
> - `/cmd_vel` (velocity commands to base)
> - Costmap updates

### 2.5.2 Costmap Layers

The costmap_2d package maintains a 2D grid where each cell has an integer cost $c \in \{0, 1, \dots, 254, 255\}$:

- **0** — FREE (green)
- **1–252** — Inflation cost (yellow→red gradient)
- **253** — NO_INFORMATION (gray)
- **254** — LETHAL (obstacle, red)
- **255** — INSCRIBED (inside obstacle)

Inflation formula (radial decay from obstacle):

$$
\text{cost}(d) = \text{cost}_{\max} \cdot \exp(-k \cdot d^{2})
$$

Where $d$ is the distance to the nearest lethal cell and $k$ is a decay constant.

### 2.5.3 Global Planner — Dijkstra / A*

$$
f(n) = g(n) + h(n)
$$

Where:
- $g(n)$ = actual cost from start to node $n$
- $h(n)$ = heuristic estimate from $n$ to goal
- For 4-connected grid: $h(n) = \vert x_n - x_{\text{goal}} \vert + \vert y_n - y_{\text{goal}} \vert$ (Manhattan)
- For 8-connected grid: $h(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_{\text{goal}})^{2} + (y_n - y_{\text{goal}})^{2}}$ (Euclidean)

### 2.5.4 Local Planner — Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)

DWA samples velocities $(v, \omega)$ within a "dynamic window" reachable in one time-step:

$$
v \in [v_{\min},\; v_{\max}]
$$

$$
\omega \in [\omega_{\min},\; \omega_{\max}]
$$

A score is computed for each $(v, \omega)$:

$$
G(v, \omega) = \alpha \cdot \text{heading}(v, \omega) + \beta \cdot \text{clearance}(v, \omega) + \gamma \cdot \text{velocity}(v, \omega)
$$

The $(v^{*}, \omega^{*})$ that maximizes $G$ is published on `/cmd_vel`.

### 2.5.5 AMCL — Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization

AMCL maintains a particle set $\{x^{(i)}, w^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{N}$ representing the posterior belief:

$$
\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \int p(x_t \mid u_{t-1}, x_{t-1})\, \text{bel}(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}
$$

Each particle has a weight updated by sensor likelihood:

$$
w_t^{(i)} = w_{t-1}^{(i)} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)})
$$

Resampling occurs when effective particle count drops:

$$
N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (w^{(i)})^{2}}
$$

If $N_{\text{eff}} < N_{\text{threshold}}$ (typically $0.5 N$), particles are resampled with replacement.

### 2.5.6 SLAM — Simultaneous Localization and Mapping

The classic SLAM posterior:

$$
p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t-1})
$$

Where:
- $x_{1:t}$ — robot trajectory (poses)
- $m$ — map
- $z_{1:t}$ — sensor observations
- $u_{1:t-1}$ — control inputs (odometry)

Common ROS SLAM packages:
- **gmapping** (Grid-based FastSLAM, 2D, particle filter)
- **cartographer** (Google, 2D/3D, graph-based, loop closure via scan matching)
- **hector_slam** (no odometry required, 2D, scan-match only)
- **slam_toolbox** (lifetime mapping, Karto-based, ROS 2 native)

---

## 2.6 ROS 2 Lifecycle Nodes (KTU 2024 Highlight)

Lifecycle nodes enforce explicit state transitions for safety-critical systems:

$$
\text{Unconfigured} \rightarrow \text{Inactive} \rightarrow \text{Active} \rightarrow \text{Finalized}
$$

Transitions are managed via the standard `lifecycle_msgs/srv/ChangeState` and `GetState` services.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Homogeneous Transform | $T \in SE(3) \in \mathbb{R}^{4\times 4}$ | $\det(R) = 1$, $R^{T}R = I$ |
| 2 | TF Composition | $T_{A}^{C} = T_{B}^{C} \cdot T_{A}^{B}$ | Matrix multiplication |
| 3 | Cuboid Inertia Tensor | $I_{xx} = \tfrac{m}{12}(b^{2}+c^{2})$ | $kg \cdot m^{2}$ |
| 4 | 2-Link IK $\theta_2$ | $\theta_2 = \arccos\!\big(\tfrac{x^{2}+y^{2}-L_{1}^{2}-L_{2}^{2}}{2L_{1}L_{2}}\big)$ | radians |
| 5 | 2-Link IK $\theta_1$ | $\theta_1 = \arctan2(y,x) - \arctan2(L_2\sin\theta_2, L_1+L_2\cos\theta_2)$ | radians |
| 6 | Jacobian Pseudoinverse | $J^{+} = J^{T}(JJ^{T})^{-1}$ | Used in numerical IK |
| 7 | A* Search Cost | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Heuristic $h$ must be admissible |
| 8 | Costmap Inflation | $\text{cost}(d) = C_{\max} \cdot e^{-k d^{2}}$ | $d$ in meters |
| 9 | DWA Velocity Score | $G = \alpha\cdot\text{head} + \beta\cdot\text{clear} + \gamma\cdot\text{vel}$ | All weights $\ge 0$ |
| 10 | Particle Weight Update | $w_t^{(i)} = w_{t-1}^{(i)} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)})$ | Sum $\sum w = 1$ after normalize |
| 11 | Effective Sample Size | $N_{\text{eff}} = 1 / \sum_i (w^{(i)})^{2}$ | Resample if $N_{\text{eff}} < 0.5N$ |
| 12 | SLAM Posterior | $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t-1})$ | Joint trajectory-map probability |
| 13 | Quaternion to Euler (ZYX) | $\phi = \arctan2(2(q_0 q_1 + q_2 q_3),\; 1-2(q_1^{2}+q_2^{2}))$ | Roll $\phi$ |
| 14 | PID Controller | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \tfrac{de}{dt}$ | Used in motion control |
| 15 | Publish Rate (sensors) | $f = 10$ Hz typical; $f = 100$ Hz for control | Hz |
| 16 | TF Buffer Length | default $= 10$ seconds | configurable |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Quaternion to Euler Angle Conversion (Roll-Pitch-Yaw)

### Background

URDF and TF broadcast orientation as **quaternions** $(q_x, q_y, q_z, q_w)$ to avoid gimbal lock and enable smooth interpolation (SLERP). However, RViz and human-readable logs prefer **Euler angles** (roll $\phi$, pitch $\theta$, yaw $\psi$).

### Given

A unit quaternion representing rotation:

$$
q = q_w + q_x \mathbf{i} + q_y \mathbf{j} + q_z \mathbf{k}, \quad \text{where } q_w^{2} + q_x^{2} + q_y^{2} + q_z^{2} = 1
$$

The equivalent rotation matrix (in ZYX intrinsic order) is:

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix}
q_w^{2}+q_x^{2}-q_y^{2}-q_z^{2} & 2(q_x q_y - q_w q_z) & 2(q_x q_z + q_w q_y) \\
2(q_x q_y + q_w q_z) & q_w^{2}-q_x^{2}+q_y^{2}-q_z^{2} & 2(q_y q_z - q_w q_x) \\
2(q_x q_z - q_w q_y) & 2(q_y q_z + q_w q_x) & q_w^{2}-q_x^{2}-q_y^{2}+q_z^{2}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 1 — Identify Roll ($\phi$) from $R_{21}$ and $R_{11}$

For ZYX Euler decomposition:

$$
R = R_z(\psi) \cdot R_y(\theta) \cdot R_x(\phi)
$$

Expanding:

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix}
c\psi c\theta & c\psi s\theta s\phi - s\psi c\phi & c\psi s\theta c\phi + s\psi s\phi \\
s\psi c\theta & s\psi s\theta s\phi + c\psi c\phi & s\psi s\theta c\phi - c\psi s\phi \\
-s\theta & c\theta s\phi & c\theta c\phi
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Where $c\psi = \cos\psi$, $s\psi = \sin\psi$, etc.

### Step 2 — Derive $\phi$ (Roll)

From element $R_{31}$:

$$
R_{31} = -\sin\theta
$$

From element $R_{32}$:

$$
R_{32} = \cos\theta \sin\phi
$$

From element $R_{33}$:

$$
R_{33} = \cos\theta \cos\phi
$$

Dividing $R_{32}$ by $R_{33}$:

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{R_{32}}{R_{33}} = \frac{\cos\theta \sin\phi}{\cos\theta \cos\phi}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\phi = \arctan2\!\big(R_{32},\; R_{33}\big)}
$$

### Step 3 — Derive $\theta$ (Pitch)

From $R_{31}$:

$$
R_{31} = -\sin\theta
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\theta = \arcsin(-R_{31}) = -\arcsin(R_{31})}
$$

### Step 4 — Derive $\psi$ (Yaw)

From $R_{11}$:

$$
R_{11} = \cos\psi \cos\theta
$$

From $R_{21}$:

$$
R_{21} = \sin\psi \cos\theta
$$

Dividing $R_{21}$ by $R_{11}$:

$$
\boxed{\psi = \arctan2\!\big(R_{21},\; R_{11}\big)}
$$

### Step 5 — Express in Quaternion Form

Substituting the quaternion rotation matrix elements:

$$
\boxed{\phi = \arctan2\!\big(2(q_w q_x + q_y q_z),\; 1 - 2(q_x^{2} + q_y^{2})\big)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta = \arcsin\!\big(2(q_w q_y - q_z q_x)\big)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\psi = \arctan2\!\big(2(q_w q_z + q_x q_y),\; 1 - 2(q_y^{2} + q_z^{2})\big)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gimbal Lock Warning:**
> When $\theta = \pm \pi/2$, the expression inside $\arcsin$ saturates at $\pm 1$, and roll and yaw become **coupled** — only their sum $\phi + \psi$ is meaningful. This is why ROS **stores quaternions natively** and converts only for display.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Costmap Inflation

### Given

A lethal obstacle at distance $d = 0$. We wish to compute the cost at distance $d > 0$ from the obstacle, decaying smoothly to zero at distance $r_{\text{inscribed}}$.

### Step 1 — Define Boundary Conditions

$$
\text{cost}(0) = C_{\text{lethal}} = 254
$$

$$
\text{cost}(r_{\text{inscribed}}) = 0
$$

We require a smooth, monotonically decreasing function.

### Step 2 — Gaussian Decay Form

Choose a Gaussian:

$$
\text{cost}(d) = C_{\max} \cdot \exp\!\left(-k\, d^{2}\right)
$$

At $d = 0$:

$$
\text{cost}(0) = C_{\max} = 254
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Boundary Condition at $r_{\text{inscribed}}$

$$
\text{cost}(r_{\text{inscribed}}) = 254 \cdot \exp(-k \cdot r_{\text{inscribed}}^{2}) = 0
$$

But a Gaussian never reaches exactly zero — it asymptotically approaches. Therefore ROS uses a **truncated Gaussian**:

$$
\text{cost}(d) = \begin{cases}
254 & d = 0 \\
\text{round}(254 \cdot \exp(-k d^{2})) & 0 < d \le r_{\text{inscribed}} \\
0 & d > r_{\text{inscribed}}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $k$

Using the convention that $\text{cost} = 1$ at $d = r_{\text{inscribed}} / 2$ (midpoint practical decay):

$$
1 = 254 \cdot \exp\!\left(-k \left(\frac{r_{\text{inscribed}}}{2}\right)^{2}\right)
$$

$$
\exp\!\left(-k \frac{r_{\text{inscribed}}^{2}}{4}\right) = \frac{1}{254}
$$

$$
-k \frac{r_{\text{inscribed}}^{2}}{4} = \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{254}\right) = -\ln(254)
$$

$$
k = \frac{4 \ln(254)}{r_{\text{inscribed}}^{2}}
$$

For $r_{\text{inscribed}} = 0.5$ m:

$$
k = \frac{4 \cdot \ln(254)}{0.25} = 16 \cdot 5.537 = 88.59 \; \text{m}^{-2}
$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Verification

At $d = 0.1$ m:

$$
\text{cost}(0.1) = 254 \cdot \exp(-88.59 \cdot 0.01) = 254 \cdot \exp(-0.886) = 254 \cdot 0.412 = 104.6 \approx 105
$$

At $d = 0.25$ m (midpoint):

$$
\text{cost}(0.25) = 254 \cdot \exp(-88.59 \cdot 0.0625) = 254 \cdot \exp(-5.537) = 254 \cdot 0.00394 \approx 1
$$

✅ Matches boundary.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: 2-Link Planar Inverse Kinematics

### Given

A 2R planar manipulator with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$, joint angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$, end-effector position $(x, y)$.

### Step 1 — Forward Kinematics (Trigonometric)

$$
x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

$$
y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

### Step 2 — Compute $x^{2} + y^{2}$

$$
x^{2} + y^{2} = L_1^{2} \cos^{2}\theta_1 + 2L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_1 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) + L_2^{2} \cos^{2}(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

$$
+ L_1^{2} \sin^{2}\theta_1 + 2L_1 L_2 \sin\theta_1 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) + L_2^{2} \sin^{2}(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

Grouping terms:

$$
x^{2} + y^{2} = L_1^{2}(\cos^{2}\theta_1 + \sin^{2}\theta_1) + L_2^{2}(\cos^{2}(\theta_1+\theta_2) + \sin^{2}(\theta_1+\theta_2))
$$

$$
+ 2L_1 L_2[\cos\theta_1 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) + \sin\theta_1 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)]
$$

Using $\cos A \cos B + \sin A \sin B = \cos(A-B)$:

$$
x^{2} + y^{2} = L_1^{2} + L_2^{2} + 2L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $\theta_2$

$$
\cos\theta_2 = \frac{x^{2} + y^{2} - L_1^{2} - L_2^{2}}{2L_1 L_2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta_2 = \arccos\!\left(\frac{x^{2} + y^{2} - L_1^{2} - L_2^{2}}{2L_1 L_2}\right)}
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $\theta_1$

Use the "four-quadrant" inverse tangent:

$$
\theta_1 = \arctan2(y, x) - \arctan2\!\big(L_2 \sin\theta_2,\; L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2\big)
$$

### Step 5 — Two Solutions (Elbow Up / Down)

$$
\theta_2^{(\pm)} = \pm \arccos\!\left(\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}-L_1^{2}-L_2^{2}}{2L_1 L_2}\right)
$$

This corresponds to the **elbow-up** and **elbow-down** configurations.

### Step 6 — Reachability Condition

For a solution to exist:

$$
-1 \le \frac{x^{2}+y^{2}-L_1^{2}-L_2^{2}}{2L_1 L_2} \le 1
$$

Equivalently:

$$
\vert L_1 - L_2 \vert \le \sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}} \le L_1 + L_2
$$

The reachable workspace is an **annulus** of inner radius $\vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$ and outer radius $L_1 + L_2$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: A* Path Optimality

### Setup

A* expands nodes in order of $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$. To guarantee optimality, $h(n)$ must be **admissible** (never overestimates true cost).

### Theorem

If $h(n)$ is admissible, then A* returns the optimal path.

### Proof Sketch

Suppose A* returns path $P$ through goal node $G$, with $f(G) = g(G)$ (since $h(G) = 0$).

Suppose an alternative path $P^*$ exists with true cost $g^*(G) < g(G)$.

At any step, A* picks the lowest $f$. For any unexpanded node $n$:

$$
f(n) = g(n) + h(n) \le g(n) + h^{*}(n) = g(n) + \text{true cost from } n \text{ to } G
$$

Since the true cost from $n$ to $G$ is at most $g^*(G)$:

$$
f(n) \le g^*(G) < g(G)
$$

So A* would have expanded that alternative path node before terminating. Contradiction. ∎

### Implication for ROS Navigation

The default ROS global planner (Navfn) uses **Dijkstra's algorithm** (no heuristic). To accelerate, switch to `global_planner` with `A*` and a consistent heuristic (e.g., Euclidean for 4-connected grids).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Complexity & Properties

| Algorithm | Time Complexity | Space | Completeness | Optimality |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dijkstra | $O(V^{2})$ or $O(E + V \log V)$ with heap | $O(V)$ | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| A* (admissible $h$) | $O(b^{d})$ worst case | $O(b^{d})$ | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| RRT | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | ✅ Probabilistic | ❌ Not optimal |
| RRT* | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | ✅ Asymptotic | ✅ Asymptotic |
| PRM | $O(n \log n)$ build, $O(\log n)$ query | $O(n)$ | ✅ Probabilistic | ❌ Not optimal |
| DWA | $O(V_{samples} \cdot T_{traj})$ | $O(1)$ | ❌ Local minima | ❌ Local optimum |
| AMCL (particle filter) | $O(N \cdot \text{sensor-update})$ | $O(N)$ | — | Bayesian-optimal |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation:** $V$ = number of vertices (grid cells), $b$ = effective branching factor, $d$ = solution depth, $n$ = number of samples, $N$ = particle count.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ROS Master-Node Communication Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Master[ROS MASTER<br/>Parameter Server + Name Service]
    
    Pub1[Camera Driver Node<br/>Publisher]
    Pub2[LiDAR Driver Node<br/>Publisher]
    Pub3[Planner Node<br/>Publisher cmd_vel]
    
    Sub1[Image Viewer Node<br/>Subscriber]
    Sub2[Navigation Node<br/>Subscriber]
    Sub3[Robot Controller Node<br/>Subscriber]
    
    Param[Parameter Server<br/>Shared Configuration]
    
    Topic1[Topic image_raw]
    Topic2[Topic scan]
    Topic3[Topic cmd_vel]
    
    Master -. registers .-> Pub1
    Master -. registers .-> Pub2
    Master -. registers .-> Pub3
    Master -. registers .-> Sub1
    Master -. registers .-> Sub2
    Master -. registers .-> Sub3
    
    Pub1 --> Topic1
    Pub2 --> Topic2
    Pub3 --> Topic3
    
    Topic1 --> Sub1
    Topic2 --> Sub2
    Topic3 --> Sub3
    
    Param -. read/write .-> Pub1
    Param -. read/write .-> Sub2
    Param -. read/write .-> Sub3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Dotted arrows represent **registration / parameter access** with the Master. Solid arrows represent **direct peer-to-peer TCP/UDP data flow** (the Master is *not* in the data path). This is a critical ROS design principle: the Master enables discovery but does **not** relay messages, which is why ROS scales to multi-camera, multi-robot systems efficiently.

---

## 4.2 TF Tree for a Mobile Manipulator

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    map[map<br/>global fixed root]
    odom[odom<br/>locally consistent]
    base[base_link<br/>robot chassis]
    
    laser[base_laser<br/>LiDAR mount]
    imu[imu_link<br/>IMU sensor]
    cam[ camera_link<br/>RGB-D camera]
    optical[camera_optical_frame<br/>Z-forward optics]
    arm[arm_base_link<br/>manipulator origin]
    
    shoulder[shoulder_link]
    elbow[elbow_link]
    wrist[wrist_link]
    gripper[gripper_link]
    
    map --> odom
    odom --> base
    
    base --> laser
    base --> imu
    base --> cam
    base --> arm
    
    cam --> optical
    
    arm --> shoulder
    shoulder --> elbow
    elbow --> wrist
    wrist --> gripper
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strict tree rule:** Note that there are **no cycles** — every frame has exactly one parent. Adding `gripper_link → base_link` would create a cycle and ROS will reject the broadcast with a TF error. Joint state publisher nodes broadcast the `base_link → arm_base_link → shoulder → ... → gripper` chain at typically 100 Hz.

---

## 4.3 MoveIt! Motion Planning Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    User[User Code / MoveGroupInterface]
    
    MG[MoveGroup Node<br/>Central Orchestrator]
    
    Plan[Planning Pipeline<br/>OMPL CHOMP STOMP]
    Kin[Kinematics Solver<br/>KDL IKFast TRAC-IK]
    Coll[Collision Checking<br/>FCL Library]
    Per[Perception Pipeline<br/>Octomap PointCloud]
    
    Ctrl[Trajectory Execution<br/>FollowJointTrajectory Action]
    Robot[Robot Controller / Drivers]
    
    RViz[RViz Plugin<br/>MotionPlanning]
    
    User -->|setGoal| MG
    MG -->|plan| Plan
    MG -->|computeIK| Kin
    MG -->|checkCollision| Coll
    MG -->|sense| Per
    
    Plan --> MG
    Kin --> MG
    Coll --> MG
    Per --> MG
    
    MG -->|execute| Ctrl
    Ctrl --> Robot
    Robot -->|feedback joint states| MG
    
    RViz -->|interactive markers| MG
    MG -->|display planned path| RViz
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Bidirectional arrows** indicate the feedback loop: the robot publishes its current joint states back to MoveIt, enabling collision-aware replanning if the environment changes mid-motion.

---

## 4.4 ROS Navigation Stack Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Goal[User Goal Topic move_base_simple/goal]
    
    MB[move_base Node<br/>Central Coordinator]
    
    GP[Global Planner<br/>Navfn A Star Dijkstra]
    LP[Local Planner<br/>DWA TEB MPC]
    
    CMG[Global Costmap costmap_2d]
    CML[Local Costmap costmap_2d]
    
    SLAM[SLAM Node<br/>gmapping or cartographer]
    AMCL[AMCL Localization Node]
    MS[map_server<br/>Static Map]
    
    Sensors[Sensor Sources<br/>scan pointcloud odom tf]
    Robot[Robot Base<br/>cmd_vel driver]
    
    Goal --> MB
    
    MB --> GP
    MB --> LP
    MB --> CMG
    MB --> CML
    
    GP -->|global path| MB
    LP -->|velocity commands| MB
    
    CMG --> MB
    CML --> MB
    
    SLAM --> AMCL
    MS --> AMCL
    Sensors --> SLAM
    Sensors --> AMCL
    Sensors --> CMG
    Sensors --> CML
    
    MB -->|cmd_vel| Robot
    Robot -->|odometry| Sensors
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** The `move_base` node does **not** own a planner — it composes plug-ins. Swapping the global planner from Navfn to `global_planner/A*` is a single launch-file parameter change. Swapping the local planner from DWA to TEB is similarly modular. This **pluginlib architecture** is a key design strength of ROS.

---

## 4.5 Lifecycle Node State Machine (ROS 2)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Unconfigured
    Unconfigured --> Inactive: configure
    Inactive --> Active: activate
    Active --> Inactive: deactivate
    Inactive --> Unconfigured: cleanup
    Active --> Finalized: shutdown
    Inactive --> Finalized: shutdown
    Unconfigured --> Finalized: shutdown
    Finalized --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why lifecycle?** A sensor driver for a safety-critical robot should not silently start publishing while still calibrating. The lifecycle pattern forces the system manager to **explicitly** transition through Unconfigured → Inactive → Active, with `activate()` only called after all preconditions are verified. This is mandatory for ISO 10218 (industrial robot safety) and ISO 13482 (personal care robot) compliance.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Mark Distribution Summary (Module 4 — ROS):**
> - **Part A (3 marks):** Definition / short conceptual — *Bloom's Level: Remember/Understand*
> - **Part B (14 marks):** Internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**, each split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks — *Bloom's Level: Apply/Analyze/Evaluate*
> - Total question-paper weight: 14 marks from this module cluster.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between ROS topics, services, and actions. Give one robotics use-case for each.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

| Feature | Topics | Services | Actions |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pattern | Publish/Subscribe | Request/Reply | Goal/Feedback/Result |
| Direction | Many-to-many | One-to-one | One-to-one |
| Synchronous? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ❌ Asynchronous |
| Pre-emptable? | ❌ N/A | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Feedback? | ❌ Continuous stream | ❌ Single reply | ✅ Periodic feedback |

**Use-cases:**
1. **Topic** — `/scan` (LiDAR publishes at 10 Hz; multiple nodes subscribe: AMCL, costmap, RViz).
2. **Service** — `/get_plan` (request a path, receive it, then execute separately).
3. **Action** — `/move_base` (send a goal, receive periodic feedback, can cancel mid-flight).

> [!NOTE]
> *Award 1 mark for correct differentiation table, 1 mark for any 2 features explained, 1 mark for valid robotics use-cases. Examiners expect keywords: "asynchronous, pre-emptable, feedback-rich" for actions.*

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is URDF? Mention any four key tags used inside a `<link>` element with their purpose.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

**URDF (Unified Robot Description Format)** is an XML representation of a robot's kinematic and dynamic model, including links, joints, inertial properties, visual geometry, and collision geometry. It is consumed by RViz (visualization), Gazebo (physics simulation), and MoveIt! (motion planning).

**Four key tags inside `<link>`:**

| Tag | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `<visual>` | Defines how the link is rendered in RViz (mesh, color, material) |
| `<collision>` | Defines simplified geometry for physics/collision checking |
| `<inertial>` | Specifies mass, center of mass, and inertia tensor (for dynamics) |
| `<inertial><mass value="..."/>` | Mass of the link in kg |

*(Other acceptable tags: `<origin>`, `<geometry>` inside `<visual>` or `<collision>`.)*

> [!NOTE]
> *Award 1 mark for correct URDF definition, 2 marks for 4 valid tags with purpose. Students frequently lose marks by listing tags like `<joint>` which belong **outside** `<link>` — read the question carefully.*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Each 14-mark question is internally split: (a) 7 marks, (b) 7 marks. Examiners expect detailed working, labelled diagrams, and clear step-by-step reasoning. Refer to the mermaid schematics in SECTION 4.**

---

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

#### (a) Explain the TF (Transform) system in ROS with a suitable mobile-robot example. Describe how the TF tree is structured and how a transform lookup query is resolved. (7 marks)

#### Model Answer:

**Definition (1 mark):**
The TF system is a distributed, time-synchronized library that maintains the spatial relationship between all coordinate frames in a robot system as a **tree structure**, allowing any node to query the pose of any frame relative to any other frame at any past timestamp up to the buffer length (default **10 s**).

**Tree Structure (2 marks):**
For a differential-drive mobile robot with a LiDAR:
- Root: `map` (global, static)
- Child: `odom` (drift-free, fed by AMCL)
- Child: `base_link` (chassis origin, fed by wheel odometry)
- Children of `base_link`: `base_laser` (LiDAR mount), `wheel_left_link`, `wheel_right_link`, `imu_link`

**Lookup Resolution (2 marks):**
To find `base_laser` in `map` at time $t$, TF:
1. Walks up: $T_{\text{odom}}^{\text{map}}(t)$ and $T_{\text{base}}^{\text{odom}}(t)$.
2. Computes $T_{\text{base}}^{\text{map}}(t) = T_{\text{odom}}^{\text{map}}(t) \cdot T_{\text{base}}^{\text{odom}}(t)$.
3. Walks down: $T_{\text{laser}}^{\text{base}}(t)$ from joint state.
4. Composes: $T_{\text{laser}}^{\text{map}}(t) = T_{\text{base}}^{\text{map}}(t) \cdot T_{\text{laser}}^{\text{base}}(t)$.

**Broadcast Mechanism (1 mark):**
The `robot_state_publisher` node subscribes to `/joint_states` and broadcasts TF at 100 Hz. The wheel odometry node broadcasts $T_{\text{base}}^{\text{odom}}$. The AMCL node broadcasts $T_{\text{odom}}^{\text{map}}$.

**Tooling (1 mark):**
- `rosrun tf2_tools view_frames.py` generates a PDF of the entire tree.
- `rosrun tf tf_echo base_laser map` live-prints the transform.

#### (b) Explain the URDF kinematic description with a simple 2-link revolute robot. Write the URDF XML skeleton and explain how MoveIt! consumes this. (7 marks)

#### Model Answer:

**Conceptual Setup (1 mark):**
A 2-link planar arm has 2 links (`link1`, `link2`) and 2 revolute joints (`joint1` between `base_link ↔ link1`, `joint2` between `link1 ↔ link2`).

**URDF XML Skeleton (3 marks):**

```xml
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<robot name="two_link_arm">
  <link name="base_link">
    <visual>
      <geometry><box size="0.1 0.1 0.1"/></geometry>
    </visual>
    <collision>
      <geometry><box size="0.1 0.1 0.1"/></geometry>
    </collision>
    <inertial>
      <mass value="1.0"/>
      <inertia ixx="0.01" iyy="0.01" izz="0.01" ixy="0" ixz="0" iyz="0"/>
    </inertial>
  </link>

  <link name="link1">
    <visual><geometry><cylinder length="1.0" radius="0.05"/></geometry></visual>
    <collision><geometry><cylinder length="1.0" radius="0.05"/></geometry></collision>
    <inertial>
      <mass value="0.5"/>
      <inertia ixx="0.01" iyy="0.01" izz="0.005" ixy="0" ixz="0" iyz="0"/>
    </inertial>
  </link>

  <joint name="joint1" type="revolute">
    <parent link="base_link"/>
    <child link="link1"/>
    <origin xyz="0 0 0.05" rpy="0 0 0"/>
    <axis xyz="0 0 1"/>
    <limit lower="-3.14" upper="3.14" effort="10" velocity="1"/>
  </joint>

  <link name="link2">
    <visual><geometry><cylinder length="0.8" radius="0.04"/></geometry></visual>
    <collision><geometry><cylinder length="0.8" radius="0.04"/></geometry></collision>
    <inertial>
      <mass value="0.3"/>
      <inertia ixx="0.005" iyy="0.005" izz="0.003" ixy="0" ixz="0" iyz="0"/>
    </inertial>
  </link>

  <joint name="joint2" type="revolute">
    <parent link="link1"/>
    <child link="link2"/>
    <origin xyz="0 0 1.0" rpy="0 0 0"/>
    <axis xyz="0 0 1"/>
    <limit lower="-3.14" upper="3.14" effort="10" velocity="1"/>
  </joint>
</robot>
```

**MoveIt! Consumption (3 marks):**
1. **Setup Assistant** (`rosrun moveit_setup_assistant moveit_setup_assistant`) loads this URDF.
2. Generates an **SRDF** (Semantic Robot Description Format) defining planning groups (e.g., `arm_group = link1, link2`).
3. MoveIt! launches:
   - `move_group` node (planning server)
   - `robot_state_publisher` (TF from `/joint_states`)
   - `rviz` with `MotionPlanning` plugin
4. OMPL planners (RRT, RRT*, PRM) consume the SRDF + URDF to compute collision-free trajectories.
5. Executed trajectories are sent via `FollowJointTrajectory` action to the `ros_control` hardware interface.

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Explain the ROS Navigation Stack in detail with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of move_base, global planner, local planner, and costmap. (7 marks)

#### Model Answer:

**Overview (1 mark):**
The ROS Navigation Stack is a 2D mobile-robot navigation framework that takes a goal pose, a static map, sensor data, and odometry as inputs, and outputs safe velocity commands (`/cmd_vel`) to drive the robot to the goal while avoiding obstacles.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** *(Refer to SECTION 4.4 Mermaid diagram)*

**`move_base` Node (1 mark):**
The central coordinator. It instantiates **one global planner** (e.g., `navfn/NavfnROS` or `global_planner/GlobalPlanner`) and **one local planner** (e.g., `dwa_local_planner/DWAPlannerROS` or `teb_local_planner/TebLocalPlannerROS`). It also maintains **two costmaps** (global + local).

**Global Planner Role (1 mark):**
Plans a **long-horizon, complete** path from start to goal using the static map. Default algorithm: Dijkstra. Alternative: A* with Euclidean heuristic. Output: a sequence of poses (`nav_msgs/Path`).

**Local Planner Role (1 mark):**
Executes the global path **incrementally** while avoiding dynamic obstacles not on the static map. DWA samples $(v, \omega)$ pairs, simulates trajectories, scores them on heading + clearance + velocity, and selects the optimum. Output: `(linear_x, angular_z)` at 20 Hz.

**Costmap Role (1 mark):**
A 2D occupancy grid maintained by `costmap_2d`. Layers include:
- **Static map layer** (from `map_server`).
- **Obstacle layer** (sensor data inflated by robot radius).
- **Inflation layer** (Gaussian decay: $\text{cost}(d) = 254 e^{-k d^{2}}$).

#### (b) Describe AMCL and SLAM packages in ROS. Differentiate between gmapping, cartographer, and hector_slam. (7 marks)

#### Model Answer:

**AMCL — Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization (2 marks):**
AMCL localizes the robot in a known map using a **particle filter**. Each particle is a hypothesis $(x, y, \theta)$ with weight $w$. On every scan:
1. Predict particles using odometry.
2. Score each particle by scan-matching against the static map: $w_i \propto p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)})$.
3. Compute $N_{\text{eff}} = 1/\sum w_i^{2}$; resample if $N_{\text{eff}} < N_{\text{threshold}}$.
4. Publish mean pose as `amcl_pose`.

**SLAM Packages (5 marks):**

| Feature | gmapping | cartographer | hector_slam |
|---|---|---|---|
| Algorithm | Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter (Grid FastSLAM) | Graph-based scan matching with loop closure | Scan-match only (no filter) |
| Odometry required? | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (but recommended) | ❌ No |
| Loop closure? | ❌ Limited | ✅ Yes (via CSM + sparse pose graph) | ❌ No |
| Output | 2D occupancy grid | 2D + 3D maps + serialized `.pbstream` | 2D occupancy grid |
| Compute cost | Low | High (GPU recommended for 3D) | Very low |
| ROS 1 node | `gmapping` (`openslam_gmapping`) | `cartographer_node` | `hector_mapping` |
| Best for | Indoor 2D mapping | Large indoor + outdoor, multi-session | Lightweight drones, no odom available |

**Key Distinctions (additional marks):**
- `gmapping` cannot re-localize after kidnapping (no loop closure); `cartographer` can via SPA (Sparse Pose Adjustment).
- `hector_slam` is **odometry-free** — uses LiDAR scan-matching (Gauss-Newton iteration on the occupancy grid) — ideal for aerial vehicles with poor wheel odometry.
- `cartographer` produces both `.pbstream` (trajectory graph) and `.pgm` (rasterized map), enabling **lifelong mapping** via `cartographer_pbstream_to_rosbag`.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Leading to Mark Deductions:**
> 
> 1. **Topics vs Services confusion (–1 mark):** Writing "services can be subscribed" loses a mark. Topics are subscribed, services are *called* by a client.
> 
> 2. **TF tree cycle (–2 marks):** Drawing a bidirectional arrow between `base_link` and `arm_base_link` in a TF diagram is wrong. TF is a strict **tree**, not a graph.
> 
> 3. **URDF `<joint>` tags inside `<link>` (–2 marks):** Joints and links are **siblings** in URDF, not nested. Examiners will mark this as a structural error.
> 
> 4. **Costmap units forgotten (–1 mark):** Writing "cost = 254 × e^(-kd)" without specifying $d$ is in meters and $k$ in $\text{m}^{-2}$ loses 1 mark.
> 
> 5. **AMCL vs SLAM confusion (–2 marks):** AMCL **localizes** in a known map. SLAM **builds** the map. They are different problems. Writing "AMCL does SLAM" is incorrect.
> 
> 6. **MoveIt! = IK only (–2 marks):** MoveIt! is a **full manipulation framework** (planning + kinematics + perception + execution), not just an IK solver. Reducing it to "computes joint angles" loses descriptive marks.
> 
> 7. **Forgetting `tf2` vs legacy `tf`** (–1 mark): Since 2013, the standard is **tf2**. Writing plain "tf" without "tf2" may lose 1 mark in up-to-date question papers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### Core Architecture
- ✅ ROS = **distributed publish/subscribe middleware**, not a true OS.
- ✅ **ROS 1** uses a central Master (`roscore`); **ROS 2** uses **DDS** (no Master, true peer-to-peer).
- ✅ Three communication patterns: **topics** (async stream), **services** (sync RPC), **actions** (preemptable goal with feedback).

### TF System
- ✅ TF is a **tree** (no cycles), root = `map`, default buffer = **10 s**.
- ✅ Each transform is a $4\times 4$ homogeneous matrix $T \in SE(3)$.
- ✅ Always use `tf2` (not legacy `tf`); use `tf2_ros::Buffer.lookupTransform()`.

### URDF
- ✅ URDF = XML kinematic + dynamic description.
- ✅ Tags: `<link>`, `<joint>`, `<visual>`, `<collision>`, `<inertial>`.
- ✅ URDF cannot natively represent parallel linkages (use dummy links).

### MoveIt!
- ✅ Central node = `move_group`; default planner = **OMPL (RRT/RRT*)**.
- ✅ Kinematics solvers: KDL, IKFast, TRAC-IK.
- ✅ Collision checking via **FCL**; planning scene managed via PlanningSceneMonitor.
- ✅ Communication with robot = `FollowJointTrajectory` **action**.

### Navigation Stack
- ✅ Core node = **`move_base`**; depends on `/tf`, `/odom`, `/scan`, `/map`, `/cmd_vel`.
- ✅ **Costmap values**: 0 = free, 254 = lethal, 255 = inscribed.
- ✅ Global planners: Navfn (Dijkstra), `global_planner` (A*).
- ✅ Local planners: DWA, TEB, MPC.
- ✅ SLAM: `gmapping` (2D particle), `cartographer` (graph-based, loop closure), `hector_slam` (no odom).
- ✅ AMCL = particle filter for localization in known map; resample when $N_{\text{eff}} < 0.5N$.

### ROS 2
- ✅ DDS middleware (default: Cyclone DDS or Fast DDS).
- ✅ **Lifecycle nodes** for safety-critical state management.
- ✅ Default QoS: RELIABLE + VOLATILE + KEEP_LAST(10).
- ✅ Build system = `colcon` (replaces `catkin`).

### High-Yield Formulas
- ✅ TF composition: $T_A^C = T_B^C \cdot T_A^B$
- ✅ Costmap inflation: $\text{cost}(d) = 254 e^{-k d^{2}}$
- ✅ A*: $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ (with admissible heuristic)
- ✅ 2-link IK: $\theta_2 = \arccos\!\big(\tfrac{x^2+y^2-L_1^2-L_2^2}{2L_1 L_2}\big)$
- ✅ Cuboid inertia: $I_{xx} = \tfrac{m}{12}(b^2 + c^2)$
- ✅ AMCL ESS: $N_{\text{eff}} = 1 / \sum_i (w^{(i)})^2$

### Numbers Worth Memorizing
- ✅ Publish rate: **10 Hz** sensors, **100 Hz** motor control.
- ✅ TF buffer: **10 s**.
- ✅ Costmap resolution: **0.05 m**.
- ✅ Default particle count (AMCL): **5000**.
- ✅ OMPL planning time default: **5 s**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Future Trends in Robotics and Automation: Autonomous Systems, Robotics in Healthcare, AI-Driven Automation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT865 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Future Trends in Robotics and Automation: Autonomous Systems, Robotics in Healthcare, AI-Driven Automation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Autonomous Systems — The Self-Thinking Machine

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
An **Autonomous System (AS)** is a robotic or cyber-physical system capable of perceiving its environment, reasoning about dynamic situations, and executing goal-directed actions **without continuous human intervention**. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme, autonomy is graded along the **Levels of Autonomy (LoA)**, ranging from *Level 0 (Remote-Controlled)* to *Level 5 (Full Autonomy)*, as adapted from the SAE J3016 and ISO 13482 frameworks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU 2024 Scheme, autonomy is **not** synonymous with automation. *Automation* implies rule-based repetition; *Autonomy* implies adaptive decision-making under uncertainty. This distinction is a guaranteed **3-mark question** territory.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Self-Driving Car" Intuition
Imagine a student riding a bicycle through the KTU campus. The student's eyes (sensors) detect a speed-breaker; the brain (AI planner) decides to slow down and steer left; the hands and feet (actuators) execute the maneuver. The student does not need a coach standing beside them — they **perceive, decide, and act** independently. An autonomous robot does exactly this, but with **LiDAR, cameras, deep neural networks, and motor controllers** instead of eyes, brain, and limbs.

### The Three Pillars of Autonomy
| Pillar | Technical Term | Human Analogy | KTU Exam Keyword |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sensing** | Perception Stack | Eyes, Ears, Skin | Sensor Fusion |
| **Reasoning** | Cognitive Planning Engine | Brain (Cerebrum) | Decision Theory |
| **Acting** | Actuation & Control Loop | Hands, Legs, Muscles | Motion Control |

> [!NOTE]
> The benchmark **autonomy metric** is the **"Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)"** expressed in hours, and the **operational design domain (ODD)** boundary, beyond which the system must request human handover.

---

## 1.2 Robotics in Healthcare — The Precision Partner

### Formal Definition
**Medical / Healthcare Robotics** refers to the integrated application of robotic manipulators, computer vision, haptic feedback, and AI algorithms to **assist, augment, or replace** clinical functions such as surgery, rehabilitation, diagnosis, and patient care. The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies these into **three operational classes**:
1. **Surgical Robotics** — Invasive and Minimally Invasive (e.g., Da Vinci Surgical System).
2. **Rehabilitation & Assistive Robotics** — Exoskeletons, prosthetics, mobility aids.
3. **Service & Social Robotics** — Hospital logistics, elderly care, disinfecting robots.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Surgeon's Third Hand"
Think of a surgeon performing delicate eye surgery. Their hands, however steady, have a natural micro-tremor of about **50–100 micrometers**. A surgical robot acts as a *mechanical extension* of the surgeon's hands — it **filters tremor**, scales down motion (a 1 cm hand movement becomes a 0.1 cm tool tip movement), and provides a magnified 3D view. The surgeon still makes every decision; the robot simply **executes with superhuman precision**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The **"Master–Slave Teleoperation"** architecture is the cornerstone of surgical robotics. The *Master* (console) sends commands, the *Slave* (patient-side manipulator) executes them. A typical **communication latency budget** is **≤ 200 ms** to maintain haptic fidelity.

### Safety & Performance Metrics
| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Positional Accuracy | $\epsilon_p$ | **± 0.1 mm** | Surgical precision |
| Haptic Force Resolution | $F_{res}$ | **0.25 N** | Tissue feedback fidelity |
| Repeatability | $R_{6\sigma}$ | **≤ 0.05 mm** | ISO 9283 standard |
| Mean Time to Failure | MTTF | **≥ 10,000 hr** | Clinical reliability |

---

## 1.3 AI-Driven Automation — The Cognitive Layer

### Formal Definition
**AI-Driven Automation** is the integration of **Artificial Intelligence (AI)** sub-fields — Machine Learning (ML), Deep Learning (DL), Natural Language Processing (NLP), and Computer Vision (CV) — into automated industrial and service processes to enable **self-optimization, predictive maintenance, and adaptive quality control**. In the KTU 2024 context, this represents the transition from **Industry 4.0** (connected cyber-physical systems) to **Industry 5.0** (human–machine collaboration).

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Factory Apprentice"
A traditional CNC machine is like a worker who **only follows a fixed recipe** written on paper. An AI-driven automated cell, on the other hand, is like an apprentice who **watches, learns, and improvises**. If a vibration pattern on a spindle changes, the AI predicts a bearing failure 48 hours in advance and reschedules maintenance — *before* a breakdown halts production. The apprentice gets *smarter with every part produced*.

> [!NOTE]
> The **"Closed-Loop Optimization Cycle"** in AI automation is built on the classical **Sense → Analyze → Decide → Act** paradigm, augmented with a **Learn** stage — turning the loop into a *spiral* that improves over time.

### Core AI Sub-Fields in Robotics (KTU High-Yield)
- **Machine Learning (ML):** Supervised, Unsupervised, Reinforcement Learning.
- **Deep Learning (DL):** CNNs for vision, RNNs/LSTMs for time-series, Transformers for multimodal fusion.
- **Reinforcement Learning (RL):** Agent learns optimal policy $\pi^*(s) \to a$ via reward maximization.
- **Digital Twin:** Virtual replica of the physical robot for simulation and what-if analysis.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not confuse *AI-Driven Automation* with *Hard Automation*. Hard automation is rigid (e.g., a dedicated welding line); AI-driven automation is **adaptive and data-driven** (e.g., a vision-guided pick-and-place cell that handles SKU variation automatically).

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of an Autonomous System

A fully autonomous robotic system follows a hierarchical **4-Layer Cognitive Architecture**:

### Layer 1 — Perception (The Sensor Layer)
- **Sensors Used:** LiDAR, RGB-D Cameras, IMU, GPS, Radar, Ultrasonic, Tactile.
- **Function:** Raw data acquisition and feature extraction.
- **Core Algorithm:** **Sensor Fusion** — combines heterogeneous sensor streams into a unified world model.

### Layer 2 — Localization & Mapping (The Spatial Awareness Layer)
- **Core Algorithm:** **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)**.
- **Variants:** EKF-SLAM, FastSLAM, Graph-SLAM, ORB-SLAM3, LIO-SAM.

### Layer 3 — Planning & Reasoning (The Cognitive Layer)
- **Path Planning:** A*, Dijkstra, RRT (Rapidly-exploring Random Tree), RRT*.
- **Decision Making:** Markov Decision Process (MDP), Partially Observable MDP (POMDP).
- **Behavior Arbitration:** Subsumption architecture, Behavior Trees.

### Layer 4 — Control & Actuation (The Motor Layer)
- **Control Laws:** PID, Model Predictive Control (MPC), Adaptive Control.
- **Actuators:** BLDC motors, servo drives, hydraulic/pneumatic systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry 4.0 → 5.0 Connection:** The KTU 2024 scheme stresses the **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** integration where these four layers are mirrored by a **digital twin** in the cloud for offline training and online monitoring.

---

## 2.2 Healthcare Robotics — Deep Taxonomy

### 2.2.1 Surgical Robotics
- **Master–Slave Teleoperation** with **6+ DOF** (Degrees of Freedom) instruments.
- **Haptic Feedback Loop:** Force reflection from slave to master surgeon.
- **Kinematic Mapping:** Hand motion (master) → scaled tool motion (slave).
- **Scale Factor:** Typically **3:1 to 10:1** (hand movement : tool movement).

### 2.2.2 Rehabilitation Robotics
- **Exoskeletons:** Wearable robots for gait assistance (e.g., Ekso GT, ReWalk).
- **End-Effector Therapy:** Robot moves patient's limb through predefined trajectories.
- **Performance Metric:** **Barthel Index improvement** post-therapy.

### 2.2.3 Assistive & Social Robotics
- **Prosthetics:** Myoelectric arms using EMG (Electromyography) signals.
- **Social Robots:** PARO (seal pup for dementia therapy), Pepper (humanoid companion).
- **Hospital Logistics:** TUG robot for medicine/meal delivery (Aethon TUG).

---

## 2.3 AI-Driven Automation Frameworks

### 2.3.1 Reinforcement Learning (RL) Framework
- **Agent** interacts with **Environment** via **Actions**, receiving **Rewards**.
- **Goal:** Learn optimal policy $\pi^*$ maximizing cumulative discounted reward.

### 2.3.2 Digital Twin Architecture
- **Three Components:** Physical Asset, Digital Replica, Bidirectional Data Link.
- **Use Cases:** Predictive maintenance, virtual commissioning, operator training.

### 2.3.3 Human–Robot Collaboration (Cobots)
- **ISO/TS 15066** defines force and pressure limits for collaborative operations.
- **Power and Force Limiting (PFL):** Max **quasi-static contact force = 140 N**; max **transient contact = 280 N**.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bellman Optimality Equation for Autonomous Path Planning

We begin with the **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** framework, which is the formal mathematical backbone of autonomous decision-making in modern robotics.

### Step 1 — Define the MDP Tuple
An MDP is defined as the tuple:
$$M = \langle S, A, P, R, \gamma \rangle$$

where:
- $S$ = finite set of **states** the robot can be in.
- $A$ = finite set of **actions** available to the robot.
- $P(s' \vert s, a)$ = transition probability of moving from state $s$ to $s'$ when action $a$ is taken.
- $R(s, a, s')$ = **immediate reward** received after the transition.
- $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ = **discount factor** that prioritizes immediate over delayed rewards.

### Step 2 — Define the Return
The total discounted return from time $t$ is defined as:

$$G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma r_{t+2} + \gamma^2 r_{t+3} + \ldots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1}$$

**Conversion Logic:** This equation says that the value of a future reward is exponentially less valuable the further away in time it occurs. For example, with $\gamma = 0.99$, a reward received 10 steps later is worth $0.99^{10} \approx 0.904$ of its face value.

### Step 3 — Define the State-Value Function
The expected return when starting in state $s$ and following policy $\pi$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^{\pi}(s) &= \mathbb{E}_{\pi} [G_t \vert S_t = s] \\
&= \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1} \,\Big\vert\, S_t = s \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** This converts the random return $G_t$ into an *expected* value across all possible trajectories the agent might take.

### Step 4 — Expand the Expectation (Bellman Expectation)
We unroll the sum by one step to obtain the **recursive Bellman equation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^{\pi}(s) &= \mathbb{E}_{\pi} [r_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \vert S_t = s] \\
&= \sum_{a} \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_{t+1} \vert S_{t+1} = s'] \right] \\
&= \sum_{a} \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V^{\pi}(s') \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** In line 1, we split the return into the immediate reward and the discounted continuation. In line 2, we apply the law of total expectation: first over actions (weighted by policy $\pi$) and then over next states (weighted by transition probability $P$). In line 3, we recognize that the expected continuation from $s'$ is, by definition, $V^{\pi}(s')$.

### Step 5 — Derive the Bellman Optimality Equation
We now seek the *optimal* policy $\pi^*$ that maximizes $V^{\pi}(s)$ for every state. Define:

$$V^{*}(s) = \max_{\pi} V^{\pi}(s)$$

Replacing the policy-weighted sum with a maximization over actions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^{*}(s) &= \max_{a} \mathbb{E} [r_{t+1} + \gamma V^{*}(S_{t+1}) \vert S_t = s, A_t = a] \\
&= \max_{a} \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V^{*}(s') \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** At the optimal policy, the agent always *chooses* the action that yields the highest expected long-term value. This is a **maximization** instead of a weighted average.

### Step 6 — Convert to Q-Function (Action-Value)
We can also express optimality in terms of the action-value function $Q^{*}(s, a)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^{*}(s, a) &= \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a') \right] \\
V^{*}(s) &= \max_{a} Q^{*}(s, a)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** $Q^{*}(s, a)$ is the value of *taking a specific action* $a$ in state $s$ and acting optimally thereafter. The maximum of $Q$ over all actions gives the state value.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Kalman Filter for Sensor Fusion (Healthcare Robot Localization)

The Kalman Filter recursively estimates the state of a linear dynamic system from noisy sensor measurements — used in surgical robots to track instrument tip position.

### Step 1 — Define the Linear System
$$x_k = A x_{k-1} + B u_{k-1} + w_{k-1}, \quad w \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)$$

$$z_k = H x_k + v_k, \quad v \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$$

where $Q$ = process noise covariance, $R$ = measurement noise covariance.

### Step 2 — Prediction Step (Time Update)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} &= A \hat{x}_{k-1 \vert k-1} + B u_{k-1} \\
P_{k \vert k-1} &= A P_{k-1 \vert k-1} A^T + Q
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The state estimate is projected forward using the motion model $A$, and the uncertainty $P$ grows by $Q$ because the process itself is noisy.

### Step 3 — Update Step (Measurement Update)
$$
\begin{aligned}
K_k &= P_{k \vert k-1} H^T \left( H P_{k \vert k-1} H^T + R \right)^{-1} \\
\hat{x}_{k \vert k} &= \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} + K_k \left( z_k - H \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} \right) \\
P_{k \vert k} &= (I - K_k H) P_{k \vert k-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The Kalman Gain $K_k$ balances trust between the prediction ($H P H^T$ term) and the measurement ($R$). When $R \to 0$ (sensor is very accurate), $K_k \to H^{-1}$ and we trust the measurement. When $R \to \infty$ (noisy sensor), $K_k \to 0$ and we trust the prediction.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: AI vs. Classical Automation

| Criterion | Classical Automation | AI-Driven Automation | Healthcare Equivalent |
|---|---|---|---|
| Decision Logic | Rule-based (IF-THEN) | Data-driven (Neural Nets) | Pre-op checklist vs. ML triage |
| Adaptability | **Low** (reprogram needed) | **High** (online learning) | Fixed protocol vs. adaptive surgery |
| Data Requirement | Minimal | **Large datasets** | Patient records, imaging |
| Failure Mode | Predictable | **Black-box** | Hard to debug AI hallucinations |
| Transparency | Full | Limited (XAI needed) | FDA requires explainability |
| Cycle Time | Fixed | Optimized via RL | OR scheduling vs. dynamic sched. |
| Cost (Initial) | **Lower** | Higher | Capital equipment cost |
| Cost (Long-term) | Higher (retooling) | Lower (self-improving) | Hospital operational cost |
