# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]**
**Define an Autonomous System. Differentiate between Level 3 and Level 5 autonomy as per the SAE J3016 standard.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** An Autonomous System is a robotic or cyber-physical system that can **perceive** its environment, **reason** about dynamic situations, and **execute** goal-directed actions without continuous human intervention.
>
> **Level 3 — Conditional Automation (1 mark):** The system performs the entire dynamic driving task under specific Operational Design Domain (ODD) conditions, but **expects a human driver to take over** when requested. The human is the fallback.
>
> **Level 5 — Full Automation (1 mark):** The system performs the entire dynamic driving task under **all conditions** that a human driver could manage. **No human driver is required** at any time, and the system may even operate without a steering wheel or pedals.
>
> **Key Distinction:** Level 3 still requires human fallback readiness; Level 5 is unconditionally driverless across all environments.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
**List and briefly explain any three sub-fields of Artificial Intelligence used in modern AI-driven automation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> 1. **Machine Learning (1 mark):** Algorithms that learn patterns from data without being explicitly programmed. Example: Random Forest for defect classification in a manufacturing line.
> 2. **Computer Vision (1 mark):** CNN-based image processing for visual inspection, object detection (YOLO), and pose estimation. Example: Robotic bin-picking of randomly oriented parts.
> 3. **Reinforcement Learning (1 mark):** An agent learns an optimal policy $\pi^*$ by interacting with an environment and maximizing a cumulative reward. Example: Teaching a robotic arm to grasp novel objects via trial-and-error.
>
> *(Acceptable alternatives: Natural Language Processing, Digital Twins, Explainable AI.)*

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Answer **any ONE** full question from the choice pair. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### ✅ QUESTION A — **Autonomous Systems + AI-Driven Automation** (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the Sense–Plan–Act architecture of an autonomous mobile robot. With a neat block diagram, describe the role of each module in the perception and planning layers.**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The Sense–Plan–Act paradigm is the classical cognitive architecture for autonomous robots. It decomposes robot behavior into three functional loops: *sensing* the environment, *planning* actions, and *actuating* them. Modern systems extend this to **Sense–Plan–Act–Learn** (a spiral loop).

**Perception Layer (3 marks):**
- **Sensors Used:** LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging) provides 360° range scans; RGB-D cameras provide color and depth; IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit) provides acceleration and angular rate; GPS provides global position.
- **Sensor Fusion:** Combines these streams using a Kalman Filter or Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) to produce a coherent world state.
- **SLAM Module:** Simultaneously localizes the robot and builds a map. Outputs are an occupancy grid and the robot pose $(x, y, \theta)$.
- **Output of Perception:** A unified state vector $\hat{x}_k$ with associated covariance $P_k$ representing the robot's belief about itself and its environment.

**Planning Layer (3 marks):**
- **Global Path Planner:** Computes a coarse route from start to goal using algorithms like **A\*** or **Dijkstra's** on the occupancy grid.
- **Local Trajectory Planner:** Refines the path in real-time using **Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)** or **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** to handle moving obstacles.
- **Behavior Arbitration:** **Behavior Trees** or the **Subsumption Architecture (Brooks, 1986)** arbitrate between competing goals (e.g., "reach goal" vs. "avoid pedestrian").
- **Output of Planning:** A time-parameterized trajectory $x(t), y(t), \theta(t)$ sent to the controller.

**Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 architecture diagram.

**Conclusion (Included implicitly in marks):**
The Sense–Plan–Act loop runs at frequencies of 10–100 Hz for planning and 1 kHz for low-level control, enabling real-time autonomy.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the role of Reinforcement Learning in AI-driven industrial automation. Derive the Bellman Optimality Equation from first principles.**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of RL in Industry (2 marks):**
- RL enables **self-optimizing control policies** in dynamic environments.
- **Use Cases:** Robotic bin-picking, adaptive process control, energy optimization in HVAC, AGV fleet routing.
- Unlike supervised learning, RL **does not require labeled data** — it learns by trial and error using a reward signal.
- The agent–environment loop: at each step, the agent observes state $s_t$, takes action $a_t$, receives reward $r_{t+1}$, and transitions to $s_{t+1}$.

**Derivation of Bellman Optimality (5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Define the Return:**
$$G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1}, \quad \gamma \in [0,1)$$

**Step 2 — State-Value Function:**
$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \vert S_t = s]$$

**Step 3 — Bellman Expectation (unroll one step):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
V^{\pi}(s) &= \sum_{a} \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s,a) \left[ R(s,a,s') + \gamma V^{\pi}(s') \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Define the Optimal Value Function:**
$$V^{*}(s) = \max_{\pi} V^{\pi}(s)$$

**Step 5 — Replace Weighted Sum with Maximization:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
V^{*}(s) &= \max_{a} \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s,a) \left[ R(s,a,s') + \gamma V^{*}(s') \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Express via Q-function:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^{*}(s, a) &= \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s,a) \left[ R(s,a,s') + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a') \right] \\
V^{*}(s) &= \max_{a} Q^{*}(s, a)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The Bellman Optimality Equation states that the value of a state under the optimal policy is the maximum expected value achievable by any single action, accounting for future optimal behavior.

---

### ✅ QUESTION B — **Robotics in Healthcare + AI-Driven Automation** (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the architecture of a surgical robotic system. With the help of a block diagram, describe the master–slave teleoperation principle and the role of haptic feedback.**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A surgical robot is a **master–slave teleoperated system** in which a surgeon (master) remotely controls a robotic manipulator (slave) inserted into the patient's body. The Da Vinci Surgical System is the most widely deployed commercial example.

**Architecture Description (3 marks):**
- **Master Console:** A 3D HD display, two finger-loop manipulators, and foot pedals. The surgeon's hand movements are captured by high-resolution encoders.
- **Motion Scaling:** Hand motion is scaled down (typical factor **3:1 to 10:1**) so that a 1 cm hand movement results in a 1–3 mm tool tip movement. This eliminates physiological hand tremor (~50–100 µm).
- **Communication Link:** A bidirectional channel (typically fiber-optic or wireless with redundancy) carrying position commands and force feedback. **Latency budget ≤ 200 ms** is mandatory.
- **Slave Manipulator (Patient-Side Cart):** A 6+ DOF instrument with a remote-center-of-motion (RCM) kinematic constraint to pivot around a small abdominal incision.
- **Endoscope:** A stereo 3D camera with 10–15× magnification.

**Haptic Feedback (3 marks):**
- Force sensors at the slave tool tip measure interaction forces with tissue ($F_{slave}$).
- These forces are **reflected to the master** via motorized handgrip actuators: $F_{master} = k \cdot F_{slave}$, where $k$ is a scaling gain.
- The surgeon **feels** tissue stiffness, suture tension, and collision events, dramatically improving surgical precision.
- Open challenges: **transparency** (faithful force reflection), **stability under time delay**, and **bilateral control design**.

**Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.4 block diagram.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Describe the concept of a Digital Twin in AI-driven automation. Discuss its role in predictive maintenance, virtual commissioning, and operator training.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A **Digital Twin** is a dynamic, virtual replica of a physical asset, process, or system that is updated in real time using sensor data and used for simulation, monitoring, optimization, and predictive analytics.

**Three Core Components (2 marks):**
1. **Physical Asset:** The real robot, machine, or production line equipped with IoT sensors.
2. **Digital Replica:** A high-fidelity simulation model (often built in tools like Siemens NX MCD, ANSYS Twin Builder, or Dassault 3DEXPERIENCE).
3. **Bidirectional Data Link:** A high-speed communication channel (MQTT, OPC-UA, or 5G) that synchronizes sensor data *from* the physical asset *to* the twin, and control commands *from* the twin *to* the asset.

**Predictive Maintenance (1.5 marks):**
- ML models trained on the digital twin predict equipment failures *before* they occur.
- Example: A vibration signature anomaly in a spindle motor is detected 48 hours in advance, allowing scheduled replacement during planned downtime — reducing unplanned downtime by up to **70%**.
- Key metrics: Remaining Useful Life (RUL), Mean Time To Failure (MTTF), and Probability of Failure at time $t$.

**Virtual Commissioning (1.5 marks):**
- The entire robotic cell is simulated and validated in the digital twin *before* physical installation.
- PLC code, robot trajectories, and sensor logic are tested virtually, reducing on-site commissioning time by **40–60%**.
- What-if analyses (e.g., change in part dimensions, sensor failure) are performed risk-free.

**Operator Training (1 mark):**
- New operators practice on the digital twin in immersive VR/AR environments.
- They learn fault recovery, emergency stops, and routine operations without risking real equipment or product.
- AI-driven adaptive training personalizes the curriculum based on operator performance.

**Conclusion:**
Digital Twins bridge the gap between the physical and digital worlds, forming the **backbone of Industry 4.0/5.0** AI-driven automation.

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in Module 4 — A KTU Examiner's Note:**
> 
> 1. **Conflating "Autonomy" with "Automation" (–1 mark typical):** Always state explicitly that *automation* is rule-based, while *autonomy* is adaptive. Examiners actively hunt for this distinction.
> 2. **Skipping the Derivation in RL Questions (–2 to –3 marks):** If the question asks to "derive the Bellman equation," merely writing the final form without the unrolling step loses almost all marks. Always show the unrolling: $V(s) = \mathbb{E}[r_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1})]$.
> 3. **Forgetting Units in Healthcare Robotics (–1 mark):** Always mention the **scale factor (3:1 to 10:1)**, **haptic latency (≤ 200 ms)**, and **cobot force limits (140 N)** — these are numerical must-haves.
> 4. **No Diagram in 7-mark Sub-parts (–1 mark):** KTU 2024 valuation key mandates *at least one* block diagram or flowchart per 7-mark answer. Reproduce one from the Section 4 diagrams.
> 5. **Confusing SLAM Variants:** EKF-SLAM, FastSLAM, and Graph-SLAM are *not* interchangeable. EKF-SLAM scales poorly; FastSLAM uses particle filters; Graph-SLAM optimizes a pose graph. Pick the right one for the problem.
> 6. **Vague AI Definitions:** "AI means making machines smart" is a **0-mark answer**. Always anchor AI to a sub-field (ML, DL, RL, CV) and give an *industrial* example.
> 7. **Missing Human-in-the-Loop:** Industry 5.0 explicitly emphasizes human–machine collaboration. Any AI automation answer that ignores the human is marked down by the 2024-scheme examiner.

## 5.4 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Future Trends in Robotics & Automation**

### 🔹 Autonomous Systems
- ✅ Definition: Sense–Plan–Act (extended to **Sense–Plan–Act–Learn**).
- ✅ **SAE J3016 Levels:** 0 (no automation) → 5 (full autonomy).
- ✅ Core Algorithms: **SLAM (EKF, FastSLAM, Graph-SLAM)**, **A\***, **RRT/RRT\***, **MPC**.
- ✅ Key Sensors: **LiDAR, RGB-D Camera, IMU, GPS, Radar**.
- ✅ Sensor Fusion: **Kalman Filter** (linear) and **EKF** (nonlinear).
- ✅ KPI: **Operational Design Domain (ODD)** and **MTBF**.
- ✅ ISO/TS 15066 limits for cobots: **140 N (quasi-static), 280 N (transient)**.

### 🔹 Robotics in Healthcare
- ✅ Three Classes: **Surgical, Rehabilitation, Service/Social**.
- ✅ Master–Slave Teleoperation with motion scaling (typical **3:1 to 10:1**).
- ✅ Haptic feedback latency budget: **≤ 200 ms**.
- ✅ Surgical accuracy: **± 0.1 mm**; repeatability: **≤ 0.05 mm** (ISO 9283).
- ✅ Da Vinci System, **RCM (Remote Center of Motion)** kinematic constraint.
- ✅ Exoskeletons: **Ekso GT, ReWalk**; Social Robots: **PARO, Pepper**.

### 🔹 AI-Driven Automation
- ✅ AI ≠ Automation. **AI = adaptive + data-driven**.
- ✅ Sub-fields: **ML, DL (CNNs, RNNs, Transformers), RL, CV, NLP**.
- ✅ **Bellman Optimality Equation:** $V^{*}(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s,a)[R + \gamma V^{*}(s')]$.
- ✅ **Q-Learning Update:** $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha [r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]$.
- ✅ **Digital Twin:** Physical Asset + Digital Replica + Bidirectional Data Link.
- ✅ **Industry 4.0 → 5.0** shift: CPS → **Human–Machine Collaboration**.
- ✅ Predictive Maintenance reduces unplanned downtime by **up to 70%**.

### 🔹 Must-Remember Numerical Values
| Quantity | Value | Context |
|---|---|---|
| Surgical scale factor | 3:1 to 10:1 | Da Vinci |
| Haptic latency | ≤ 200 ms | Teleop |
| Cobot contact force | 140 N (QS), 280 N (T) | ISO/TS 15066 |
| Surgical positional accuracy | ± 0.1 mm | Clinical |
| Battery/sensor Nyquist | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | DAQ |
| Discount factor range | $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ | RL |
| Hand tremor (human) | 50–100 µm | Surgery |
| MTBF (clinical robot) | ≥ 10,000 hr | Reliability |

### 🔹 Golden One-Liners for Examiner Impress
- *"Autonomy is graded by the ODD, not by the hardware."*
- *"Surgical robots filter tremor and scale motion; they do not replace the surgeon."*
- *"AI-driven automation turns the Sense–Plan–Act loop into a self-improving spiral."*
- *"Digital Twins are the nervous system of Industry 4.0."*
- *"Reinforcement learning is the only ML paradigm that learns from interaction, not labels."*
- *"Cobots are bounded by ISO/TS 15066 force limits — safety is engineered, not assumed."*
